Nitroglycerin is optimal given no contraindications, evolving MI, and HF symptoms — titrated to 25% MAP reduction (Answer A correct).
Q163: A 56-year-old white woman with longstanding hypertension and recently diagnosed HF (EF 35%) presents to the ED with BP 210/12...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ C
A) Sodium nitroprusside 0.25 mcg/kg/minute titrated to 25% MAP reduction
B) Labetalol 80 mg IV; repeat until BP is <120/80 mmHg
C) Resumption of home medications; refer for follow-up within 2 days
D) Resumption of home medications; initiate amlodipine 10 mg daily; refer for follow-up in 1 week
Explanation: This patient has an asymptomatic hypertensive crisis WITHOUT acute target-organ damage (no hypertensive emergency). IV medications and hospital admission (Answers A and B) are unnecessary. This presentation is likely due to recent nonadherence. Resuming her home medications (Answer C) is most appropriate with close follow-up. Adding a fourth agent (Answer D) is unnecessary when her current regimen could work if she is adherent. Follow-up should be within a few days rather than waiting 1 week.
Q164: Which adverse effect is dose-dependent and predictable (Type A) rather than idiosyncratic?
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ C
A) ACE inhibitor angioedema
B) Heparin-induced thrombocytopenia
C) Digoxin bradyarrhythmia
D) Stevens-Johnson syndrome
Explanation: Type A reactions are predictable and related to the drug's pharmacologic action (e.g., digoxin causes bradycardia via its mechanism). Others are Type B (idiosyncratic) reactions.
Q165: Which property makes nitroprusside suitable for hypertensive emergencies?
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ B
A) β₁ agonism
B) Rapid onset and ultrashort half-life via cyanide metabolism
C) Renal elimination only
D) Positive inotropic action
Explanation: IV nitroprusside acts within seconds and is metabolized to cyanide then thiocyanate. Monitor for cyanide toxicity with prolonged use, especially in renal impairment.
Q166: Which antiarrhythmic is contraindicated in structural heart disease due to proarrhythmic risk?
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Arrhythmia✅ B
A) Amiodarone
B) Flecainide
C) Sotalol
D) Dofetilide
Explanation: Class IC agents (flecainide, propafenone) increase mortality post-MI or in LV dysfunction — they are contraindicated in structural heart disease due to proarrhythmic risk (CAST trial).
Q167: A patient on verapamil reports constipation and bradycardia. What is the primary cause?
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Hypertension✅ A
A) Blockade of intestinal smooth muscle Ca²⁺ channels
B) Cholinergic stimulation
C) Na⁺-channel inhibition
D) Increased sympathetic tone
Explanation: Non-dihydropyridine CCBs (verapamil, diltiazem) relax smooth muscle and depress the AV node, causing both constipation (GI smooth muscle relaxation) and bradycardia.
Q168: Which arrhythmia can be treated with lidocaine?
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Arrhythmia✅ D
A) Paroxysmal supraventricular tachycardia
B) Atrial fibrillation
C) Atrial flutter
D) Ventricular tachycardia
Explanation: Lidocaine has little effect on atrial or AV nodal tissue. It is used for ventricular arrhythmias such as ventricular tachycardia.
Q169: A clinician wants to initiate a drug for rhythm control of atrial fibrillation. Which coexisting condition would allow initia...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Arrhythmia✅ A
A) Hypertension
B) Left ventricular hypertrophy
C) Coronary artery disease
D) Heart failure
Explanation: Flecainide can increase the risk of sudden cardiac death in structural heart disease. Only coexisting hypertension (without LVH) allows initiation. Structural heart disease includes LVH, heart failure, and atherosclerotic heart disease — all contraindications.
Q170: Which of the following correctly ranks the calcium channel blockers from most active on the myocardium to most peripherally a...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Hypertension✅ B
A) Diltiazem, amlodipine, verapamil
B) Verapamil, diltiazem, nifedipine
C) Nifedipine, verapamil, diltiazem
D) Amlodipine, diltiazem, verapamil
Explanation: Verapamil has the most negative inotropic effects (most myocardium-active). Nifedipine/amlodipine are dihydropyridines — predominantly peripheral vasodilators. Diltiazem is intermediate.
Q171: A 62-year-old female with HTN, HLD, and type 2 diabetes is hospitalized with acute STEMI. She is started on dual antiplatelet...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ A
A) Add a mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist (MRA) if her LVEF is ≤ 40%
B) Switch her ACE inhibitor to an ARB
C) Add a calcium channel blocker
D) Initiate a thiazide diuretic for blood pressure
Explanation: Post-MI patients with LVEF ≤ 40% who are on an ACE inhibitor and beta-blocker should have an MRA added (e.g., eplerenone), as shown in the EPHESUS trial to reduce mortality and hospitalizations.
Q172: A 55-year-old male with type 2 diabetes on metformin 1000 mg BID has A1c 8.2% and HFrEF. eGFR is 65 mL/min/1.73 m². Which is ...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ B
A) Glipizide
B) Dapagliflozin
C) Sitagliptin
D) Pioglitazone
Explanation: Dapagliflozin is an SGLT2 inhibitor with demonstrated significant reductions in HF hospitalization and cardiovascular death in HFrEF (DAPA-HF trial), regardless of glycemic status. Pioglitazone is contraindicated in HF.
Q173: A 45-year-old female with HTN and newly diagnosed Raynaud's phenomenon is being started on an antihypertensive. She also has ...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Hypertension✅ C
A) Atenolol
B) Lisinopril
C) Diltiazem
D) Hydrochlorothiazide
Explanation: Diltiazem, a non-dihydropyridine CCB, is effective for HTN, can reduce migraine frequency, and improves Raynaud's symptoms via vasodilation — an ideal choice for all three conditions.
Q174: A 70-year-old male with HTN and history of gout is on hydrochlorothiazide. BP is well-controlled but he has recurrent gout fl...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ A
A) Switch to an ARB like losartan
B) Increase the dose of hydrochlorothiazide
C) Add allopurinol
D) Switch to a beta-blocker
Explanation: Losartan is an ARB with a unique uricosuric effect — it lowers uric acid levels. It is the preferred antihypertensive in patients with HTN and a history of gout. HCTZ worsens gout.
Q175: A patient is admitted for ACS and is on aspirin 81 mg daily. Platelet count is 100 × 10⁹/L. The physician wants to start dual...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Anticoagulation✅ C
A) Warfarin
B) Prasugrel
C) Clopidogrel
D) Ticagrelor
Explanation: Clopidogrel is appropriate as a P2Y12 inhibitor for DAPT. Prasugrel carries higher bleeding risk and is contraindicated at lower platelet counts. Clopidogrel's side effect profile is less severe, making it safer in this patient.
Q176: A patient with newly diagnosed subclinical hypothyroidism (TSH 7.2 mIU/L) has a history of atrial fibrillation. Which is the ...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Arrhythmia✅ A
A) Start levothyroxine at a low dose and titrate very slowly
B) Monitor TSH every 6 months without treatment
C) Initiate levothyroxine at a full replacement dose
D) Refer for radioiodine ablation
Explanation: In patients with cardiovascular disease, it is critical to start with a very low dose of levothyroxine and titrate slowly to avoid increasing the risk of atrial fibrillation and other cardiac events.
Q177: A 65-year-old male with recurrent peptic ulcers is on chronic high-dose naproxen for osteoarthritis, omeprazole for GI prophy...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Anticoagulation✅ C
A) Omeprazole and naproxen
B) Clopidogrel and naproxen
C) Clopidogrel and omeprazole
D) All of the above are equally significant
Explanation: Omeprazole inhibits CYP2C19, which is required to activate clopidogrel (a prodrug). This significantly reduces clopidogrel's antiplatelet activity, increasing the risk of cardiovascular events. This is the most clinically significant interaction.
Q178: A 62-year-old male with HTN, HLD, and recent NSTEMI is discharged on atorvastatin 80 mg, aspirin 81 mg, clopidogrel 75 mg, an...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Anticoagulation✅ D
A) Add a thiazide diuretic
B) Add amlodipine
C) Increase metoprolol succinate dose
D) Add ramipril
Explanation: ACE inhibitors (e.g., ramipril) are indicated post-MI to reduce mortality and HF risk, especially when BP is not at goal. Ramipril has demonstrated mortality benefit post-MI (HOPE trial). It also provides BP control.
Q179: A 68-year-old male with HTN and T2DM is on lisinopril 20 mg daily, metformin 1000 mg BID, and atorvastatin 40 mg. He is hospi...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Anticoagulation✅ A
A) Ticagrelor 90 mg BID
B) Furosemide 40 mg BID
C) Warfarin
D) Hydrochlorothiazide 25 mg daily
Explanation: Post-NSTEMI patients should receive DAPT (aspirin + a P2Y12 inhibitor). Ticagrelor 90 mg BID is preferred over clopidogrel in ACS patients per PLATO trial data, providing superior outcomes when added to aspirin.
Q180: A 55-year-old male with T2DM and HTN is on metformin 1000 mg BID and lisinopril 20 mg. His A1c is 8.5% and UACR is 450 mg/g. ...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Hypertension✅ D
A) Glimepiride
B) Insulin detemir
C) Sitagliptin
D) Canagliflozin
Explanation: Canagliflozin (SGLT2 inhibitor) reduces A1c and has demonstrated significant reduction in progression of diabetic kidney disease in the CREDENCE trial (reduced risk of ESKD, doubling of SCr, and CV death/MI/stroke).
Q181: A 70-year-old female with HTN, CAD, and osteoporosis is on metoprolol succinate, atorvastatin, and calcium/vitamin D. Her BP ...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Dyslipidemia✅ C
A) Diltiazem
B) Amlodipine
C) Lisinopril
D) Hydrochlorothiazide
Explanation: For a patient with CAD and HTN already on a beta-blocker, an ACE inhibitor is the preferred addition. ACE inhibitors reduce cardiovascular events in patients with CAD (HOPE trial). Diltiazem combined with a beta-blocker risks AV block.
Q182: A 58-year-old male with hyperlipidemia on atorvastatin 80 mg complains of new muscle aches and weakness. CK level is 5 times ...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Dyslipidemia✅ C
A) Switch to a less potent statin like pravastatin
B) Discontinue atorvastatin immediately and do not restart
C) Discontinue atorvastatin and re-challenge with a lower dose after symptoms resolve
D) Lower the dose of atorvastatin to 40 mg daily
Explanation: CK 5x ULN without rhabdomyolysis (CK >10x ULN with myoglobinuria) allows for discontinuation and re-challenge after resolution. Permanent discontinuation (Answer B) is premature. Statins provide significant CV benefit and a lower dose re-challenge is appropriate after symptoms resolve.
Q183: A patient with newly diagnosed T2DM has BMI of 38 and A1c 8.9%. His PMH is significant for HTN and HLD. Which initial therapy...
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ D
A) Insulin detemir and metformin
B) Metformin and pioglitazone
C) Metformin and glipizide
D) Metformin and semaglutide
Explanation: Semaglutide (GLP-1 agonist) combined with metformin provides A1c reduction and significant weight loss (7-15% body weight), making it ideal for this obese patient. Pioglitazone causes weight gain; glipizide is weight-neutral. GLP-1 agonists also reduce CV events in high-risk patients.
Q184: A 60-year-old male with no known CAD or HF is diagnosed with hypertension and BPH. The physician starts doxazosin. What is th...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Hypertension✅ B
A) Risk of QT prolongation
B) Increased risk of HF hospitalization compared to ACE inhibitors/diuretics (ALLHAT data)
C) High risk of hyperkalemia
D) Absolute contraindication in patients with asthma
Explanation: The ALLHAT trial demonstrated that doxazosin was associated with a significantly higher rate of HF hospitalization compared to chlorthalidone. The doxazosin arm was terminated early due to this finding. Alpha-blockers are not recommended as first-line antihypertensive monotherapy.
Q185: A 68-year-old male with NSTE-ACS is initiated on aspirin, ticagrelor, and enoxaparin. He has chronic gout and takes colchicin...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Anticoagulation✅ C
A) Increased bleeding risk from ticagrelor/enoxaparin combination
B) Increased risk of statin-induced myopathy
C) Increased plasma concentration and toxicity of colchicine
D) Decreased antiplatelet effect of ticagrelor
E) Decreased efficacy of enoxaparin
Explanation: Ticagrelor is a potent P-glycoprotein (P-gp) and moderate CYP3A4 inhibitor. Colchicine is a P-gp and CYP3A4 substrate. Co-administration can significantly increase colchicine plasma levels, leading to toxicity (GI, neuromuscular, bone marrow suppression).
Q186: A 55-year-old female with STEMI undergoes successful PCI with stent placement and is discharged on aspirin and ticagrelor. Th...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Anticoagulation✅ B
A) Ticagrelor-induced hemoptysis leading to pulmonary edema
B) Ticagrelor-induced adenosine reuptake inhibition
C) Aspirin-exacerbated respiratory disease (AERD)
D) Ticagrelor-related neutropenia causing opportunistic lung infection
E) Coronary restenosis manifesting as dyspnea
Explanation: Ticagrelor inhibits adenosine reuptake (via ENT1), increasing adenosine levels. This causes dyspnea (adenosine-induced bronchoconstriction) and bradycardia (adenosine's negative chronotropic effect). This occurs in up to 15% of patients and is a class effect distinct from aspirin-induced respiratory disease.
Q187: A 72-year-old patient receives primary PCI for STEMI. Prior to the procedure, they received aspirin, ticagrelor, and UFH. The...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Anticoagulation✅ B
A) Continue UFH and add bivalirudin infusion immediately
B) Discontinue UFH and start a direct thrombin inhibitor (bivalirudin or argatroban)
C) Discontinue UFH and switch to fondaparinux 2.5 mg SC daily
D) Discontinue UFH and start an IV GP IIb/IIIa inhibitor
E) Discontinue all anticoagulants and rely only on dual antiplatelet therapy
Explanation: Any prior history of HIT is a contraindication to UFH (and LMWH). A direct thrombin inhibitor (bivalirudin or argatroban) must be used. Fondaparinux carries a risk of catheter-related thrombosis in the cath lab setting and is not ideal for PCI. Continuing UFH (Answer A) is dangerous.
Q188: A 62-year-old male is treated for NSTE-ACS with aspirin, clopidogrel, and fondaparinux. What major advantage does fondaparinu...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Anticoagulation✅ D
A) Direct thrombin inhibition effect
B) Can be easily monitored using aPTT
C) Only injectable factor Xa inhibitor
D) Not associated with heparin-induced thrombocytopenia (HIT)
E) Has a reversible antagonist (protamine sulfate)
Explanation: Fondaparinux is a synthetic pentasaccharide that selectively inhibits factor Xa and does NOT bind to platelet factor 4 (PF4), therefore it cannot cause HIT. Enoxaparin can rarely cause HIT. However, fondaparinux requires supplemental UFH or bivalirudin if PCI is performed due to catheter thrombosis risk.
Q189: Which P2Y12 inhibitor is specifically contraindicated in a patient with a history of stroke or TIA?
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Anticoagulation✅ C
A) Clopidogrel
B) Ticagrelor
C) Prasugrel
D) Cangrelor
E) All P2Y12 inhibitors are contraindicated in TIA/Stroke
Explanation: Prasugrel is contraindicated in patients with prior stroke or TIA due to net harm (increased fatal and intracranial bleeding risk) demonstrated in the TRITON-TIMI 38 subgroup analysis. Clopidogrel and ticagrelor do not share this contraindication.
Q190: A patient on maintenance DAPT (aspirin + clopidogrel) post-PCI develops a gastric ulcer. High-dose IV pantoprazole is initiat...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Anticoagulation✅ C
A) Increased risk of C. difficile infection due to combined effect
B) Decreased serum concentration of aspirin
C) Reduced antiplatelet activity of clopidogrel
D) Increased bleeding risk due to synergistic platelet inhibition
E) Hyperkalemia due to unknown concomitant interaction
Explanation: Omeprazole and, to a lesser extent, other PPIs (especially omeprazole > esomeprazole > pantoprazole) inhibit CYP2C19, which is required to convert clopidogrel (a prodrug) to its active metabolite. This reduces antiplatelet activity and may increase the risk of cardiovascular events. Pantoprazole has the least interaction of all PPIs.
Q191: A 70-year-old female with NSTE-ACS has CrCl of 25 mL/min. She is scheduled for PCI. Which anticoagulant should be avoided or ...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Anticoagulation✅ B
A) Unfractionated heparin (UFH)
B) Enoxaparin
C) Bivalirudin
D) Fondaparinux
E) All are equally safe
Explanation: Enoxaparin is renally eliminated and accumulates significantly in renal impairment (CrCl <30 mL/min), increasing bleeding risk. Dose adjustment (1 mg/kg once daily instead of BID) is required, but the risk remains high. UFH and bivalirudin do not rely on renal clearance to the same extent and are preferred in severe CKD.
Q192: Which antiplatelet agent is structurally similar to adenosine, reversibly inhibits the P2Y12 receptor, and simultaneously inh...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Anticoagulation✅ C
A) Clopidogrel
B) Prasugrel
C) Ticagrelor
D) Cilostazol
E) Eptifibatide
Explanation: Ticagrelor is a cyclopentyl-triazolo-pyrimidine (structurally similar to adenosine). It reversibly inhibits P2Y12 and also inhibits adenosine reuptake via ENT1, elevating adenosine levels. This accounts for its unique side effects: dyspnea and bradycardia (adenosine-mediated).
Q193: In a patient with STEMI undergoing primary PCI, a GP IIb/IIIa inhibitor is considered. Which drug from this class requires do...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Anticoagulation✅ D
A) Abciximab
B) Eptifibatide
C) Tirofiban
D) Both eptifibatide and tirofiban
E) None of them
Explanation: Eptifibatide and tirofiban are both small-molecule GP IIb/IIIa inhibitors that are renally eliminated and require dose adjustment in renal impairment. Abciximab is a large monoclonal antibody fragment that is not renally cleared and does not require dose adjustment for renal impairment.
Q194: Which anticoagulant, when used for initial management of ACS, is contraindicated in patients with CrCl < 30 mL/min?
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Anticoagulation✅ C
A) Unfractionated heparin
B) Bivalirudin
C) Fondaparinux
D) Argatroban
E) Warfarin
Explanation: Fondaparinux is a factor Xa inhibitor that is primarily renally excreted. It is contraindicated when CrCl <30 mL/min due to drug accumulation and risk of bleeding. UFH is preferred in severe renal impairment as it does not depend on renal clearance.
Q195: A 75-year-old male with HFrEF (LVEF 25%) is started on Entresto (sacubitril/valsartan). One week later, he presents with faci...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ D
A) Valsartan-induced bradykinin accumulation
B) Sacubitril-induced NF-κB pathway activation
C) ACE inhibitor residual effect from inadequate washout time
D) Sacubitril's inhibition of neprilysin, leading to increased bradykinin levels
E) Hypokalemia secondary to new diuretic initiation
Explanation: Sacubitril inhibits neprilysin, the enzyme that degrades bradykinin. This leads to bradykinin accumulation, causing angioedema. If a patient transitions from an ACE inhibitor (which also elevates bradykinin) without a 36-hour washout, the additive effect dramatically increases angioedema risk — this is a black-box warning.
Q196: A 50-year-old female with HFrEF and diabetes is on carvedilol, sacubitril/valsartan, and dapagliflozin. She presents to the E...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ C
A) Carvedilol-induced hypoglycemia (masked)
B) Sacubitril/valsartan causing ACE-related acidosis
C) Dapagliflozin-induced euglycemic diabetic ketoacidosis (eDKA)
D) Aldosterone antagonist-induced hyperkalemia
E) Long-term aspirin use causing salicylism
Explanation: SGLT2 inhibitors (like dapagliflozin) can cause euglycemic DKA — DKA in the absence of significant hyperglycemia. Glucose levels appear normal because SGLT2 inhibitors promote urinary glucose excretion. The mechanism involves increased glucagon and decreased insulin secretion, promoting lipolysis and ketone production.
Q197: Which of the following is an absolute contraindication for starting a β1-selective beta-blocker in a patient with a recent MI...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Arrhythmia✅ A
A) Symptomatic bradycardia (HR < 50 bpm) or high-degree AV block
B) Concomitant use of verapamil
C) History of severe COPD
D) Ejection fraction less than 40%
E) NYHA Class III heart failure symptoms
Explanation: Absolute contraindications to beta-blockers include significant bradycardia (HR <50 bpm) and high-degree AV block (2nd or 3rd degree without a pacemaker). Severe COPD is a relative contraindication. Low EF and NYHA Class III HF are actually indications — beta-blockers improve outcomes in HFrEF when initiated in stable, euvolemic patients.
Q198: A 60-year-old patient with ASCVD on atorvastatin 80 mg daily has persistently elevated LDL. The physician adds ezetimibe. Wha...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Dyslipidemia✅ D
A) PCSK9 inhibition
B) Inhibition of cholesterol synthesis in the liver
C) Increased LDL receptor expression
D) Selective inhibition of the NPC1L1 cholesterol transport protein in the small intestine
E) Inhibition of CETP
Explanation: Ezetimibe selectively inhibits the NPC1L1 (Niemann-Pick C1-Like 1) protein in the intestinal brush border, blocking cholesterol absorption from the gut. This is distinct from statins (HMG-CoA reductase inhibition) and PCSK9 inhibitors. The combination of statin + ezetimibe reduces LDL by an additional 15-25%.
Q199: Which diuretic class is associated with ototoxicity (hearing impairment), especially with rapid IV administration?
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Arrhythmia✅ B
A) Thiazides (e.g., hydrochlorothiazide)
B) Loop diuretics (e.g., furosemide, bumetanide)
C) Potassium-sparing diuretics (e.g., spironolactone)
D) Carbonic anhydrase inhibitors (e.g., acetazolamide)
E) Vasopressin receptor antagonists (e.g., tolvaptan)
Explanation: Loop diuretics (furosemide, ethacrynic acid, bumetanide) are associated with ototoxicity, particularly with rapid IV administration or in renal failure. Ethacrynic acid carries the highest risk. The mechanism involves inhibition of ion transport in the stria vascularis of the inner ear.
Q200: A patient with refractory hypertension is initiated on spironolactone. Which adverse effect requires close lab monitoring for...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ A
A) Hyperkalemia
B) Hypocalcemia
C) Gynecomastia
D) Hyperuricemia
E) Neutropenia
Explanation: Both spironolactone and eplerenone are aldosterone receptor antagonists that reduce urinary potassium excretion, causing hyperkalemia. Serum potassium and creatinine must be monitored closely. Note: Gynecomastia occurs with spironolactone (but NOT eplerenone, which is more selective) — so gynecomastia is NOT shared by both.
Q201: A 45-year-old male with familial hypercholesterolemia is initiated on the PCSK9 inhibitor evolocumab. What is the mechanism b...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Dyslipidemia✅ C
A) Decreases the liver's synthesis of VLDL
B) Promotes the reverse cholesterol transport system
C) Prevents the degradation of LDL receptors on the hepatocyte surface
D) Binds to and directly inactivates LDL particles in the bloodstream
E) Inhibits the absorption of bile acids in the ileum
Explanation: PCSK9 normally binds to LDL receptors on hepatocytes and targets them for lysosomal degradation. Evolocumab (a monoclonal antibody) inhibits PCSK9, preventing LDL receptor degradation. This allows LDL receptors to be recycled back to the cell surface, where they continue to clear LDL from the bloodstream (up to 60% LDL reduction).
Q202: A 65-year-old male with HFrEF is switched from lisinopril to Entresto (sacubitril/valsartan) without observing the necessary ...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ B
A) Severe hypotension and syncope
B) Angioedema
C) Acute kidney injury
D) Euglycemic DKA
E) Atrial fibrillation
Explanation: Both ACE inhibitors and sacubitril (neprilysin inhibitor) elevate bradykinin levels. Without a 36-hour washout period, the additive bradykinin accumulation can cause severe, potentially fatal angioedema. This is a black-box warning and absolute requirement for Entresto initiation.
Q203: Which beta-blocker is preferred for initiating therapy in chronic HFrEF due to evidence from large-scale mortality trials?
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Arrhythmia✅ A
A) Metoprolol succinate (extended-release)
B) Metoprolol tartrate (immediate-release)
C) Atenolol
D) Sotalol
E) Esmolol
Explanation: Only three beta-blockers have mortality benefit in HFrEF: carvedilol (COPERNICUS trial), metoprolol succinate ER (MERIT-HF trial), and bisoprolol (CIBIS-II). Metoprolol tartrate (immediate-release) is NOT proven for HFrEF. Atenolol lacks mortality benefit in HFrEF. Sotalol is an antiarrhythmic.
Q204: A patient on digoxin for atrial fibrillation develops blurred yellow vision, nausea, and vomiting. What is the most likely el...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Arrhythmia✅ B
A) Hypernatremia
B) Hypokalemia
C) Hypercalcemia
D) Hypomagnesemia
E) Metabolic acidosis
Explanation: Hypokalemia sensitizes the myocardium to digoxin toxicity by competing for the same binding site on Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase. Even at therapeutic digoxin levels, hypokalemia can precipitate toxicity (arrhythmias, GI symptoms, visual disturbances including yellow-green halos). Hypomagnesemia also sensitizes, but hypokalemia is the primary electrolyte concern.
Q205: A patient with hypertension and angina is treated with metoprolol and verapamil. Both are associated with which of the follow...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ C
A) Diarrhea
B) Hypoglycemia
C) Increased PR interval
D) Tachycardia
E) Thyrotoxicosis
Explanation: Both metoprolol (beta-blocker) and verapamil (non-DHP CCB) slow conduction through the AV node, prolonging the PR interval on ECG. Combining these agents requires caution due to additive risk of bradycardia and heart block.
Q206: Which antiarrhythmic is preferred for maintenance of sinus rhythm in HF with reduced EF (HFrEF)?
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Arrhythmia✅ A
A) Amiodarone
B) Flecainide
C) Propafenone
D) Dofetilide
Explanation: In HFrEF, class IC agents (flecainide, propafenone) are contraindicated due to proarrhythmic risk and increased mortality. Dofetilide is an alternative for AF in HF but requires careful QT monitoring. Amiodarone is the preferred antiarrhythmic for rhythm control in HFrEF patients due to its safety profile in structural heart disease.
Q207: A patient with ACS is in atrial fibrillation and requires anticoagulation plus DAPT. How should antiplatelet therapy be manag...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Arrhythmia✅ B
A) Triple therapy (warfarin + DAPT) long-term for all patients
B) Use shortest possible triple therapy, then dual therapy (OAC + single antiplatelet), balancing bleeding risk
C) Stop anticoagulation and use DAPT only
D) Replace aspirin with ticagrelor only, no OAC needed
Explanation: Guidelines recommend the shortest possible duration of triple therapy (OAC + aspirin + P2Y12 inhibitor), then transition to dual therapy (OAC + single antiplatelet, preferably P2Y12 inhibitor). The balance between ischemic benefit and bleeding risk guides duration. Long-term triple therapy significantly increases bleeding without proportional ischemic benefit.
Q208: Which agent is indicated for coronary vasospasm (Prinzmetal/variant angina)?
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ A
A) Nitrates and calcium channel blockers
B) Beta-blockers
C) ACE inhibitors
D) Thrombolytics
Explanation: Prinzmetal (variant) angina is caused by coronary artery vasospasm. Treatment includes nitrates (for acute episodes) and calcium channel blockers (for prevention). Beta-blockers are avoided as they may worsen coronary vasospasm by allowing unopposed alpha-mediated vasoconstriction.
Q209: Use of GP IIb/IIIa inhibitors is preferred in which setting?
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Anticoagulation✅ B
A) Routine upfront in all NSTEMI patients
B) Bailout during PCI or high thrombus burden
C) As oral alternative to P2Y12 inhibitors
D) In place of aspirin
Explanation: GP IIb/IIIa inhibitors (abciximab, eptifibatide, tirofiban) are currently reserved for bailout situations during PCI (e.g., failed initial recanalization) or when there is high thrombus burden. Routine upfront use in NSTEMI has not shown consistent mortality benefit and increases bleeding risk.
Q210: A 70-year-old male with HFrEF (LVEF 20%) and AF is stable on low-dose carvedilol, sacubitril/valsartan, and warfarin. He is s...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Arrhythmia✅ B
A) Double the dose of carvedilol
B) Reduce the dose of warfarin by 30% to 50%
C) Increase INR monitoring to every 3 days
D) Discontinue sacubitril/valsartan and switch to lisinopril
E) Switch warfarin to rivaroxaban
Explanation: Amiodarone is a potent inhibitor of CYP2C9 (warfarin's metabolizing enzyme) and CYP3A4. Co-administration significantly increases warfarin levels, leading to supratherapeutic INR and bleeding risk. The warfarin dose must be empirically reduced by 30-50% when amiodarone is initiated, with close INR monitoring.
Q211: A 58-year-old female with STEMI is rushed to the cath lab. She received aspirin and ticagrelor in the ED. She has known sever...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Anticoagulation✅ A
A) Unfractionated heparin (UFH) with aPTT monitoring
B) Bivalirudin
C) Enoxaparin
D) Fondaparinux
E) High-dose rivaroxaban
Explanation: Bivalirudin is primarily cleared by hepatic metabolism and proteolysis — severe hepatic impairment prolongs its effect unpredictably. Fondaparinux carries catheter-thrombosis risk in the cath lab. UFH is the safest choice in hepatic impairment as it is not metabolized by the liver and can be titrated with aPTT monitoring.
Q212: A patient with ACS is initiated on DAPT and atorvastatin 80 mg. Four weeks later he presents with dark urine, muscle aches, a...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Anticoagulation✅ D
A) Aspirin
B) Clopidogrel
C) Prasugrel
D) Ticagrelor
E) Cangrelor
Explanation: Ticagrelor is a moderate CYP3A4 inhibitor. Atorvastatin is metabolized by CYP3A4. Inhibition of CYP3A4 by ticagrelor increases atorvastatin plasma concentrations, raising the risk of statin-induced myopathy and rhabdomyolysis. This interaction is clinically significant at high statin doses.
Q213: Which of the following statements about ACE inhibitors in Heart Failure is FALSE?
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ C
A) ACEi reduce both preload and afterload
B) The cough associated with ACEi is due to bradykinin accumulation
C) ACEi are absolutely contraindicated in bilateral renal artery stenosis due to risk of angioedema
D) ARBs are the preferred alternative in patients intolerant of ACEi cough
E) ACEi can cause a creatinine-independent rise in serum creatinine
Explanation: This statement is FALSE. ACEi ARE absolutely contraindicated in bilateral renal artery stenosis, but the reason is acute kidney injury (AKI), NOT angioedema. ACEi block the angiotensin II-mediated efferent arteriolar constriction that maintains GFR in the setting of bilateral RAS, causing precipitous renal failure.
Q214: What is the primary clinical disadvantage of atenolol compared to metoprolol succinate ER or carvedilol in chronic stable CAD...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ B
A) Atenolol causes a higher incidence of erectile dysfunction
B) Atenolol is not proven to reduce mortality in patients with heart failure
C) Atenolol has a shorter half-life requiring twice-daily dosing
D) Atenolol is renally cleared, making it unsafe for most HF patients
E) Atenolol is the only one associated with a rebound phenomenon upon abrupt cessation
Explanation: Atenolol lacks mortality benefit in HF — it was NOT included in the landmark HF mortality trials (MERIT-HF, COPERNICUS, CIBIS-II). Only carvedilol, metoprolol succinate ER, and bisoprolol have proven mortality benefit in HFrEF. Atenolol's renal clearance is actually an advantage in hepatic impairment.
Q215: A patient on chronic warfarin for a mechanical mitral valve is prescribed rifampin for a Staphylococcus aureus infection. Wha...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Anticoagulation✅ B
A) INR will decrease; rifampin is a P-gp inhibitor
B) INR will decrease; rifampin is a potent CYP2C9 inducer
C) INR will increase; rifampin is a potent CYP2C9 inhibitor
D) INR will increase; rifampin causes platelet destruction
E) No change in INR expected
Explanation: Rifampin is one of the most potent CYP enzyme inducers (CYP2C9, CYP3A4, and P-gp). It dramatically increases warfarin metabolism, decreasing warfarin levels and INR. Warfarin dose may need to be increased 2-5 fold. INR must be monitored closely and dose adjusted when rifampin is started and stopped.
Q216: A 68-year-old male with HFrEF is on four guideline-directed medical therapy (GDMT) drugs including spironolactone. Which lab ...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ C
A) Serum sodium
B) Serum magnesium
C) Testosterone/estrogen ratio (gynecomastia risk)
D) BNP
E) Hemoglobin
Explanation: Spironolactone is a non-selective mineralocorticoid receptor antagonist that also blocks androgen receptors and inhibits testosterone synthesis. In men, this leads to gynecomastia, breast tenderness, and sexual dysfunction. This is a sex-hormone-specific side effect NOT seen with eplerenone (selective MRA). Monitoring testosterone/estrogen ratio helps identify this early.
Q217: Which is the most appropriate strategy for antiplatelet therapy in a patient undergoing urgent CABG surgery 12 hours after re...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Anticoagulation✅ D
A) Proceed to CABG; the P2Y12 effect is rapidly reversible
B) Administer a platelet transfusion immediately before incision
C) Delay CABG by 24 hours as prasugrel has a shorter half-life than clopidogrel
D) Delay CABG by at least 7 days
E) Switch to cangrelor immediately
Explanation: Prasugrel causes irreversible platelet inhibition. Guidelines recommend holding prasugrel for at least 7 days before CABG (vs 5 days for clopidogrel and ticagrelor, though ticagrelor is reversible). Platelet transfusions within 7 days of prasugrel are not effective since the newly transfused platelets are inhibited by circulating active metabolite.
Q218: In a patient who is post-MI taking aspirin and metoprolol, a medication is added that primarily lowers triglycerides and caus...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Anticoagulation✅ B
A) Gemfibrozil; increased risk of rhabdomyolysis
B) Niacin; increased risk of new-onset diabetes/hyperglycemia
C) Ezetimibe; increased liver transaminases
D) Omega-3 fatty acids; increased risk of atrial fibrillation
E) Colesevelam; severe constipation
Explanation: Niacin (nicotinic acid) lowers triglycerides (by 20-50%) and causes flushing due to prostaglandin-mediated vasodilation (reduced by pretreatment with aspirin). The most clinically important metabolic adverse effect is worsening of glycemic control — niacin causes insulin resistance and can precipitate new-onset diabetes, especially in patients already at risk.
Q219: A 58-year-old woman with hypertension and CAD underwent PCI with placement of two drug-eluting stents one week ago. Genotypin...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Anticoagulation✅ C
A) Clopidogrel plus aspirin
B) Clopidogrel plus prasugrel
C) Prasugrel plus aspirin
D) High-dose aspirin
E) Dipyridamole plus aspirin
Explanation: Clopidogrel is a prodrug requiring CYP2C19 for activation. CYP2C19 poor metabolizers cannot effectively activate clopidogrel, resulting in reduced platelet inhibition and higher risk of stent thrombosis. Guidelines recommend switching to prasugrel or ticagrelor in patients known to be CYP2C19 poor metabolizers post-PCI. Prasugrel does not require CYP2C19 for activation.
Q220: A 62-year-old male with T2DM and CAD has: Total Cholesterol 185 mg/dL, LDL-C 110 mg/dL, HDL-C 45 mg/dL, Triglycerides 175 mg/...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Dyslipidemia✅ A
A) Increase atorvastatin to 80 mg daily
B) Add ezetimibe to current therapy
C) Add a PCSK9 inhibitor
D) Continue current therapy and monitor
E) Switch from atorvastatin to rosuvastatin
Explanation: This patient has ASCVD (CAD + T2DM) and should be on high-intensity statin therapy (atorvastatin 40-80 mg or rosuvastatin 20-40 mg) per ACC/AHA guidelines. Atorvastatin 40 mg is moderate-to-high intensity — increasing to 80 mg to achieve high-intensity therapy and LDL <70 mg/dL is most appropriate as a first step before adding ezetimibe.
Q221: A 45-year-old female presents with severe hypertriglyceridemia (TG > 700 mg/dL). She has no history of pancreatitis. What is ...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Dyslipidemia✅ B
A) LDL-C reduction
B) Prevention of pancreatitis
C) Total cholesterol reduction
D) Cardiovascular risk reduction
E) HDL-C elevation
Explanation: When TG > 500-1000 mg/dL, the primary concern is prevention of acute pancreatitis (not cardiovascular risk). Fibrates (fenofibrate) and omega-3 fatty acids are first-line for severe hypertriglyceridemia. Statins are used primarily for LDL-C reduction and CV risk, but are not the primary treatment for very high TG.
Q222: A 55-year-old patient is initiated on high-intensity statin therapy. She is also taking gemfibrozil for severe hypertriglycer...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Dyslipidemia✅ A
A) Statin and gemfibrozil; increased risk of rhabdomyolysis
B) Statin and gemfibrozil; increased risk of hyperkalemia
C) Statin and metoprolol; increased risk of bradycardia
D) Statin and warfarin; increased risk of bleeding
E) Statin and levothyroxine; decreased absorption of levothyroxine
Explanation: Gemfibrozil significantly inhibits the glucuronidation of statins (particularly simvastatin, cerivastatin), dramatically increasing statin plasma levels and risk of myopathy/rhabdomyolysis. Fenofibrate is preferred over gemfibrozil when combined with statins as it carries a much lower risk of myopathy.
Q223: A 50-year-old male with ASCVD is on rosuvastatin 20 mg daily. His LDL-C is 85 mg/dL, and the physician wants to achieve a goa...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Dyslipidemia✅ B
A) Increase rosuvastatin to 40 mg daily
B) Add ezetimibe 10 mg daily
C) Add fenofibrate
D) Switch to pravastatin
E) Continue current therapy
Explanation: Rosuvastatin 20 mg is already high-intensity statin therapy. Increasing to 40 mg provides modest additional LDL reduction (~6%). Adding ezetimibe provides an additional 15-25% LDL reduction via a complementary mechanism (intestinal cholesterol absorption inhibition), making it more effective for reaching the <70 mg/dL goal.
Q224: A 68-year-old patient on atorvastatin 80 mg daily complains of severe muscle aches and weakness. CPK level is elevated to 15 ...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Dyslipidemia✅ B
A) Decrease the dose of atorvastatin
B) Discontinue atorvastatin immediately
C) Add coenzyme Q10
D) Switch to a different statin
E) Initiate a fibrate
Explanation: CPK >10x ULN with symptoms constitutes statin-induced myopathy at the severe end — approaching rhabdomyolysis. The statin must be discontinued immediately to prevent rhabdomyolysis, myoglobinuria, and acute kidney injury. After symptoms fully resolve and CPK normalizes, consider re-challenge with a lower dose of a less potent statin (e.g., pravastatin, fluvastatin).
Q225: A 59-year-old man with hypertension, dyslipidemia, ischemic heart disease, and pulmonary hypertension is on aspirin 81 mg, at...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Anticoagulation✅ C
A) Add felodipine
B) Add isosorbide mononitrate
C) Add ranolazine
D) Decrease metoprolol XL to 50 mg daily
E) Increase metoprolol XL to 200 mg daily
Explanation: Nitrates (Answer B) are absolutely contraindicated with sildenafil (PDE5 inhibitor) due to life-threatening hypotension. Felodipine combined with beta-blockers risks bradycardia; also BP is already low (Answer A risky). Increasing metoprolol at already low HR/BP risks bradycardia (Answer E). Ranolazine reduces angina by blocking late sodium channel current (reducing calcium overload) without affecting BP or HR — safe in this context (Answer C).
Q226: A 68-year-old male presents with ADHF. He has significant peripheral edema, orthopnea, and JVD. However, his extremities are ...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ D
A) Warm and Dry (Subset I)
B) Cold and Dry (Subset III)
C) Cold and Wet (Subset IV)
D) Warm and Wet (Subset II)
Explanation: Warm extremities indicate adequate perfusion (warm), while edema, orthopnea, and JVD indicate volume overload (wet). This places the patient in Subset II: Warm and Wet.
Q227: For a patient admitted with ADHF who was previously taking Furosemide 40 mg PO twice daily, what is the recommended initial i...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ C
A) Withhold diuretics until a pulmonary artery catheter is placed.
B) Switch immediately to a continuous infusion of Bumetanide.
C) Administer Furosemide 80 mg to 100 mg IV (2 to 2.5 times the total daily oral dose).
Explanation: The DOSE trial showed that a high-dose strategy (2.5× the home oral dose given IV) was associated with better fluid removal without long-term worsening of renal function.
Q228: A 'Cold and Wet' patient with a low Cardiac Index and a Systolic BP of 82 mmHg requires inotropic support. The patient is cur...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ B
A) Digoxin
B) Milrinone
C) Isoproterenol
D) Dobutamine
Explanation: Milrinone is a PDE-3 inhibitor that works downstream of the beta-receptor. Its efficacy is NOT reduced by chronic beta-blocker therapy. Dobutamine is a direct beta-agonist and its effect is significantly blunted in patients on high-dose beta-blockers.
Q229: A patient is receiving aggressive IV diuresis but has 'diuretic resistance' with minimal urine output. Which medication is mo...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ A
A) Metolazone
B) Mannitol
C) Spironolactone
D) Acetazolamide
Explanation: Metolazone is a thiazide-like diuretic that blocks sodium reabsorption in the distal tubule, counteracting compensatory hypertrophy that occurs with chronic loop diuretic use — the hallmark of 'sequential nephron blockade.'
Q230: What is the primary hemodynamic effect of IV Nitroglycerin in the management of 'Warm and Wet' ADHF?
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ B
A) Direct stimulation of myocardial contractility.
B) Venodilation, leading to a reduction in Pulmonary Capillary Wedge Pressure (PCWP).
C) Potent arterial dilation to increase systemic vascular resistance.
D) Inhibition of the Renin-Angiotensin-Aldosterone System (RAAS).
Explanation: At standard doses, Nitroglycerin acts primarily as a venodilator. This reduces venous return (preload) and lowers PCWP, relieving pulmonary congestion.
Q231: Which of the following is a common, dose-limiting side effect of Milrinone that requires continuous ECG monitoring?
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ B
A) Acute Hypertension.
B) Ventricular arrhythmias.
C) Nephrotoxicity.
D) Thrombocytopenia.
Explanation: By increasing intracellular cAMP in the myocardium, Milrinone is pro-arrhythmic and increases the risk of ventricular tachycardia, requiring continuous ECG monitoring.
Q232: A patient with ADHF and a history of chronic kidney disease is receiving Sodium Nitroprusside. After 48 hours, the patient be...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ C
A) Digoxin-induced hyperkalemia.
B) Hypernatremia from the drug's sodium content.
C) Thiocyanate and Cyanide toxicity.
D) Serotonin Syndrome.
Explanation: Nitroprusside contains cyanide groups. In renal or hepatic impairment, its metabolites (thiocyanate and cyanide) can accumulate, leading to CNS changes and lactic acidosis.
Q233: A patient presents with 'Cold and Dry' (Subset III) symptoms. Their BP is 95/60 mmHg and they have no signs of pulmonary edem...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ B
A) High-dose IV Vasopressin.
B) Careful fluid bolus with normal saline.
C) Immediate start of IV Furosemide.
D) Oral Lisinopril to improve long-term survival.
Explanation: Subset III patients are often 'under-filled' (low preload). A careful fluid bolus improves cardiac output by increasing preload, potentially moving the patient into Subset I (Warm and Dry).
Q234: What is the role of Nesiritide in ADHF management based on the ASCEND-HF trial?
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ C
A) It is used primarily to increase heart rate in bradycardic HF patients.
B) It is the first-line vasodilator for all ADHF patients.
C) It is rarely used because it did not show a mortality benefit or significant reduction in rehospitalization.
D) It is the only medication proven to reverse renal failure in ADHF.
Explanation: Nesiritide (synthetic BNP) failed to show superiority over standard care in the ASCEND-HF trial, with no mortality benefit or significant reduction in rehospitalization, leading to its limited use.
Q235: Which laboratory marker is most useful for 'ruling out' ADHF in a patient presenting to the ED with shortness of breath?
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ C
A) White Blood Cell (WBC) count.
B) Troponin I.
C) BNP or NT-proBNP.
D) Serum Potassium.
Explanation: BNP and NT-proBNP are released in response to myocardial wall stretch. A low value has a very high negative predictive value for heart failure, making them the best 'rule out' markers.
Q236: A patient with ADHF is categorized as 'Cold and Wet' (Subset IV) with a blood pressure of 88/60 mmHg and signs of poor end-or...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ D
A) Aggressive bolus of IV Furosemide 200 mg.
B) Administer 1 liter of Normal Saline to improve the 'Cold' status.
C) Start high-dose IV Nitroglycerin to reduce afterload.
D) Initiate an inotropic agent (e.g., Dobutamine) while continuing cautious diuresis.
Explanation: In Subset IV, restoring cardiac output with an inotrope addresses poor end-organ perfusion, while cautious diuresis simultaneously addresses volume overload.
Q237: A patient is stabilized after an ADHF episode and is ready to transition to oral therapy. They have a history of HFrEF (EF 25...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ D
A) Only after the patient has reached their target dose of an ACE inhibitor.
B) Beta-blockers should be avoided for at least 4 weeks following any ADHF hospitalization.
C) Immediately upon admission to prevent sympathetic surge.
D) As soon as the patient is euvolemic and off all intravenous vasoactive agents.
Explanation: Guidelines recommend restarting beta-blockers once the patient is euvolemic and no longer requires IV vasoactive agents, as hemodynamic stability must be confirmed before reintroducing their negative inotropic effect.
Q238: While receiving aggressive diuresis, a patient's serum creatinine rises from 1.2 to 1.8 mg/dL, but they are still clinically ...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ A
A) It may represent 'permissive' worsening of renal function due to effective decongestion and should not necessarily lead to stopping diuretics.
B) It indicates the patient is now 'Dry' and should receive a fluid bolus.
C) It is a sign of acute tubular necrosis (ATN) and diuretics must be stopped immediately.
D) The patient has developed an allergy to loop diuretics.
Explanation: A modest creatinine rise during aggressive diuresis may represent 'permissive' cardiorenal worsening from effective decongestion. Stopping diuresis in a still-congested patient may worsen outcomes.
Q239: A patient with ADHF and atrial fibrillation is on Digoxin. During aggressive diuresis, their potassium drops to 3.2 mEq/L. Wh...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ C
A) Hypokalemia causes the Digoxin to precipitate in the blood vessels.
B) Low potassium prevents Digoxin from working at all.
C) Hypokalemia increases the risk of Digoxin toxicity and related arrhythmias.
D) Hypokalemia causes Digoxin to be cleared more slowly by the kidneys.
Explanation: Hypokalemia increases the binding of Digoxin to the Na+/K+-ATPase pump, potentiating its toxic effects and dramatically increasing the risk of Digoxin-induced arrhythmias.
Q240: Which of the following is an advantage of using Milrinone over Dobutamine in a patient with ADHF and secondary pulmonary hype...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ C
A) Milrinone is not cleared by the kidneys, so it is safer in renal failure.
B) Milrinone has a much shorter half-life, allowing for quicker 'off' titration.
C) Milrinone provides more significant reduction in pulmonary vascular resistance.
D) Milrinone does not cause hypotension.
Explanation: As a PDE-3 inhibitor, Milrinone reduces cAMP breakdown in both cardiac and pulmonary vascular smooth muscle, leading to more significant pulmonary vasodilation (reduced pulmonary vascular resistance) compared to Dobutamine.
Q241: A patient with ADHF develops 'Diuretic Braking.' What is the physiological mechanism behind this phenomenon?
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ A
A) The activation of the RAAS and sympathetic nervous system causes increased sodium reabsorption in distal segments.
B) The patient has developed antibodies against the Furosemide molecule.
C) The drug's volume of distribution has suddenly doubled.
D) The loop diuretic has permanently damaged the Loop of Henle.
Explanation: 'Diuretic Braking' occurs because loop diuretics activate the RAAS and sympathetic nervous system as a compensatory response, leading to increased sodium reabsorption in distal nephron segments and reduced diuretic efficacy.
Q242: In the management of ADHF, when is the use of an IV Vasopressor (like Norepinephrine) indicated over an inotrope?
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ A
A) When the patient is in cardiogenic shock with persistent hypotension despite inotropic support.
B) To treat pulmonary edema in a 'Warm and Wet' patient.
C) As the first-line treatment for all 'Wet' patients.
D) When the BNP is > 2000 pg/mL.
Explanation: IV vasopressors like Norepinephrine are reserved for cardiogenic shock with persistent hypotension despite inotropic support, to maintain adequate perfusion pressure to vital organs.
Q243: A patient with ADHF is receiving Furosemide. Which acid-base disturbance is most commonly associated with aggressive loop diu...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ B
A) Metabolic acidosis.
B) Metabolic alkalosis.
C) Respiratory alkalosis.
D) Respiratory acidosis.
Explanation: Loop diuretics promote loss of H+, Cl-, and K+. The loss of chloride and potassium leads to hypochloremic, hypokalemic metabolic alkalosis — the most common acid-base disturbance with aggressive loop diuretic therapy.
Q244: Why is it important to monitor the 'Internal Jugular Venous' (IJV) distension or use ultrasound to check the Inferior Vena Ca...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ D
A) To determine the exact ejection fraction of the left ventricle.
B) To screen for carotid artery stenosis.
C) To identify if the patient has a pulmonary embolism.
D) To estimate the Right Atrial Pressure and guide the adequacy of decongestion.
Explanation: Monitoring JVD or IVC collapsibility estimates Right Atrial Pressure (RAP). An elevated non-collapsing IVC indicates persistent elevated RAP, signaling incomplete decongestion and guiding further diuretic therapy.
Q245: Ultrafiltration (aquapheresis) may be considered in ADHF when:
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ A
A) The patient has failed to respond to optimized diuretic therapy (refractory volume overload).
B) Immediately upon arrival to the ER to speed up discharge.
C) The patient's BNP is normal.
D) To treat low blood pressure in 'Cold' patients.
Explanation: Ultrafiltration (aquapheresis) is considered when patients have refractory volume overload that has failed to respond to optimized diuretic therapy, including high-dose loop diuretics and sequential nephron blockade.
Q246: A 55-year-old non-Hispanic black male has hypertension. His past medical history also includes diabetes and hyperlipidemia. A...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Hypertension✅ C
A) Less than /85
B) Less than /85
C) Less than /80
D) Less than /80
Explanation: Goals of therapy differ depending on which guidelines the clinician uses in practice. According to the ACC/AHA guidelines the goal blood pressure for a diabetic patient is less than /80.
Q247: A 59-year-old non-Hispanic white patient presents for treatment of hypertension. His past medical history also includes diabe...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Hypertension✅ A
A) Enalapril
B) Hydralazine
C) Verapamil
D) Metoprolol
Explanation: Enalapril is an ACE inhibitor and is recommended for first-line therapy in various patient populations, including those who have a compelling indication such as diabetes. The other therapies are not considered first-line therapy.
Q248: A 45-year-old male complains of constipation. He was recently started on two antihypertensives due to elevated systolic blood...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Hypertension✅ B
A) Chlorthalidone
B) Verapamil
C) Aspirin
D) Lisinopril
Explanation: Common side effects specific for verapamil include constipation and first-degree atrioventricular block, which typically are dose-dependent. Electrolyte disturbances are often associated with both diuretics (chlorthalidone) and ACE inhibitors (lisinopril).
Q249: Which antihypertensive medication can cause the rare side effect of angioedema?
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Hypertension✅ B
A) Amlodipine
B) Fosinopril
C) Prazosin
D) Propranolol
Explanation: ACE inhibitors (fosinopril), ARBs (for example, losartan), and renin inhibitors (aliskiren) can cause angioedema. The occurrence of angioedema is more common with ACE inhibitors. Amlodipine can cause dizziness, headache, and peripheral edema. Prazosin can cause reflex tachycardia and postural hypotension. Propranolol can cause insomnia, decreased libido, fatigue, and bradycardia.
Q250: A 52-year-old female has uncontrolled hypertension (blood pressure /82 mm Hg) on treatment with lisinopril. She recently had ...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Hypertension✅ D
A) Clonidine
B) Olmesartan
C) Furosemide
D) Metoprolol
Explanation: Individual patient care is warranted particularly in the case of a compelling indication for certain medication. Considering her recent myocardial infarction, the best choice is a β1-blocker (metoprolol). It is not appropriate to combine an ACE inhibitor (lisinopril) and ARB (olmesartan). The other agents are not considered first-line therapy and do not have a compelling indication for addition to the regimen.
Q251: The blood pressure of a patient with essential hypertension is at goal on treatment with enalapril. Since initiation of enala...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Hypertension✅ C
A) Discontinue enalapril.
B) Reduce dose of enalapril.
C) Continue current dose of enalapril.
D) Increase dose of enalapril.
Explanation: The blood pressure is at goal. Electrolytes (such as potassium) and serum creatinine should be monitored in patients who initiate ACE inhibitors. Increases in serum creatinine up to 30% above baseline are acceptable and do not warrant discontinuation or reduction of treatment. Since the blood pressure is at goal, increasing the enalapril is not necessary.
Q252: Which of the following correctly outlines a major difference in electrolyte disturbances associated with thiazide and loop di...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Hypertension✅ D
A) Thiazide diuretics decrease potassium and loop diuretics increase potassium.
B) Thiazide diuretics increase potassium and loop diuretics decrease potassium.
C) Thiazide diuretics decrease calcium and loop diuretics increase calcium.
D) Thiazide diuretics increase calcium and loop diuretics decrease calcium.
Explanation: Thiazide and loop diuretics decrease potassium, sodium and magnesium. However, thiazide diuretics increase calcium (through reduced urinary excretion), while loop diuretics reduce calcium (through enhanced urinary excretion).
Q253: Which can precipitate a hypertensive crisis following abrupt cessation of therapy?
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Hypertension✅ A
A) Clonidine
B) Diltiazem
C) Valsartan
D) Hydrochlorothiazide
Explanation: Increased sympathetic nervous system activity occurs if clonidine therapy is abruptly stopped after prolonged administration. Uncontrolled elevation of blood pressure can occur. Patients should be slowly weaned from clonidine while other antihypertensive medications are initiated. The other drugs on the list do not produce this phenomenon.
Q254: Which of the following is a dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker?
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Hypertension✅ A
A) Amlodipine
B) Metoprolol
C) Verapamil
D) Lisinopril
Explanation: There are three classes of calcium channel blockers: nondihydropyridines (benzothiazepines, diphenylalkylamines) and dihydropyridines. Amlodipine is a member of the dihydropyridine class of calcium channel blockers, which also includes nifedipine and felodipine. Verapamil is a benzothiazepine calcium channel blocker, metoprolol is a β-blocker, and lisinopril is an ACE inhibitor.
Q255: A 45-year-old man was started on therapy for hypertension and developed a persistent, dry cough. Which is most likely respons...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Hypertension✅ A
A) Lisinopril
B) Losartan
C) Nifedipine
D) Atenolol
Explanation: The cough is most likely an adverse effect of the ACE inhibitor lisinopril. Losartan is an ARB that has the same beneficial effects as an ACE inhibitor but is less likely to produce a cough. Nifedipine and atenolol do not cause this side effect.
Q256: An elderly patient with a history of heart disease has difficulty breathing and is diagnosed with acute pulmonary edema. Whic...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ C
A) Acetazolamide
B) Chlorthalidone
C) Furosemide
D) Spironolactone
Explanation: This is a potentially fatal situation. It is important to administer a diuretic that reduces fluid accumulation in the lungs and improves oxygenation and heart function. The loop diuretics are most effective in removing large fluid volumes from the body and are the treatment of choice in this situation. In this situation, furosemide should be administered intravenously. The other choices are inappropriate.
Q257: A group of college students is planning a mountain climbing trip to the Andes. Which is most appropriate for them to take to ...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ C
A) A thiazide diuretic such as hydrochlorothiazide
B) An anticholinergic such as atropine
C) A carbonic anhydrase inhibitor such as acetazolamide
D) A loop diuretic such as furosemide
Explanation: Acetazolamide is used prophylactically for several days before an ascent above 10, feet. This treatment prevents the cerebral and pulmonary problems associated with altitude sickness as well as other difficulties, such as nausea.
Q258: An alcoholic male has developed hepatic cirrhosis. To control the ascites and edema, which should be prescribed?
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ D
A) Acetazolamide
B) Chlorthalidone
C) Furosemide
D) Spironolactone
Explanation: Spironolactone is very effective in the treatment of hepatic edema. These patients are frequently resistant to the diuretic action of loop diuretics, although a combination with spironolactone may be beneficial. The other agents are not indicated.
Q259: A 55-year-old male with kidney stones needs a medication to decrease urinary calcium excretion. Which diuretic is best for th...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ B
A) Torsemide
B) Hydrochlorothiazide
C) Spironolactone
D) Triamterene
Explanation: Hydrochlorothiazide is effective in increasing calcium reabsorption, thus decreasing the amount of calcium excreted, and decreasing the formation of kidney stones that contain calcium phosphate or calcium oxalate. Furosemide increases the excretion of calcium, whereas the K+ -sparing diuretics, spironolactone, and triamterene do not have an effect.
Q260: A 75-year-old woman with hypertension and glaucoma is being treated with chlorthalidone, amlodipine, lisinopril, and acetazol...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ C
A) Amlodipine
B) Acetazolamide
C) Chlorthalidone
D) Lisinopril
Explanation: Thiazides such as chlorthalidone compete with uric acid for secretion into the lumen of the nephron at the proximal convoluted tubule. This competition decreases uric acid secretion, raising the serum concentration and increasing the risk of a gout attack. Loop diuretics have the same risk.
Q261: Which is contraindicated in a patient with hyperkalemia?
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ D
A) Acetazolamide
B) Chlorothiazide
C) Ethacrynic acid
D) Eplerenone
Explanation: Eplerenone acts in the collecting tubule via aldosterone antagonism to inhibit Na+ reabsorption and K+ excretion. It is extremely important that patients who are treated with any potassium-sparing diuretic be closely monitored for potassium levels. Exogenous potassium supplementation is usually discontinued when potassium-sparing diuretic therapy is initiated. The other drugs promote the excretion of potassium.
Q262: A 59-year-old male patient in the intensive care unit has a metabolic alkalosis. Which therapy will treat this condition?
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ D
A) Amiloride
B) Hydrochlorothiazide
C) Mannitol
D) Acetazolamide
Explanation: Acetazolamide causes an increase in the urinary excretion of bicarbonate, lowering the pH of the blood.
Q263: A male patient is placed on a new medication and notes that his breasts have become enlarged and tender to the touch. Which m...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ C
A) Furosemide
B) Hydrochlorothiazide
C) Spironolactone
D) Triamterene
Explanation: An adverse drug reaction to spironolactone is gynecomastia due to its effects on androgens and progesterone in the body. Eplerenone may be a suitable alternative if the patient is in need of an aldosterone antagonist but has a history of gynecomastia.
Q264: A patient with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction researched his medications on the Internet and found he was takin...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ B
A) Spironolactone is used to prevent hyponatremia.
B) Spironolactone is used to reduce heart structure changes and decrease the risk of death.
C) Bumetanide is used to decrease the potassium lost from spironolactone therapy.
D) This is a duplication error and one diuretic should be stopped.
Explanation: Aldosterone antagonists are used at non-diuretic doses in heart failure to prevent myocardial remodeling and decrease mortality. Bumetanide is used as a diuretic to treat edema from heart failure. Both are appropriate to use together because of the unique indications. Spironolactone reduces the potassium lost from diuresis with bumetanide.
Q265: Which diuretic has been shown to improve blood pressure in resistant hypertension or those already treated with three blood p...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ D
A) Indapamide
B) Furosemide
C) Mannitol
D) Spironolactone
Explanation: Resistant hypertension, defined by the use of three or more medications without reaching the blood pressure goal, often responds well to aldosterone antagonists. This effect can be seen in those with or without elevated aldosterone levels.
Q266: A patient is newly diagnosed with HFrEF and is asymptomatic. Which is the most appropriate drug to initiate for symptomatic a...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ C
A) Dobutamine
B) Furosemide
C) Lisinopril
D) Sacubitril/valsartan
Explanation: ACE inhibitors should be initiated in all patients, unless contraindicated, if they have HFrEF and are asymptomatic. This is known as stage B HF. Dobutamine and furosemide improve symptoms only. Sacubitril/valsartan will replace an ACE inhibitor if the patient remains symptomatic on optimal HF pharmacotherapy.
Q267: Which of the following statements best describes the action of ACE inhibitors on the failing heart?
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ C
A) Increased vascular resistance.
B) Decreased cardiac output.
C) Reduced preload.
D) Increased aldosterone.
Explanation: ACE inhibitors decrease vascular resistance, decrease preload, decrease afterload, and increase cardiac output. In addition, ACE inhibitors blunt aldosterone release.
Q268: A Hispanic man with HFrEF currently takes maximally tolerated doses of metoprolol succinate and enalapril, along with moderat...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ D
A) Stop enalapril, wait 36 hours, and start sacubitril/valsartan.
B) Start digoxin.
C) Start fixed-dose hydralazine and isosorbide dinitrate.
D) Start spironolactone.
Explanation: Because the patient is on optimal pharmacotherapy and continues to have symptoms, another agent is warranted. Adding low-dose spironolactone is unlikely to decrease the blood pressure and will confer a survival and symptomatic benefit. Changing to sacubitril/valsartan will likely worsen the low blood pressure. Digoxin will only improve symptoms and not improve survival. Fixed-dose hydralazine and isosorbide dinitrate would be appropriate if the patient were African American.
Q269: β-Blockers improve cardiac function in HF by
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ A
A) decreasing cardiac remodeling
B) increasing heart rate
C) increasing renin release
D) activating norepinephrine
Explanation: Although it seems counterintuitive to decrease heart rate in HF, β-blockers improve cardiac function by slowing heart rate, decreasing renin release, and preventing the direct effects of norepinephrine on cardiac muscle to decrease remodeling.
Q270: A 70-year-old woman has HFrEF and hypertension. She takes lisinopril and metoprolol tartrate. She feels well and has no cough...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ D
A) Initiate digoxin.
B) Change lisinopril to losartan.
C) Initiate ivabradine.
D) Change metoprolol tartrate to metoprolol succinate.
Explanation: Metoprolol succinate should be used in HF, given that there is mortality benefit shown with metoprolol succinate in landmark HF trials. Digoxin and ivabradine are not indicated since symptoms are minimal and she is not yet on optimized guideline-directed medical therapy; there is no reason to change to an ARB since the patient has no cough or history of angioedema.
Q271: A 75-year-old white man has HFrEF and reports stable HF symptoms. His current drug therapy includes optimal-dose enalapril, c...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ C
A) Start fixed-dose hydralazine/isosorbide dinitrate.
B) Start ivabradine.
C) Replace enalapril with sacubitril/valsartan.
D) Start digoxin.
Explanation: Since patient is on optimal doses of HF medications and he continues to have symptoms, replacing enalapril with sacubitril/valsartan is the only option that improves both symptoms and survival in a white patient.
Q272: How is spironolactone beneficial in HF?
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ C
A) Promotes potassium secretion
B) Acts as aldosterone agonist
C) Prevents cardiac hypertrophy
D) Decreases blood glucose
Explanation: Spironolactone antagonizes aldosterone, which in turn prevents salt/water retention, cardiac hypertrophy, and hypokalemia. Spironolactone has endocrine effects on hormones, but not on glucose.
Q273: Which of the following is important to monitor in patients taking digoxin?
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ B
A) Chloride
B) Potassium
C) Sodium
D) Zinc
Explanation: Hypokalemia can lead to life-threatening arrhythmias and increases the potential of cardiac toxicity with digoxin.
Q274: Which of the following describes the mechanism of action of milrinone in HF?
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ B
A) Decreases intracellular calcium
B) Increases cardiac contractility
C) Decreases cAMP
D) Activates phosphodiesterase
Explanation: Milrinone is a phosphodiesterase inhibitor that leads to increased cAMP, increased intracellular calcium, and therefore increased contractility.
Q275: BH is a 52-year-old African American woman who has HFrEF. She is seen in clinic today reporting stable HF symptoms, but is ha...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ D
A) Stop all HF medications immediately.
B) Discontinue sacubitril/valsartan only.
C) Do nothing; this adverse effect will slowly improve over time.
D) Reduce the dose of ivabradine.
Explanation: Luminous phenomena can occur with ivabradine. This adverse effect can be minimized with dose reduction or discontinuation.
Q276: A 60-year-old woman had a myocardial infarction. Which agent should be used to prevent life-threatening arrhythmias that can ...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Arrhythmia✅ C
A) Digoxin
B) Flecainide
C) Metoprolol
D) Procainamide
Explanation: β-Blockers such as metoprolol prevent arrhythmias that occur subsequent to a myocardial infarction. None of the other drugs has been shown to be effective in preventing postinfarct arrhythmias. Flecainide should be avoided in patients with structural heart disease.
Q277: Suppression of arrhythmias resulting from a reentry focus is most likely to occur if the drug:
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Arrhythmia✅ C
A) has vagomimetic effects on the AV node
B) is a β-blocker
C) converts a unidirectional block to a bidirectional block
D) slows conduction through the atria
Explanation: Current theory holds that a reentrant arrhythmia is caused by damaged heart muscle, so that conduction is slowed through the damaged area in only one direction. A drug that prevents conduction in either direction through the damaged area interrupts the reentrant arrhythmia. Class I antiarrhythmics, such as lidocaine, are capable of producing bidirectional block. The other choices do not have any direct effects on the direction of blockade of conduction through damaged cardiac muscle.
Q278: A 57-year-old man is being treated for an atrial arrhythmia. He complains of dry mouth, blurred vision, and urinary hesitancy...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Arrhythmia✅ B
A) Metoprolol
B) Disopyramide
C) Dronedarone
D) Sotalol
Explanation: The clustered symptoms of dry mouth, blurred vision, and urinary hesitancy are characteristic of anticholinergic adverse effects, which are caused by class IA agents (in this case, disopyramide). The other drugs do not cause anticholinergic effects.
Q279: A 58-year-old woman is being treated for chronic suppression of a ventricular arrhythmia. After 1 week of therapy, she compla...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Arrhythmia✅ C
A) Amiodarone
B) Digoxin
C) Mexiletine
D) Propranolol
Explanation: The patient is exhibiting a classic adverse effect of mexiletine. None of the other agents listed are likely to cause dyspepsia.
Q280: A 78-year-old woman has been newly diagnosed with atrial fibrillation. She is not currently having symptoms of palpitations o...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Arrhythmia✅ D
A) Dronedarone
B) Esmolol
C) Flecainide
D) Metoprolol
Explanation: Only B and D are options to control rate. The other options are used for rhythm control in patients with atrial fibrillation. Since esmolol is IV only, the only option to start as an outpatient is metoprolol.
Q281: Which of the following is correct regarding digoxin when used for atrial fibrillation?
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Arrhythmia✅ D
A) Digoxin works by blocking voltage-sensitive calcium channels.
B) Digoxin is used for rhythm control in patients with atrial fibrillation.
C) Digoxin increases conduction velocity through the AV node.
D) Digoxin levels of 1 to 2 ng/mL are desirable in the treatment of atrial fibrillation.
Explanation: Digoxin works by inhibiting the Na+/K+ -ATPase pump. It decreases conduction velocity through the AV node and is used for rate control in atrial fibrillation (not rhythm control). Digoxin levels between 1 and 2 ng/mL are more likely to exhibit negative chronotropic effects desired in atrial fibrillation or flutter. A serum drug concentration between 0.5 and 0.8 ng/mL is for symptomatic management of heart failure.
Q282: All of the following are adverse effects of amiodarone except:
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Arrhythmia✅ A
A) Cinchonism
B) Hypothyroidism
C) Pulmonary fibrosis
D) Blue skin discoloration
Explanation: Cinchonism is a constellation of symptoms (blurred vision, tinnitus, headache, psychosis) that is known to occur with quinidine. All other options are adverse effects with amiodarone that require close monitoring.
Q283: Which arrhythmia can be treated with lidocaine?
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Arrhythmia✅ D
A) Paroxysmal supraventricular ventricular tachycardia
B) Atrial fibrillation
C) Atrial flutter
D) Ventricular tachycardia
Explanation: Lidocaine has little effect on atrial or AV nodal tissue; thus, it used for ventricular arrhythmias such as ventricular tachycardia.
Q284: A clinician would like to initiate a drug for rhythm control of atrial fibrillation. Which of the following coexisting condit...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Arrhythmia✅ A
A) Hypertension
B) Left ventricular hypertrophy
C) Coronary artery disease
D) Heart failure
Explanation: Since flecainide can increase the risk of sudden cardiac death in those with a history of structural heart disease, only coexisting hypertension will allow for flecainide initiation. Structural heart disease includes left ventricular hypertrophy, heart failure, and atherosclerotic heart disease.
Q285: Which statement regarding dronedarone is correct?
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Arrhythmia✅ C
A) Dronedarone is more effective than is amiodarone.
B) QT interval prolongation is not a risk with dronedarone.
C) Dronedarone increases the risk of death in patients with permanent atrial fibrillation or symptomatic heart failure.
D) There is no need to monitor liver function with dronedarone.
Explanation: Dronedarone is not as effective as amiodarone, QT prolongation is a risk with this drug, and liver function should be monitored when taking dronedarone since it increases the risk of liver failure. The drug is contraindicated in those with symptomatic heart failure or permanent atrial fibrillation due to an increased risk of death.
Q286: Which of the following best describes stable angina?
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Angina✅ C
A) Angina that occurs more frequently or with progressively less exercise or stress than before
B) Angina due to spasm of coronary arteries
C) Angina due to increased myocardial demand which is reproducible and relieved by rest or nitroglycerin
D) Angina pain accompanied by increases in serum biomarkers of myocardial necrosis
Explanation: When the pattern of the chest pain and the amount of effort needed to trigger the chest pain does not vary over time, the angina is named “stable angina. ”
Q287: Which medication should be prescribed to all angina patients to treat an acute attack?
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Angina✅ C
A) Isosorbide dinitrate
B) Nitroglycerin patch
C) Nitroglycerin sublingual tablet or spray
D) Ranolazine
Explanation: The other options will not provide prompt relief of angina and should not be used to treat an acute attack.
Q288: Which of the following instructions is important to communicate to a patient receiving a prescription for the nitroglycerin p...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Angina✅ D
A) Apply the patch at onset of angina symptoms for quick relief.
B) Remove the old patch after 24 hours of use, then immediately apply the next patch to prevent any breakthrough angina pain.
C) Do not use sublingual nitroglycerin in combination with the patch.
D) Have a nitrate-free interval of 10 to 12 hours every day to prevent development of nitrate tolerance.
Explanation: Nitrate-free intervals help prevent the development of nitrate tolerance. Sublingual nitroglycerin should be used to treat breakthrough angina due to its quick onset of action; transdermal nitroglycerin has a delayed onset of action.
Q289: A 64-year-old man was prescribed atenolol and sublingual nitroglycerin after his recent hospitalization for unstable angina. ...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Angina✅ A
A) Sildenafil
B) Amlodipine
C) Metformin
D) Lisinopril
Explanation: Sildenafil and other PDE-5 inhibitors can potentiate the vasodilator effects of nitrates and cause an unsafe drop in blood pressure. Concomitant use of nitrates and PDE-5 inhibitors should be avoided.
Q290: Which of the following correctly ranks the calcium channel blockers from most active on the myocardium to most peripherally a...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Angina✅ B
A) Diltiazem, amlodipine, verapamil
B) Verapamil, diltiazem, nifedipine
C) Nifedipine, verapamil, diltiazem
D) Amlodipine, diltiazem, verapamil
Explanation: Verapamil has the most negative inotropic effects, nifedipine is a peripheral vasodilator, and diltiazem is intermediate with actions on both myocardial and peripheral calcium channels.
Q291: A 76-year-old man with uncontrolled hypertension is experiencing typical angina pain that is relieved with rest and sublingua...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Angina✅ D
A) Ranolazine
B) Verapamil
C) Metoprolol
D) Amlodipine
Explanation: Amlodipine is the best choice because it will improve angina as well as help control blood pressure, without further reducing the heart rate. Adding verapamil or metoprolol may worsen the bradycardia. Ranolazine may help the angina, but it will not improve the hypertension.
Q292: A 65-year-old male experiences uncontrolled angina attacks that limit his ability to do household chores. He is adherent to a...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Angina✅ C
A) Nifedipine
B) Aspirin
C) Ranolazine
D) Verapamil
Explanation: Ranolazine is the best answer. The patient’s blood pressure is low, and verapamil and nifedipine may drop blood pressure further. Verapamil may also decrease heart rate. Ranolazine can be used when other agents are maximized, especially when blood pressure is well controlled. The patient will need a baseline ECG and lab work to ensure safe use of this medication.
Q293: A 62-year-old man with ischemic heart disease complains of angina pain that has been getting progressively worse over the pas...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Angina✅ C
A) Change nitroglycerin to a long-acting nitrate.
B) Initiate metoprolol.
C) Initiate ranolazine.
D) Refer him to the nearest emergency department for immediate evaluation.
Explanation: Crescendo angina is indicative of unstable angina that requires immediate evaluation.
Q294: Which is correct regarding antianginal therapy in patients with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction?
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Angina✅ A
A) β-Blockers have been associated with reduced mortality.
B) Dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers should be avoided.
C) β-Blockers with ISA are preferred over those without ISA.
D) Nondihydropyridine calcium channel blockers should be used in patients with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction who cannot tolerate β-blockers.
Explanation: β-Blockers have been shown to reduce mortality in heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, but β-blockers with ISA should be avoided in these patients. Dihydropyridine calcium channel blockers can be used in patients with heart failure with reduced ejection fraction, but nondihydropyridine calcium channel blockers should be avoided due to negative inotropic effects.
Q295: A 45-year-old woman with type 1 diabetes has been diagnosed with Prinzmetal angina. Which of the following is correct regardi...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Angina✅ D
A) β-Blockers are the treatment of choice but should be avoided because of her diabetes.
B) Nitroglycerin is not beneficial for this type of angina.
C) She should be counseled to take nitroglycerin before physical activity to prevent symptoms.
D) Felodipine will be more effective than verapamil.
Explanation: Prinzmetal or vasospastic angina responds well to vasodilators, including the dihydropyridine calcium channel blocker felodipine. Verapamil is a weak vasodilator. Beta-blockers may be used with caution in patients with diabetes, but these drugs are less effective options for Prinzmetal angina. Nitrates are also effective, but Prinzmetal angina is provoked by coronary artery vasospasm rather than physical activity.
Q296: Which of the P2Y12 ADP receptor antagonists reversibly binds the receptor?
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Anticoagulation✅ C
A) Clopidogrel
B) Prasugrel
C) Ticagrelor
D) Ticlopidine
Explanation: Of the P2Y12 ADP receptor antagonists listed, ticagrelor is the only one that reversibly binds the receptor. This is important when it comes to compliance. If a patient is not compliant, then the antiplatelet activity of ticagrelor stops when the drug is missed (since the platelets are not irreversibly inhibited as they would be with aspirin, clopidogrel, or prasugrel).
Q297: A 70-year-old woman is diagnosed with nonvalvular atrial fibrillation. Her past medical history is significant for chronic ki...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Anticoagulation✅ D
A) Apixaban
B) Dabigatran
C) Rivaroxaban
D) Warfarin
Explanation: Warfarin does not require dosage adjustment in renal dysfunction. The INR is monitored and dosage adjustments are made on the basis of this information. All of the other agents are renally cleared to some extent and require dosage adjustments in renal dysfunction.
