Q298: An 80-year-old man is taking warfarin indefinitely for the prevention of deep venous thrombosis. He is compliant, has a stabl...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Anticoagulation✅ B
A) Decreased anticoagulant effect of warfarin
B) Increased anticoagulant effect of warfarin
C) Activation of platelet activity
D) No change in anticoagulation status
Explanation: Sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim has a significant drug interaction with warfarin, such that it inhibits warfarin metabolism. Therefore, sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim will cause increased anticoagulant effects, and the patient will need to have his warfarin dose decreased and INR checked frequently while he is on this antibiotic.
Q299: A 47-year-old woman presents to the emergency room with severe bleeding. Upon evaluation of the medical record, you discover ...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Anticoagulation✅ C
A) Protamine
B) Vitamin K
C) Idarucizumab
D) A reversal agent does not exist for this medication
Explanation: Idarucizumab is used to reverse bleeding caused by dabigatran. By binding to dabigatran and its metabolites, idarucizumab neutralizes anticoagulation. It would be important to monitor this patient for any signs of thrombosis due to reversal of her anticoagulation. Vitamin K is the antidote for warfarin, and protamine is the antidote for heparin.
Q300: Which must heparin bind to in order to exert its anticoagulant effect?
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Anticoagulation✅ C
A) GP IIb/IIIa receptor
B) Thrombin
C) Antithrombin III
D) von Willebrand factor
Explanation: Heparin binds to antithrombin III, causing a conformational change. This heparin/antithrombin III complex then inactivates thrombin and factor Xa.
Q301: Which is considered "fibrin selective" because it rapidly activates plasminogen that is bound to fibrin?
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Anticoagulation✅ A
A) Alteplase
B) Fondaparinux
C) Argatroban
D) Bivalirudin
Explanation: Alteplase has a low affinity for free plasminogen in the plasma, but it rapidly activates plasminogen that is bound to fibrin in a thrombus or a hemostatic plug. It has the advantage of lysing only fibrin, without unwanted degradation of other proteins (notably fibrinogen).
Q302: A 56-year-old man presents to the emergency room with complaints of swelling, redness, and pain in his right leg. The patient...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Anticoagulation✅ A
A) Rivaroxaban
B) Betrixaban
C) Enoxaparin
D) Clopidogrel
Explanation: Betrixaban is only approved for the prophylaxis of DVT and PE; it is not approved for the treatment of acute DVT. Enoxaparin is used for the treatment of DVT, but it is an injectable medication. Clopidogrel is an antiplatelet medication that is not appropriate for acute treatment of DVT.
Q303: Which is most appropriate for reversing the anticoagulant effects of heparin?
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Anticoagulation✅ B
A) Aminocaproic acid
B) Protamine sulfate
C) Vitamin K1
D) Tranexamic acid
Explanation: Excessive bleeding may be managed by ceasing administration of heparin or by treating with protamine sulfate. Infused slowly, protamine sulfate combines ionically with heparin to form a stable, inactive complex. Aminocaproic acid and tranexamic acid are approved for the treatment of hemorrhage but do not specifically reverse the effects of heparin to stop bleeding. Vitamin K1 is used to help reverse the effects of warfarin-induced bleeding.
Q304: A 62-year-old man taking warfarin for stroke prevention in atrial fibrillation presents to his primary care physician with an...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Anticoagulation✅ C
A) 1 hour
B) 6 hours
C) 24 hours
D) 72 hours
Explanation: Vitamin K1 takes about 24 hours to see a reduction in the INR. This is due to the time required for the body to synthesize new coagulation factors.
Q305: A 58-year-old man receives intravenous alteplase treatment for acute stroke. Five minutes following completion of alteplase i...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Anticoagulation✅ A
A) ACE inhibitor
B) GP IIb/IIIa receptor antagonist
C) Phosphodiesterase inhibitor
D) Thiazide diuretic
Explanation: ACE inhibitors, aspirin, and prasugrel all have possible adverse effects including angioedema. In the setting of alteplase administration, ACE inhibitors have been associated with an increased risk of developing angioedema with concomitant use.
Q306: Which of the following is the most common adverse effect of antihyperlipidemic drug therapy?
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Dyslipidemia✅ B
A) Elevated blood pressure
B) Gastrointestinal disturbance
C) Neurologic problems
D) Heart palpitations
Explanation: Gastrointestinal disturbances frequently occur as an adverse effect of antihyperlipidemic drug therapy. The other choices are not seen as often.
Q307: Which of the following hyperlipidemias is characterized by elevated plasma levels of chylomicrons and has no drug therapy ava...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Dyslipidemia✅ A
A) Type I
B) Type II
C) Type III
D) Type IV
Explanation: Type I hyperlipidemia (hyperchylomicronemia) is treated with a low-fat diet. No drug therapy is effective for this disorder.
Q308: Which of the following drugs decreases cholesterol synthesis by inhibiting the enzyme 3-hydroxy-3- methylglutaryl coenzyme A ...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Dyslipidemia✅ C
A) Fenofibrate
B) Cholestyramine
C) Lovastatin
D) Gemfibrozil
Explanation: Lovastatin decreases cholesterol synthesis by inhibiting HMG CoA reductase. Fenofibrate and gemfibrozil increase the activity of lipoprotein lipase, thereby increasing the removal of VLDL from plasma. Cholestyramine lowers the amount of bile acids returning to the liver via the enterohepatic circulation.
Q309: Which of the following nonstatin drugs lowers LDL-C most effectively?
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Dyslipidemia✅ B
A) Niacin
B) Alirocumab
C) Cholestyramine
D) Ezetimibe
Explanation: Alirocumab is a PCSK9 inhibitor that can lower LDL-C by up to 70% in patients on statin therapy. Niacin primarily raises HDL-C and decreases triglycerides, with less potent effects on LDL-C lowering. Cholestyramine and ezetimibe both lower LDL-C, although not as potently as PCSK9 inhibitors.
Q310: Which of the following drugs binds bile acids in the intestine, thus preventing their return to the liver via the enterohepat...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Dyslipidemia✅ C
A) Niacin
B) Fenofibrate
C) Cholestyramine
D) Fluvastatin
Explanation: Cholestyramine is an anion-exchange resin that binds negatively charged bile acids and bile salts in the small intestine. The resin/bile acid complex is excreted in the feces, thus preventing the bile acids from returning to the liver by the enterohepatic circulation. The other choices do not bind intestinal bile acids.
Q311: A 65-year-old man has type 2 diabetes mellitus and an LDL-C of mg/dL. Which is the best option to lower LDL-C and decrease th...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Dyslipidemia✅ C
A) Fenofibrate
B) Colesevelam
C) Rosuvastatin
D) Ezetimibe
Explanation: Rosuvastatin, an HMG CoA reductase inhibitor (statin), is the most effective option for lowering LDL-C, achieving reductions of up to 60% from baseline levels. Statins are the primary modality for reducing ASCVD risk when drug therapy is indicated. Fenofibrate is more effective at lowering triglyceride levels or raising HDL-C. Colesevelam can reduce LDL-C, but not as effectively as statins. Ezetimibe lowers LDL-C modestly compared to the LDL-C reduction achieved by statins.
Q312: A 62-year-old female with hyperlipidemia and hypothyroidism is prescribed cholestyramine and levothyroxine (thyroid hormone)....
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Dyslipidemia✅ B
A) Stop taking the levothyroxine as it can interact with cholestyramine.
B) Take levothyroxine 1 hour before cholestyramine on an empty stomach.
C) Switch cholestyramine to colesevelam as this eliminates the interaction.
D) Switch cholestyramine to colestipol as this eliminates the interaction.
Explanation: Cholestyramine and the bile acid sequestrants can bind several medications, causing decreased absorption of medications such as levothyroxine. Administration of levothyroxine 1 hour before or 4 to 6 hours after cholestyramine can help to avoid this interaction. Choices C and D are incorrect, as all bile acid sequestrants cause this interaction. Choice A is incorrect, as this patient should not stop her thyroid medication.
Q313: A 42-year-old man was started on sustained-release niacin 2 weeks ago. He reports uncomfortable flushing and itchiness that h...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Dyslipidemia✅ A
A) Administer aspirin 30 minutes prior to taking niacin.
B) Administer aspirin 30 minutes after taking niacin.
C) Increase the dose of niacin.
D) Change the sustained-release niacin to immediate-release niacin.
Explanation: Flushing associated with niacin is prostaglandin mediated; therefore, use of aspirin (a prostaglandin inhibitor) can help to minimize this adverse effect. It must be administered 30 minutes before the dose of the niacin; therefore, choice B is incorrect. Increasing the dose of niacin is likely to increase these complaints; therefore, choice C is incorrect. The sustained-release formulation of niacin has less incidence of flushing versus that of the immediate release; therefore, choice D is incorrect.
Q314: A 72-year-old man with hyperlipidemia and renal insufficiency has been treated with high-intensity atorvastatin for 6 months....
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Dyslipidemia✅ B
A) Fenofibrate
B) Niacin
C) Colestipol
D) Gemfibrozil
Explanation: This patient has significantly elevated triglycerides and low HDL-C. Niacin can lower triglycerides by 35% to 50% and also raise HDL-C. The fibrates (fenofibrate and gemfibrozil) should not be used due to the history of renal insufficiency. In addition, the use of gemfibrozil with statins should be avoided. Colestipol should not be used because triglycerides are greater than mg/dL.
Q315: Which patient population is most likely to experience myalgia (muscle pain) or myopathy with use of HMG CoA reductase inhibit...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Dyslipidemia✅ A
A) Patients with renal insufficiency
B) Patients with gout
C) Patients with hypertriglyceridemia
D) Patients taking warfarin (blood thinner)
Explanation: Patients with a history of renal insufficiency have a higher incidence of developing myalgias, myopathy, and rhabdomyolysis with use of HMG CoA reductase inhibitors (statins), especially with those that are renally eliminated as drug accumulation can occur. The other populations have not been reported to have a higher incidence of this adverse effect with HMG CoA reductase inhibitors.
Q316: Which option is most appropriate for a patient with newly diagnosed hyperthyroidism in the first trimester of pregnancy?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Thyroid✅ B
A) Methimazole
B) Propylthiouracil (PTU)
C) Radioactive iodine
D) Surgical removal of the thyroid
Explanation: Methimazole is generally preferred over PTU because it has a longer half-life and lower incidence of adverse effects. However, PTU is recommended in the first trimester of pregnancy due to a greater risk of teratogenic effects with methimazole. Surgery is not ideal in a pregnant patient. Radioactive iodine is contraindicated due to potential effects on the fetus.
Q317: Which drug is beneficial in the treatment of patients with acromegaly?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Pituitary✅ B
A) Cosyntropin
B) Lanreotide
C) Oxytocin
D) Somatropin
Explanation: Lanreotide is a synthetic analog of somatostatin, which inhibits GH. Acromegaly is characterized by an excess of GH. Cosyntropin is used as a diagnostic tool in adrenal insufficiency. Oxytocin is used for induction of labor. Somatropin is synthetic human GH, so it would not be beneficial.
Q318: A 40-year-old female is undergoing infertility treatments. Which drug might be included in her treatment regimen?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Pituitary✅ B
A) Cabergoline
B) Follitropin
C) Methimazole
D) Vasopressin
Explanation: Follitropin is a recombinant version of FSH that causes ovarian follicular growth and maturation. Cabergoline is a dopamine agonist that is used for hyperprolactinemia. Methimazole is the treatment of choice for hyperthyroidism. Vasopressin is an antidiuretic hormone.
Q319: A 29-year-old female has a TSH of 13.5 mIU/L (normal 0.5 to 4.7 mIU/L). Which agent is most appropriate to treat the TSH abno...
🔬 Endocrine📂 Thyroid✅ A
A) Levothyroxine
B) Liothyronine
C) Liotrix
D) Propylthiouracil
Explanation: This patient presents with hypothyroidism as evidenced by high TSH. Levothyroxine is preferred due to its long half-life and better tolerability. Liothyronine (T3) and liotrix (T3/T4) are not as well tolerated. Propylthiouracil is used in the treatment of hyperthyroidism.
Q320: Which agent is correctly paired with an appropriate clinical use of the drug?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Pituitary✅ A
A) Desmopressin—treatment of diabetes insipidus
B) Goserelin—growth hormone deficiency
C) hCG—treatment of bleeding esophageal varices
D) Octreotide—treatment of infertility
Explanation: Goserelin is a GnRH analog that is used for the treatment of prostate cancer or endometriosis. HCG is used in the treatment of infertility. Octreotide is used in the treatment of bleeding esophageal varices.
Q321: Which agent is used in infertility treatment to mimic the action of luteinizing hormone and stimulate ovulation?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Pituitary✅ B
A) Cetrorelix
B) Choriogonadotropin alfa
C) Ganirelix
D) Leuprolide
Explanation: Effects of choriogonadotropin alfa (recombinant hCG) are similar to LH and trigger ovulation. The other agents (leuprolide, a GnRH analog; cetrorelix and ganirelix, GnRH antagonists) are all used to inhibit LH secretion.
Q322: A patient was recently placed on levothyroxine. Which of her medications may affect the levothyroxine dosage requirements?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Thyroid✅ B
A) Bromocriptine
B) Calcium carbonate
C) Metoprolol
D) Vitamin D
Explanation: Calcium carbonate may reduce the absorption of levothyroxine. The other medications should not interact with the levothyroxine.
Q323: Which is a common side effect that should be communicated to a patient prescribed octreotide?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Pituitary✅ D
A) Weight gain
B) Low blood sugar
C) Myalgia
D) Abdominal pain
Explanation: Common side effects of octreotide are gastrointestinal in nature and include diarrhea, abdominal pain, nausea, and steatorrhea.
Q324: Which symptom indicates that a patient may need a lower dosage of levothyroxine?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Thyroid✅ C
A) Bradycardia
B) Cold intolerance
C) Palpitations
D) Weight gain
Explanation: Palpitations are an adverse effect of too much thyroid supplementation. The other symptoms are indicative of untreated or undertreated hypothyroidism and may require an increase in thyroid supplementation.
Q325: The adrenocorticosteroid synthetic pathway is responsible for the synthesis and release of cortisol. Which of the following e...
🔬 Endocrine📂 Adrenal✅ C
A) Insulin release
B) Production of inflammation
C) Increased gluconeogenesis
D) Decreased protein breakdown
Explanation: See Figure 23.3. Cortisol has anti-inflammatory actions, increases gluconeogenesis, and increases protein breakdown.
Q1: A 45-year-old female with Type 1 Diabetes on a basal-bolus insulin regimen presents with recurrent post-prandial blood glucos...
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ B
A) Increase her insulin glargine dose.
B) Increase her insulin aspart dose.
C) Add metformin.
D) Add a sulfonylurea.
Explanation: Post-prandial hyperglycemia despite normal pre-meal glucose indicates the bolus (mealtime) insulin dose is insufficient to cover the carbohydrates consumed. Insulin glargine (basal) controls fasting and between-meal glucose, while insulin aspart (rapid-acting bolus) controls post-meal glucose spikes. Since pre-meal glucose is well-controlled at 120 mg/dL but post-meal readings exceed 250 mg/dL, the issue is clearly inadequate bolus coverage — the aspart dose should be increased or the insulin-to-carbohydrate ratio adjusted. Adding metformin or a sulfonylurea would be inappropriate in T1DM.
Q2: Which of the following is NOT one of the three characteristic features of Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA)?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ C
A) Hyperglycemia
B) Metabolic acidosis
C) Hypokalemia
D) Ketonuria
Explanation: The three hallmark features of DKA are hyperglycemia (usually >250 mg/dL), metabolic acidosis (low pH/bicarbonate), and ketonemia/ketonuria. Hypokalemia is NOT a defining feature — in fact, total body potassium is depleted, but serum potassium is usually normal or even elevated due to the acidosis-driven transcellular shift of K+ from the intracellular to the extracellular space. As treatment begins (insulin + fluids + correction of acidosis), potassium shifts back intracellularly, which is why close monitoring and supplementation are critical.
Q3: In the pathophysiology of DKA, what is the direct cause of hepatic ketogenesis?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ A
A) The tricarboxylic acid (TCA) cycle capacity is exceeded by acetyl-CoA.
B) Increased peripheral glucose utilization.
C) Suppression of lipolysis by counter-regulatory hormones.
D) Decreased hepatic gluconeogenesis.
Explanation: In DKA, insulin deficiency combined with glucagon excess accelerates lipolysis in adipose tissue, flooding the liver with free fatty acids. In the liver, fatty acids are oxidized to acetyl-CoA. Normally acetyl-CoA enters the TCA (Krebs) cycle, but two factors prevent this: (1) oxaloacetate is being diverted to gluconeogenesis, depleting TCA substrates, and (2) the sheer volume of acetyl-CoA produced exceeds TCA cycle capacity. The excess acetyl-CoA is then channeled into ketogenesis, producing acetoacetate and beta-hydroxybutyrate — the ketoacids responsible for the anion gap acidosis.
Q4: A patient is admitted with severe DKA. Their initial serum potassium (K) is 3.1 mEq/L. According to the treatment guidelines,...
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ C
A) Start an IV insulin infusion at 0.1 units/kg/hr.
B) Administer a rapid fluid bolus of 0.9% NaCl.
C) Hold insulin and administer 20–40 mEq/hour of potassium.
D) Administer sodium bicarbonate because the DKA is severe.
Explanation: When serum potassium is <3.3–3.5 mEq/L in DKA, insulin therapy must be HELD and potassium replaced first (typically 20–40 mEq/hour IV). Insulin drives potassium from the extracellular space back into cells, and if started when K+ is already low, it can cause life-threatening hypokalemia and fatal cardiac arrhythmias. Potassium replacement continues until K+ ≥ 3.3–3.5 mEq/L before insulin is started.
Q5: When treating DKA, at what serum glucose level should the IV fluids be changed to 5% dextrose with 0.45% NaCl?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ C
A) 400 mg/dL
B) 300 mg/dL
C) 200 mg/dL
D) 100 mg/dL
Explanation: As insulin treatment suppresses ketogenesis, the primary goal is to close the anion gap — not just to normalize blood glucose. Blood glucose typically normalizes before the acidosis resolves. When glucose falls to ~200–250 mg/dL, dextrose (5% or 10%) is added to IV fluids to prevent hypoglycemia while maintaining the insulin infusion at a sufficient rate to continue suppressing ketone production. Stopping insulin too early risks rebound ketoacidosis.
Q6: Sodium bicarbonate administration is indicated in DKA management only when the arterial pH is:
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ C
A) >7.0
B) 6.9–7.0
C) <6.9
D) <7.25
Explanation: Sodium bicarbonate is NOT routinely used in DKA because: (1) it may worsen paradoxical CNS acidosis (CO2 crosses the blood-brain barrier faster than bicarbonate), (2) it promotes hypokalemia (bicarbonate drives K+ intracellularly), (3) it can overshoot to metabolic alkalosis, and (4) the acidosis corrects naturally once ketogenesis is suppressed by insulin. Bicarbonate is only considered when pH <6.9 due to risk of severe hemodynamic instability and cardiac dysrhythmias.
Q7: What is the key pathophysiological difference that explains the absence of significant ketoacidosis in Hyperosmolar Hyperglyc...
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ B
A) Patients with HHS have an absolute insulin deficiency.
B) There is enough insulin in HHS to suppress lipolysis and ketogenesis.
C) Counter-regulatory hormones are not elevated in HHS.
D) Patients with HHS have lower blood glucose levels than in DKA.
Explanation: In Type 2 DM (the setting of HHS), there is residual beta-cell function and some endogenous insulin secretion. While this is insufficient to prevent extreme hyperglycemia (due to insulin resistance + stress hormones), it IS sufficient to suppress adipose tissue lipolysis. Without significant lipolysis, there is no excess free fatty acid supply to the liver, and therefore no substrate for ketogenesis. This is why HHS presents with severe hyperglycemia and hyperosmolality but WITHOUT the significant ketoacidosis seen in DKA.
Q8: What is the recommended initial IV fluid for a patient with HHS who is not in cardiogenic shock?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ C
A) 0.45% sodium chloride
B) 5% dextrose in water (D5W)
C) 0.9% sodium chloride
D) Lactated Ringer's solution
Explanation: 0.9% NaCl (isotonic normal saline) at 1 L/hour is the standard initial fluid for HHS. The immediate priority is volume restoration to reverse hemodynamic instability and restore renal perfusion. Once the patient is stabilized, the corrected sodium is calculated. If corrected Na is high (or normal in a severely dehydrated patient), fluids are switched to 0.45% NaCl to provide free water and gradually correct hypertonicity.
Q9: What is the primary hormone responsible for acute recovery from insulin-induced hypoglycemia?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ D
A) Epinephrine
B) Cortisol
C) Insulin
D) Glucagon
Explanation: The initial fluid of choice for HHS is 0.9% isotonic sodium chloride (normal saline), regardless of the serum sodium level. The priority is rapid restoration of intravascular volume and perfusion pressure. Once hemodynamic stability is achieved and adequate urine output is established, the fluid can be transitioned to 0.45% NaCl to correct the free water deficit and gradually lower serum osmolality at a safe rate (3–8 mOsm/kg/hr).
Q10: A conscious and alert patient with diabetes checks their blood sugar and it is 65 mg/dL. According to the 'Rule of 15', what ...
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ C
A) Inject 1 mg of glucagon.
B) Eat a full meal containing protein and fat.
C) Ingest 15-20 grams of a rapidly absorbed carbohydrate.
D) Recheck their blood glucose in 1 hour.
Explanation: Glucagon is the primary counter-regulatory hormone for acute recovery from hypoglycemia. It is secreted from pancreatic α-cells in response to falling blood glucose and acts on the liver to stimulate glycogenolysis (breakdown of glycogen to glucose) and gluconeogenesis (new glucose production). Epinephrine is a secondary counter-regulatory hormone that provides the adrenergic warning symptoms (tremor, palpitations, sweating) and also promotes glycogenolysis, but glucagon is the first and most potent hepatic glucose-elevating hormone. Cortisol acts over hours to days.
Q11: What is the correct maintenance dose of IV regular insulin for an 80 kg patient with DKA?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ B
A) 0.8 units/hour
B) 8 units/hour
C) 16 units/hour
D) 80 units/hour
Explanation: The standard fixed-rate IV insulin infusion for DKA is 0.1 units/kg/hour. For an 80 kg patient: 80 × 0.1 = 8 units/hour. Alternatively, some protocols use 0.14 units/kg/hour without an initial bolus. The 2024 consensus favors fixed-rate infusions over bolus-based protocols. For pure HHS, a lower rate of 0.05 units/kg/hour is used to avoid too rapid a decline in osmolality.
Q12: Which medication class is listed as a potential cause of DKA?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ C
A) Metformin
B) Sulfonylureas
C) SGLT2 inhibitors
D) DPP-4 inhibitors
Explanation: SGLT2 inhibitors (e.g., dapagliflozin, empagliflozin, canagliflozin) are associated with euglycemic DKA — a form of DKA where blood glucose is near normal (<250 mg/dL). The mechanism involves: increased glucagon secretion, decreased insulin secretion, promotion of lipolysis and ketogenesis, while urinary glucose excretion masks the expected hyperglycemia. Stress, surgery, prolonged fasting, and reduced carbohydrate intake are common triggers in patients on SGLT2 inhibitors.
Q13: When treating DKA, how long should the IV insulin infusion be continued?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ B
A) Until the blood glucose normalizes to <120 mg/dL.
B) Until the urine ketones clear.
C) For a maximum of 12 hours.
D) Until the patient is able to eat and drink.
Explanation: The IV insulin infusion in DKA should be continued until the metabolic derangement is fully resolved — not just until glucose normalizes. Resolution criteria (2024 consensus) require glucose <200 mg/dL PLUS at least two of: pH >7.3, bicarbonate ≥15–18 mEq/L, and BHB <0.6 mmol/L. The infusion must overlap with the first dose of subcutaneous basal insulin by 1–2 hours before discontinuation to prevent rebound ketogenesis.
Q14: A patient with DKA has a serum potassium of 6.0 mEq/L. What is the appropriate management for their potassium?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ C
A) Administer 40 mEq of potassium IV immediately.
B) Administer insulin and glucose to lower the potassium.
C) Do not give potassium and check the serum level every 2 hours.
D) Add 20 mEq of potassium to each liter of IV fluid.
Explanation: When serum K is >5.5 mEq/L in DKA, potassium should be withheld from IV fluids. As insulin therapy drives K+ intracellularly and urinary losses continue, potassium will fall. Monitoring every 1–2 hours is essential. Once K falls to 4–5 mEq/L, potassium supplementation (20–40 mEq/L of IV fluid) is added to prevent hypokalemia. The goal is to maintain K between 4 and 5 mEq/L throughout treatment.
Q15: Kussmaul respiration, a sign of DKA, is best described as:
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ C
A) Slow and shallow breathing.
B) Rapid and shallow breathing.
C) Deep and rapid breathing.
D) Periods of apnea followed by rapid breathing.
Explanation: Kussmaul breathing is a deep, labored, regular respiratory pattern that is a compensatory response to metabolic acidosis. The body increases respiratory rate and tidal volume to exhale CO2, which shifts the carbonic acid equilibrium to raise blood pH (respiratory compensation for metabolic acidosis). It is a classic clinical sign of DKA and can produce the characteristic 'fruity breath' odor from expired acetone.
Q16: In the management of HHS, when should insulin therapy be initiated?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ B
A) Immediately upon diagnosis, before fluids are started.
B) Only if significant ketonemia is present.
C) When the blood glucose falls below 300 mg/dL.
D) Insulin is never used in the treatment of HHS.
Explanation: In HHS, insulin is NOT the first priority — aggressive fluid resuscitation is. The initial profound volume depletion must be corrected first because insulin lowers glucose rapidly and, without adequate fluid replacement, can cause dangerous osmotic shifts. Per the 2024 consensus, a low-dose insulin infusion (0.05 units/kg/hour) is initiated AFTER the initial fluid bolus and only if glucose is not declining adequately (by at least 50–70 mg/dL/hour) with fluids alone.
Q17: A patient is unconscious due to severe hypoglycemia. Which treatment is most appropriate if IV access is not available?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ D
A) Administer 50 mL of 50% dextrose intramuscularly.
B) Force the patient to drink orange juice.
C) Place glucose tablets under the patient's tongue.
D) Administer 1 mg of glucagon subcutaneously or intramuscularly.
Explanation: In pure HHS, adequate fluid resuscitation alone is often sufficient to significantly lower blood glucose, as renal perfusion is restored and osmotic diuresis resolves. Insulin is typically introduced only once adequate volume has been replaced AND if significant ketonemia is present (suggesting an overlap with DKA). Starting insulin prematurely — before volume is restored — risks osmotic fluid shifts, worsening hypokalemia, and cerebral edema.
Q18: Which of the following symptoms of hypoglycemia is classified as neuroglycopenic?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ C
A) Sweating
B) Palpitations
C) Confusion
D) Tremor
Explanation: When IV access is unavailable in an unconscious hypoglycemic patient, 1 mg of glucagon given subcutaneously (SC) or intramuscularly (IM) is the first-line treatment. Glucagon stimulates hepatic glycogenolysis, rapidly raising blood glucose within 10–15 minutes. 50% dextrose is given IV — it cannot be given IM. Forcing liquids into an unconscious patient risks aspiration and is dangerous. Placing glucose tablets under the tongue is ineffective and also risks airway obstruction. Nasal glucagon (Baqsimi) is also available as an alternative to the injectable form.
Q19: What is 'hypoglycemic unawareness'?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ B
A) When a patient deliberately ignores the symptoms of low blood sugar.
B) When the autonomic warning symptoms of hypoglycemia are absent or diminished.
C) When a patient's blood glucose monitor gives falsely normal readings.
D) When a patient experiences hyperglycemia immediately after a low blood sugar episode.
Explanation: Hypoglycemia symptoms are classified as either adrenergic/autonomic or neuroglycopenic. Adrenergic symptoms (tremor, sweating, palpitations, anxiety) result from catecholamine release triggered by a falling glucose. Neuroglycopenic symptoms (confusion, cognitive impairment, seizures, loss of consciousness) result directly from insufficient glucose supply to the brain. Confusion is the classic neuroglycopenic symptom. Sweating, palpitations, and tremors are adrenergic/autonomic symptoms.
Q20: Which of the following is an example of a food source that provides approximately 15g of carbohydrates for treating hypoglyce...
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ A
A) 1 cup of fat-free milk
B) 1/2 cup of grape juice
C) 1 tablespoon of peanut butter
D) 10 glucose tablets
Explanation: Hypoglycemic unawareness is the loss of the autonomic warning symptoms (sweating, tremor, palpitations) that normally alert a patient to a falling blood glucose. It typically develops after recurrent hypoglycemic episodes, which blunt the counter-regulatory hormone response and diminish adrenergic symptom thresholds. Patients with hypoglycemic unawareness may reach dangerously low glucose levels without any warning, significantly increasing the risk of severe hypoglycemia and unconsciousness. Strict avoidance of hypoglycemia for several weeks can partially restore awareness.
Q21: When transitioning a patient from an IV insulin infusion to subcutaneous (SC) insulin, how long before discontinuing the IV i...
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ B
A) The IV infusion should be stopped at the exact same time the SC dose is given.
B) 1-2 hours
C) 4-6 hours
D) 30 minutes
Explanation: IV regular insulin has an extremely short half-life (~6 minutes). If the IV drip is abruptly discontinued without subcutaneous coverage, insulin levels drop precipitously and ketogenesis can resume — leading to rebound ketoacidosis. To prevent this, the first dose of SC basal insulin (e.g., glargine) is given 1–2 hours BEFORE stopping the IV drip, allowing the SC insulin to reach therapeutic levels before the IV insulin clears.
Q22: Phosphate replacement in DKA is generally reserved for patients with a serum phosphate level of:
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ C
A) <3.0 mg/dL
B) <2.5 mg/dL
C) <1.0 mg/dL
D) Phosphate should always be replaced in DKA.
Explanation: Phosphate replacement in DKA is selective, not routine. It is reserved for patients with severe hypophosphatemia (serum phosphate <1 mg/dL) or those at high risk (hemolytic anemia, respiratory depression, cardiac dysfunction). Routine phosphate replacement has not been shown to improve outcomes and can cause hypocalcemia and hypomagnesemia.
Q23: In the initial fluid resuscitation of a 70 kg patient with DKA and a blood pressure of 110/70 mmHg, what is the recommended i...
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ C
A) 150-250 mL/hour
B) 500 mL over 10-15 minutes
C) 15-20 mL/kg/hour
D) 4-14 mL/kg/hour
Explanation: Initial resuscitation in DKA uses 0.9% NaCl at approximately 500 mL/hour to 1 L/hour (15 mL/kg/hour). For a 70 kg patient this is approximately 1,050 mL/hour. The priority is rapid volume repletion to restore renal perfusion (which improves glucosuria and helps lower blood glucose even before insulin takes effect) and hemodynamic stability.
Q24: A potential complication of DKA is thrombosis. What preventative measure is mentioned in the presentation?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ C
A) Administering prophylactic aspirin.
B) Starting a warfarin regimen.
C) Giving prophylactic Low Molecular Weight Heparin (LMWH).
D) Ensuring early ambulation.
Explanation: DKA creates a hypercoagulable state due to severe dehydration, hyperviscosity from high glucose, platelet activation, and elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines. DVT and thromboembolism are recognized DKA complications, particularly in severe cases. Prophylactic anticoagulation (subcutaneous heparin or LMWH) is recommended, especially in patients who are unconscious, immobile, or have other VTE risk factors.
Q25: Which of the following is a primary goal of fluid therapy in HHS?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ B
A) To rapidly lower blood glucose.
B) To normalize serum osmolality and restore intravascular volume.
C) To correct the metabolic acidosis.
D) To clear ketones from the urine.
Explanation: The profound volume depletion in HHS (typically 9–12 liters, much greater than in DKA) is the primary problem. Restoring intravascular volume with IV fluids: (1) improves tissue perfusion, (2) restores renal function and promotes osmotic diuresis of glucose, and (3) lowers blood glucose even before insulin is administered. Fluid therapy is the cornerstone of HHS management.
Q26: Why might glucagon be less effective in treating hypoglycemia in a patient with chronic alcoholism or prolonged fasting?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ B
A) These conditions cause resistance to glucagon's effects.
B) The patient's liver glycogen stores may be depleted.
C) Alcohol directly inactivates the glucagon molecule.
D) These patients have an impaired epinephrine response.
Explanation: Unlike DKA, where the primary treatment target is clearing the metabolic acidosis/ketones with insulin, the management of HHS centers on fluid therapy. The primary goals are: (1) restoring intravascular volume and hemodynamic stability, (2) correcting the extreme hyperosmolality and hypernatremia, and (3) restoring renal perfusion to allow glucose excretion. Since there is little ketosis in pure HHS, fluid therapy (not insulin) is the cornerstone of treatment.
Q27: A patient is being treated for HHS. The target rate for osmolality reduction is:
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ B
A) 1–2 mOsm/kg/hr
B) 3–8 mOsm/kg/hr
C) 10–15 mOsm/kg/hr
D) Osmolality should be corrected as fast as possible.
Explanation: The target rate of osmolality reduction in HHS is 3–8 mOsm/kg/hour. A gradual decline over 48–72 hours is critical. Overly rapid reduction causes water to shift into brain cells (which have adapted to the hyperosmolar state), resulting in cerebral edema, neurological deterioration, and potentially fatal herniation. The gradual approach also applies to the rate of sodium correction: no more than 10 mEq/L per 24 hours.
Q28: Which of the following oral diabetes medications can cause hypoglycemia?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ C
A) SGLT2 inhibitors
B) Metformin
C) Sulfonylureas
D) Acarbose
Explanation: Serum osmolality in HHS must be reduced gradually at a target rate of 3–8 mOsm/kg/hour. Rapid correction creates a large osmotic gradient between the blood and brain cells, causing water to shift rapidly into the brain. This osmotic fluid shift can cause potentially fatal cerebral edema, particularly in younger patients. The gradual correction over 24–48 hours allows the brain to adapt safely.
Q29: What is the recommended dose of glucagon for an unconscious adult with hypoglycemia?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ C
A) 0.1 mg intramuscularly
B) 0.5 mg intravenously
C) 1 mg subcutaneously or intramuscularly
D) 3 mg subcutaneously
Explanation: Sulfonylureas (e.g., glipizide, glibenclamide/glyburide, glimepiride) stimulate insulin secretion from pancreatic β-cells independently of blood glucose levels. This non-glucose-dependent insulin release can cause hypoglycemia, especially if a meal is delayed or skipped. SGLT2 inhibitors and metformin work through glucose-dependent or non-insulin mechanisms and have very low intrinsic hypoglycemia risk when used as monotherapy. Acarbose delays carbohydrate absorption and does not stimulate insulin secretion, making hypoglycemia highly unlikely with monotherapy.
Q30: After administering glucagon to an unconscious patient, they should be positioned on their side because:
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ C
A) It improves glucagon absorption.
B) It prevents the patient from rolling onto their back.
C) Glucagon may induce vomiting, and this prevents aspiration.
D) It helps the liver release glucose more quickly.
Explanation: The standard adult dose of glucagon for treating severe hypoglycemia with loss of consciousness is 1 mg (1,000 mcg) given subcutaneously or intramuscularly. This dose typically produces a rise in blood glucose within 10–15 minutes. The nasal powder formulation (Baqsimi) also delivers 3 mg intranasally but is a different preparation. Once the patient regains consciousness, oral carbohydrates should be given to replete glycogen stores. If there is no response within 10–15 minutes, a second dose may be given while seeking emergency care.
Q31: A Level 2 (clinically significant) hypoglycemia is defined as a blood glucose value of:
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ B
A) ≤70 mg/dL
B) <54 mg/dL
C) <40 mg/dL
D) Any value associated with cognitive impairment.
Explanation: Glucagon commonly causes nausea and vomiting as a side effect — particularly as the patient regains consciousness. An unconscious or semi-conscious patient cannot protect their own airway. Placing the patient in the recovery position (lateral decubitus — on their side) allows any vomitus to drain out of the mouth rather than being aspirated into the lungs, preventing aspiration pneumonia. This is a critical safety step that should always accompany glucagon administration in an unconscious patient.
Q32: In DKA, a WBC count of 20,000 cells/mL:
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ B
A) Is a definitive sign of a severe bacterial infection.
B) Can occur even without evidence of infection.
C) Is a sign of cerebral edema.
D) Is an expected finding in mild DKA only.
Explanation: Leukocytosis (WBC 10,000–25,000 cells/mL) is a common, expected finding in DKA and does not automatically indicate bacterial infection. It is caused by the physiological stress response — elevated cortisol and catecholamines mobilize neutrophils from the marginal pool. A WBC >25,000 cells/mL or significant bandemia (left shift) should prompt investigation for an underlying infectious precipitant. All DKA patients should be evaluated for infection as a precipitating cause.
Q33: The mortality rate for a single episode of DKA is approximately:
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ B
A) <1%
B) 5%
C) 10%
D) 25%
Explanation: With modern management, the mortality rate for a single DKA episode is approximately 1–5%. Death is more common in the elderly and in those with severe precipitating illness (MI, sepsis, stroke). The main causes of death are the precipitating illness, cerebral edema (especially in children), and serious electrolyte disturbances. By contrast, HHS carries a higher mortality of 10–20% due to the typically older, sicker patient population.
Q34: Which of the following would be an appropriate fluid choice for a DKA patient after initial resuscitation if their corrected ...
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ B
A) Continue 0.9% NaCl
B) Switch to 0.45% NaCl
C) Switch to 5% Dextrose with 0.9% NaCl
D) Switch to Lactated Ringer's
Explanation: After initial resuscitation with 0.9% NaCl, fluid selection is guided by corrected sodium. If corrected sodium is elevated (hypernatremia) or normal in the context of ongoing hyperglycemia, 0.45% NaCl (half-normal saline) is used to provide free water and correct the hyperosmolar state. If corrected sodium is low, 0.9% NaCl is continued. The formula for corrected sodium: Na+ + 1.6 × [(glucose – 100)/100].
Q35: If a DKA patient's blood glucose does not fall by 50-75 mg/dL after the first hour of insulin infusion, the next step is to:
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ B
A) Switch to subcutaneous insulin.
B) Administer a second IV bolus of 0.15 units/kg.
C) Stop the insulin infusion and focus on fluids.
D) Decrease the insulin infusion rate by half.
Explanation: If blood glucose fails to decrease by 50–75 mg/dL in the first hour of insulin infusion, the insulin dose should be doubled and fluid status reassessed. Inadequate response may indicate insufficient insulin dosing, inadequate rehydration (dehydration impairs insulin distribution and glucose clearance), or occasionally insulin resistance. Do not add a bolus dose in isolation; ensure fluids are running adequately.
Q36: Which of the following is considered a precipitating factor for both DKA and HHS?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ B
A) Poor compliance with insulin therapy
B) Infection
C) Initial presentation of Type 1 Diabetes
D) Alcohol ingestion
Explanation: Infection (pneumonia, UTI, and sepsis) is the most common precipitating factor for both DKA and HHS, accounting for 30–40% of cases. Infection triggers a catecholamine and cortisol surge that worsens insulin resistance and promotes counter-regulatory hormone release, driving hyperglycemia and (in DKA) ketogenesis. Other precipitants include myocardial infarction, stroke, pancreatitis, medication non-adherence, and new-onset diabetes.
Q37: Comparing DKA and HHS, which condition typically has a greater degree of volume depletion?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ B
A) DKA
B) HHS
C) They have an equal degree of volume depletion.
D) Neither condition involves significant volume depletion.
Explanation: HHS typically involves a much greater degree of volume depletion than DKA — approximately 9–12 liters in HHS vs. 3–6 liters in DKA. This is because HHS develops more insidiously over days to weeks (patients are often elderly and may not seek care early), with sustained osmotic diuresis from extreme hyperglycemia (often >600 mg/dL) leading to massive water and electrolyte losses. The higher osmolality also impairs thirst mechanisms.
Q38: What is the correct dosing for SC rapid-acting insulin in a patient with mild, uncomplicated DKA?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ B
A) An initial dose of 0.1 units/kg, repeated every 4 hours.
B) An initial bolus of 0.2 units/kg followed by 0.1 units/kg every hour.
C) A fixed dose of 10 units every 2 hours.
D) SC insulin is never appropriate for treating DKA.
Explanation: Mild, uncomplicated DKA (pH >7.3, bicarbonate >15, alert patient who can tolerate oral fluids) can be managed with SC rapid-acting insulin as an alternative to IV infusion. The standard protocol uses SC insulin aspart or lispro at an initial dose of 0.2–0.3 units/kg, followed by 0.1–0.2 units/kg every 1–2 hours until resolution. This approach is as effective as IV insulin for mild DKA and may reduce ICU admissions.
Q39: Which of the following describes Level 3 (severe) hypoglycemia?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ B
A) A blood glucose of <54 mg/dL without symptoms.
B) An episode associated with cognitive impairment requiring external assistance.
C) The first time a patient's blood glucose drops below 70 mg/dL.
D) Any hypoglycemic episode that occurs at night.
Explanation: For mild, uncomplicated DKA in cooperative patients who can tolerate oral fluids, subcutaneous rapid-acting insulin is an acceptable alternative to IV insulin infusion. The protocol uses an initial bolus of 0.2 units/kg, followed by 0.1 units/kg every hour until resolution criteria are met. This approach can be used in less acute settings and allows for earlier discharge planning, though IV insulin remains the standard for moderate-to-severe DKA.
Q40: The brain's exclusive fuel for metabolism is:
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ C
A) Ketones
B) Fatty acids
C) Glucose
D) Amino acids
Explanation: Level 3 (severe) hypoglycemia is defined by the ADA as any hypoglycemic episode — regardless of the actual blood glucose value — that is associated with severe cognitive impairment and requires external assistance from another person. The defining characteristic is functional impairment requiring help (cannot self-treat), not a specific glucose number. This distinguishes Level 3 from Level 2 (<54 mg/dL) which is defined numerically. Examples include seizures, loss of consciousness, or profound confusion requiring another person to administer glucagon or call emergency services.
Q41: When treating severe hypoglycemia in a hospital setting with IV access, what is the standard treatment?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ C
A) A continuous infusion of 10% dextrose.
B) 1 mg of glucagon IV.
C) 20–50 mL of 50% dextrose.
D) An IV bolus of regular insulin.
Explanation: The brain is almost exclusively dependent on glucose as its metabolic fuel under normal physiological conditions. Unlike most other tissues (which can use fatty acids and ketones), the brain has a very limited ability to metabolize fatty acids due to the blood-brain barrier. During prolonged starvation, the brain can adapt to use ketone bodies, but under normal circumstances glucose is the primary and preferred substrate. This is why even brief episodes of hypoglycemia rapidly impair brain function, causing neuroglycopenic symptoms such as confusion and loss of consciousness.
Q42: What is a common precipitating factor for HHS in elderly patients?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ B
A) Excessive physical exercise.
B) Impaired thirst or inability to access fluids.
C) Starting a new diet.
D) Poor compliance with oral hypoglycemic agents.
Explanation: Infection is the most common precipitating factor for HHS in elderly patients, particularly pneumonia and urinary tract infections. Elderly patients often have diminished thirst perception, reduced renal concentrating ability, and may be on medications (diuretics, corticosteroids) that worsen hyperglycemia. They may not present with typical symptoms of hyperglycemia, and instead present with altered mental status, which is a hallmark of HHS.
Q43: A patient is treated for DKA. Their initial K was 5.8, and after 4 hours of insulin and fluids, it is now 4.5 mEq/L. What is ...
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ B
A) Continue to hold all potassium.
B) Give 20–30 mEq of potassium in each liter of IV fluid.
C) Give a bolus of 40 ..mEq of potassium over 1 hour.
D) Stop the insulin infusion to let potassium rise.
Explanation: When serum potassium is 4–5 mEq/L during DKA treatment, potassium chloride (20–40 mEq/L of IV fluid) should be added to prevent hypokalemia. As insulin drives K+ intracellularly and ongoing urinary losses continue, K+ will continue to fall. The therapeutic target is to maintain serum potassium between 4 and 5 mEq/L throughout treatment. Monitoring every 2–4 hours is standard.
Q44: Which symptom is common in DKA but uncommon in HHS?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ C
A) Polyuria
B) Altered mental status
C) Abdominal pain
D) Dehydration
Explanation: Abdominal pain (and nausea/vomiting) is common in DKA — occurring in up to 50% of cases — due to metabolic acidosis, electrolyte disturbances (especially hypokalemia), and gastric dysmotility. These gastrointestinal symptoms are uncommon in HHS because significant acidosis is absent. If abdominal pain is the presenting complaint in a known diabetic, DKA must always be considered.
Q45: After treating a hypoglycemic episode with 15g of carbohydrates, how soon should the blood glucose be rechecked?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ B
A) 5 minutes
B) 15 minutes
C) 30 minutes
D) 60 minutes
Explanation: Abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting are characteristic symptoms of DKA, occurring in up to 50–75% of cases. The exact cause is multifactorial — involving metabolic acidosis, gastric stasis, ileus, and possibly elevated prostaglandins. These symptoms are uncommon in HHS because there is no metabolic acidosis to trigger the GI response. The presence of abdominal pain in HHS should prompt evaluation for an underlying surgical or medical precipitant (e.g., mesenteric ischemia).
Q46: Which of these is NOT listed as a potential complication of DKA?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ C
A) Cerebral edema
B) Hypoglycemia
C) Acute pancreatitis
D) Thrombosis
Explanation: Recognized DKA complications include: cerebral edema (most feared, especially in children), hypokalemia, hypoglycemia (iatrogenic from over-treatment), thromboembolism, pulmonary edema, and acute kidney injury. While elevated amylase/lipase are commonly seen in DKA (from salivary glands and gut ischemia), frank acute pancreatitis is not listed as a classic DKA complication — it is more commonly a precipitating cause of DKA than a result of it.
Q47: When treating HHS, the rate of fall of serum sodium should not exceed what value in 24 hours?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ B
A) 5 mmol/L
B) 10 mmol/L
C) 15 mmol/L
D) 20 mmol/L
Explanation: In HHS, sodium correction should not fall more than 10 mEq/L per 24 hours. Overly rapid correction of hypernatremia or serum osmolality can cause water to move into brain cells (which have adapted osmotically to the hyperosmolar state with idiogenic osmoles), resulting in cerebral edema and neurological deterioration — a potentially fatal complication. Gradual correction over 48–72 hours is recommended.
Q48: The definitive treatment for an unconscious hypoglycemic patient in a hospital is 20-50 mL of 50% dextrose IV. This provides ...
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ C
A) 1-2 grams
B) 5-10 grams
C) 10-25 grams
D) 50-100 grams
Explanation: When treating HHS, the serum sodium should not fall faster than 10 mmol/L per 24 hours. This safe rate of correction mirrors the guidelines for treating hypernatremia of any cause. Overly rapid correction creates an osmotic gradient favoring water influx into brain cells (which have adapted to the hyperosmolar state), potentially causing cerebral edema — a catastrophic and potentially fatal complication.
Q49: An arterial pH of 7.28 and a bicarbonate of 16 mEq/L would classify a patient's DKA as:
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ A
A) Mild
B) Moderate
C) Severe
D) Resolved
Explanation: DKA severity classification (traditional): Mild: pH 7.25–7.30, Bicarbonate 15–18 mEq/L. Moderate: pH 7.0–7.24, Bicarbonate 10–<15. Severe: pH <7.0, Bicarbonate <10. A pH of 7.28 (within 7.25–7.30) and bicarbonate of 16 mEq/L (within 15–18) places this patient in the Mild category.
Q50: What is the initial bolus dose of IV regular insulin for a 100 kg patient with DKA?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ C
A) 1 unit
B) 5 units
C) 10 units
D) 100 units
Explanation: The initial IV insulin bolus in DKA is 0.1 units/kg. For a 100 kg patient: 100 × 0.1 = 10 units. This is followed by a maintenance infusion of 0.1 units/kg/hour. Note: some 2024 consensus protocols omit the initial bolus and start directly at 0.14 units/kg/hour, as clinical trials have not shown a clear benefit to the bolus in adults. The bolus remains more common in pediatric protocols.
Q51: According to the presentation, what is the A1C threshold for diagnosing prediabetes?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ B
A) ≥6.5%
B) 5.7%–6.4%
C) 100–125 mg/dL
D) <5.7%
Explanation: The standard initial IV insulin bolus for DKA is 0.1 units/kg (some protocols use 0.15 units/kg). For a 100 kg patient using the 0.1 units/kg protocol: 0.1 × 100 = 10 units IV regular insulin. This bolus rapidly achieves therapeutic insulin levels before the maintenance infusion (0.1 units/kg/hr = 10 units/hr) takes effect. Note: Some 2024 consensus guidelines have moved away from the routine bolus and recommend starting directly with the maintenance infusion at 0.1–0.14 units/kg/hr.
Q52: What is the primary characteristic of Stage 1 Type 1 Diabetes Mellitus (T1DM) as defined by the ADA?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ B
A) Symptomatic with glucose concentrations consistent with diabetes.
B) Multiple autoantibodies present with normal glucose concentrations.
C) Multiple autoantibodies present and glucose levels in the prediabetes range.
D) Complete absence of autoantibodies with symptomatic hyperglycemia.
Explanation: The ADA diagnostic criteria classify glycemia into three categories based on A1C: Normal = A1C <5.7%; Prediabetes = A1C 5.7%–6.4%; Diabetes = A1C ≥6.5%. An A1C ≥6.5% on two separate occasions (or with confirmatory testing) diagnoses diabetes. The range 5.7%–6.4% identifies patients at high risk of progressing to T2DM — this is prediabetes. Option A (≥6.5%) is the diabetes threshold. Option C (100–125 mg/dL) refers to impaired fasting glucose (also prediabetes, but by FPG criteria).
Q53: Which of the following insulin preparations is considered intermediate-acting?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ D
A) Insulin Glargine
B) Insulin Lispro
C) Regular Insulin
D) NPH Insulin
Explanation: The ADA staging of T1DM recognizes that autoimmune destruction begins years before clinical hyperglycemia: Stage 1 = multiple islet autoantibodies present (≥2), normoglycemia, no symptoms. Stage 2 = multiple autoantibodies + impaired glucose tolerance (prediabetes range). Stage 3 = clinical hyperglycemia meeting diabetes diagnostic criteria (symptomatic). Stage 1 is the earliest identifiable stage — the immune attack has begun but enough β-cell function remains to maintain normal glucose. Screening for autoantibodies (anti-GAD, anti-IA2, anti-ZnT8) can identify Stage 1.
Q54: A patient with Type 1 Diabetes is using a basal-bolus regimen. If their Total Daily Insulin (TDI) is 50 units, what is the es...
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ C
A) 50 units
B) 33 units
C) 25 units
D) 17 units
Explanation: Insulin preparations are classified by their onset, peak, and duration: Rapid-acting analogs (lispro, aspart, glulisine) = onset 5–15 min, peak 1–2 hr, duration 3–5 hr. Short-acting (Regular U-100) = onset 30–60 min, peak 2–4 hr, duration 6–8 hr. Intermediate-acting (NPH) = onset 1–4 hr, peak 4–12 hr, duration 12–18 hr. Long-acting (glargine, detemir, degludec) = onset 1–4 hr, relatively peakless, duration 20–42+ hr. NPH (Neutral Protamine Hagedorn) is the only commercially available intermediate-acting insulin. Insulin glargine is long-acting, not intermediate.
Q55: Which complication is a significant risk with inhaled insulin, leading to its contraindication in patients with asthma or COP...
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ C
A) Severe hypoglycemia
B) Weight gain
C) Acute bronchospasm
D) Lipodystrophy
Explanation: In a standard basal-bolus regimen, the total daily insulin (TDI) is typically divided 50% for basal and 50% for bolus (prandial). Therefore, the once-daily basal insulin dose = 50% × 50 units = 25 units. The remaining 25 units are distributed as bolus doses before each meal (e.g., ~8 units TID). This 50/50 split is a starting estimate; it is individualized based on glucose patterns. Some guidelines suggest starting basal at 10 units or 0.1–0.2 units/kg and titrating up.
Q56: Pramlintide, a synthetic amylin analog, should be avoided in patients with which of the following conditions?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ B
A) Hypertension
B) Diabetic gastroparesis
C) Retinopathy
D) Renal insufficiency
Explanation: Inhaled insulin (Afrezza) is a rapid-acting insulin powder inhaled via a small inhaler. Its most significant safety concern is acute bronchospasm, which can be severe in patients with underlying pulmonary disease. Inhaled insulin is absolutely contraindicated in patients with asthma or COPD due to the high risk of serious bronchoconstriction. Pulmonary function testing (spirometry) is required before initiating therapy and periodically thereafter. It is also contraindicated in active smokers. Hypoglycemia is less of a concern compared to injectable insulin.
Q57: During which trimester of pregnancy do insulin requirements typically decrease due to fetal glucose uptake, increasing the ri...
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ A
A) First trimester
B) Second trimester
C) Third trimester
D) Insulin needs remain stable throughout pregnancy.
Explanation: Pramlintide (Symlin) is a synthetic amylin analog that works by slowing gastric emptying, suppressing post-meal glucagon, and promoting satiety. Because it delays gastric emptying, pramlintide is absolutely contraindicated in diabetic gastroparesis — a condition defined by already significantly delayed gastric emptying. Administering a drug that further slows gastric emptying in a patient with gastroparesis would worsen nausea, vomiting, unpredictable nutrient absorption, and glycemic control. Hypertension, retinopathy, and mild renal insufficiency are not contraindications.
Q58: What is the ‘Rule of 500’ used to calculate?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ D
A) The total daily insulin (TDI) requirement.
B) The correction factor (CF) for high blood glucose.
C) The number of U-500 regular insulin units needed.
D) The insulin-to-carbohydrate ratio (ICR).
Explanation: During the first trimester, insulin requirements typically decrease (or at minimum remain stable) due to fetal glucose consumption and the insulin-sensitizing effect of early pregnancy hormones. Morning sickness and nausea also reduce food intake, contributing to hypoglycemia risk. By contrast, in the second and especially third trimesters, placental hormones (human placental lactogen, progesterone, cortisol) progressively increase insulin resistance — often doubling or tripling pre-pregnancy insulin requirements. Clinicians must counsel patients about increased hypoglycemia vigilance in early pregnancy.
Q59: Which of the following is an advantage of using premixed insulin combinations like 70% NPH/30% Regular?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ B
A) Allows for precise adjustment of individual insulin components.
B) Decreases the number of daily injections.
C) Closely mimics the natural physiologic insulin secretion pattern.
D) Lowers the risk of hypoglycemia compared to basal-bolus therapy.
Explanation: Premixed insulins (e.g., 70/30 NPH/Regular, 75/25 lispro protamine/lispro) combine a fixed proportion of intermediate-acting and short/rapid-acting insulins, reducing the number of injections required — typically given twice daily (before breakfast and dinner) versus 4+ injections in a full basal-bolus regimen. The tradeoff is inflexibility: the fixed ratio cannot be individually adjusted for varying meals, activity levels, or correction doses. This makes premixed insulins less adaptable than basal-bolus regimens but more convenient for patients preferring fewer injections.
Q60: A patient with Type 1 Diabetes experiences a temporary reduction in insulin needs shortly after diagnosis and initiation of t...
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ C
A) The Somogyi effect
B) The dawn phenomenon
C) The honeymoon period
D) Latent autoimmune diabetes of adulthood (LADA)
Explanation: The 'honeymoon period' (also called the remission phase) occurs shortly after diagnosis of T1DM, typically within weeks to months after initiating insulin therapy. As ketotoxicity and glucotoxicity are reversed with treatment, the remaining functional β-cells recover temporarily and resume some endogenous insulin secretion. This can dramatically reduce exogenous insulin requirements — sometimes to near-zero — creating the illusion of remission. However, the autoimmune destruction of β-cells continues, and insulin requirements inevitably rise again as the remaining cells are destroyed.
Q61: Why is the measurement of C-peptide a better index of endogenous insulin production than measuring insulin levels directly?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ B
A) C-peptide has a much longer half-life than insulin.
B) Insulin undergoes significant hepatic and renal extraction, while C-peptide does not.
C) Exogenous insulin injections interfere with C-peptide measurement.
D) C-peptide is a more potent glucose-lowering hormone than insulin.
Explanation: When exogenous insulin is injected, measuring plasma insulin directly is confounded because exogenous insulin is indistinguishable from endogenous insulin by standard assays. C-peptide (connecting peptide) is co-secreted with endogenous insulin from the proinsulin molecule in a 1:1 molar ratio. Critically, C-peptide is NOT present in commercial insulin preparations and undergoes minimal hepatic extraction (unlike insulin which has ~50% first-pass hepatic extraction), giving C-peptide a more stable, reliable reflection of actual β-cell secretory function. Low/absent C-peptide = β-cell failure.
Q62: What is the recommended preprandial capillary plasma glucose goal for most nonpregnant adults with diabetes?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ C
A) 60–99 mg/dL
B) 140–180 mg/dL
C) 80–130 mg/dL
D) <70 mg/dL
Explanation: The ADA recommends a preprandial (fasting or before-meal) capillary plasma glucose goal of 80–130 mg/dL for most non-pregnant adults with diabetes. The post-prandial goal (2 hours after a meal) is <180 mg/dL. These targets aim to maintain A1C <7% while minimizing hypoglycemia risk. Goals are individualized based on age, comorbidities, hypoglycemia risk, and life expectancy. More stringent targets (<70 mg/dL preprandial) would increase hypoglycemia risk without sufficient benefit for most patients.
Q63: Which long-acting insulin analog is available as a once-weekly injection (though not yet FDA approved)?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ C
A) Insulin Degludec
B) Insulin Glargine U-300
C) Insulin Icodec
D) Insulin Detemir
Explanation: Insulin icodec is a novel ultra-long-acting basal insulin analog designed for once-weekly subcutaneous dosing. It forms a depot by reversibly binding to albumin, with a half-life of approximately 196 hours (~8 days). As of 2024–2025, it has been approved in some international markets but is under FDA review in the United States. Insulin degludec (Tresiba) is once-daily with >42 hours duration. Insulin glargine U-300 (Toujeo) is once-daily. Insulin detemir (Levemir) is once- or twice-daily.
Q64: What is a major disadvantage of Continuous Subcutaneous Insulin Infusion (CSII) or insulin pump therapy?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ D
A) Increased risk of weight gain compared to multiple daily injections.
B) Inability to use rapid-acting insulin analogs.
C) Higher frequency of severe hypoglycemia.
D) Risk of ketoacidosis if insulin delivery is interrupted.
Explanation: The most critical disadvantage of CSII (insulin pump therapy) is the risk of rapid-onset diabetic ketoacidosis if insulin delivery is interrupted for any reason — kinked or dislodged catheter, empty reservoir, pump malfunction, or blocked needle. Because insulin pumps exclusively use rapid-acting analogs (no depot of long-acting insulin), any interruption of delivery means the patient has NO insulin coverage within 4–6 hours, rapidly leading to hyperglycemia and ketoacidosis. Patients must be trained to recognize pump failures and always have backup insulin available.
Q65: Why must the dose of mealtime insulin be reduced by 50% when initiating pramlintide?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ B
A) To account for pramlintide’s mild glucose-lowering effect.
B) To avoid a risk of severe hypoglycemia.
C) Because pramlintide doubles the potency of insulin.
D) To reduce the common side effect of nausea.
Explanation: Pramlintide profoundly slows gastric emptying and suppresses post-meal glucagon, significantly blunting the post-meal glucose rise. If the full pre-meal insulin dose is maintained when pramlintide is added, the combined effect of insulin (lowering glucose) plus pramlintide (reducing the glucose rise from the meal) creates a high risk of severe post-prandial hypoglycemia. Therefore, the product labeling mandates reducing the mealtime insulin dose by 50% when initiating pramlintide. The dose is then gradually up-titrated while monitoring glucose response.
Q66: According to the presentation, intensive insulin therapy with three or more injections daily leads to a significant reduction...
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ C
A) Macrovascular complications (CVD, stroke)
B) Acute complications (DKA, HHS)
C) Microvascular complications (retinopathy, nephropathy, neuropathy)
D) Gastrointestinal complications (gastroparesis)
Explanation: The landmark DCCT (Diabetes Control and Complications Trial) demonstrated that intensive insulin therapy (3+ injections/day or CSII) in T1DM reduced microvascular complications by 50–76%: retinopathy risk ↓76%, nephropathy ↓50%, neuropathy ↓60%. The UKPDS confirmed similar benefits in T2DM. Microvascular complications (retinopathy, nephropathy, peripheral neuropathy) are primarily driven by chronic hyperglycemia and are most responsive to glycemic control. Macrovascular complications (CVD, stroke) have a more multifactorial etiology requiring additional interventions (lipids, BP, antiplatelet).
Q67: When drawing up a mixture of NPH and Regular insulin in the same syringe, which type should be drawn first?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ B
A) NPH insulin should be drawn first.
B) Regular insulin should be drawn first.
C) It does not matter which insulin is drawn first.
D) These insulins should never be mixed in the same syringe.
Explanation: The mnemonic 'Clear before Cloudy' guides NPH/Regular mixing: Regular (clear) is drawn first, NPH (cloudy) is drawn second. The reason: if NPH were drawn first and some entered the Regular vial, the protamine or zinc in NPH would contaminate the Regular vial and alter its kinetics — potentially making it behave more like NPH. By drawing Regular (clear) first into the syringe and then NPH (cloudy), any accidental contamination goes into the NPH vial rather than the Regular vial, which is less clinically significant. The vials should be gently rolled (not shaken) before use.
Q68: Which of the following is NOT a risk factor for developing Gestational Diabetes Mellitus (GDM)?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ A
A) Maternal age <25 years
B) Obesity (BMI ≥25 kg/m²)
C) Family history of diabetes
D) Previous delivery of a macrosomic infant (>4 kg)
Explanation: Risk factors for gestational diabetes mellitus (GDM) include: obesity (BMI ≥25 kg/m²), advanced maternal age (>25–35 years), previous macrosomic infant (>4 kg), family history of T2DM, previous GDM, polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS), and certain ethnicities. Young maternal age (<25 years) is actually a PROTECTIVE factor — GDM risk increases with age, not decreases. All other options listed are established GDM risk factors, making age <25 the correct 'NOT a risk factor' answer.
Q69: In the ‘two-step’ approach for diagnosing GDM, what is the next step if a patient’s 1-hour 50-g glucose challenge test is 150...
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ C
A) The patient is diagnosed with GDM.
B) No further workup is needed as the result is normal.
C) A fasting 100-g oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT) should be performed.
D) The 50-g test should be repeated in one week.
Explanation: The two-step GDM screening approach: Step 1 = non-fasting 50-g glucose challenge test (GCT). A result ≥140 mg/dL (some use ≥130 mg/dL) is abnormal. Step 2 = fasting 100-g 3-hour OGTT to confirm diagnosis. A glucose of 150 mg/dL on the 50-g GCT exceeds the 140 mg/dL threshold, so the full diagnostic 3-hour 100-g OGTT is required. GDM is diagnosed if 2 or more of the 4 OGTT values (fasting, 1-hr, 2-hr, 3-hr) meet or exceed Carpenter-Coustan criteria thresholds. A single abnormal GCT value does NOT confirm GDM.
Q70: Why is Metformin generally discontinued and replaced with insulin once pregnancy is confirmed in a patient with pregestationa...
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ C
A) Metformin is known to cause severe birth defects.
B) Metformin is ineffective at lowering blood glucose during pregnancy.
C) Insulin is the gold standard due to its established safety profile and inability to cross the placenta.
D) Metformin significantly increases the risk of preeclampsia.
Explanation: Insulin is the gold standard for managing diabetes in pregnancy because it does not cross the placenta (its molecular weight is too large), has no direct fetal effects, and has decades of safety data. Metformin does cross the placenta, reaching fetal concentrations similar to maternal levels. While some evidence suggests metformin may be used in GDM management, the FDA classifies it as Category B and most guidelines prefer insulin for pregestational T2DM due to the superior control achievable with dose titration and the lack of long-term fetal safety data for metformin.
Q71: What is the primary cause of microvascular complications like retinopathy and nephropathy in diabetes?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ A
A) Glucose toxicity affecting small blood vessels and nerves
B) Hypertension
C) Dyslipidemia
D) Autoimmune processes
Explanation: Chronic hyperglycemia causes microvascular complications through multiple pathways: (1) advanced glycation end-product (AGE) formation, (2) polyol pathway activation (aldose reductase), (3) protein kinase C activation, and (4) oxidative stress — all leading to endothelial dysfunction, thickening of basement membranes, and loss of pericytes in retinal and renal capillaries. These glucose-mediated pathways directly damage small vessels and peripheral nerves. Hypertension and dyslipidemia contribute to macrovascular disease more than microvascular disease.
Q72: A patient with an A1C of 9% has an estimated average glucose (eAG) of approximately:
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ C
A) 154 mg/dL
B) 183 mg/dL
C) 212 mg/dL
D) 240 mg/dL
Explanation: The relationship between A1C and estimated average glucose (eAG) is: eAG (mg/dL) = (28.7 × A1C%) − 46.7. For A1C = 9%: eAG = (28.7 × 9) − 46.7 = 258.3 − 46.7 = 211.6 ≈ 212 mg/dL. This formula allows clinicians to communicate A1C values to patients in the same units they use for home glucose monitoring. Each 1% change in A1C corresponds to approximately 28–29 mg/dL change in average glucose.
Q73: What is a key difference in the pharmacokinetic profile of rapid-acting insulin analogs compared to Regular human insulin?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ C
A) Rapid-acting analogs have a longer duration of action.
B) Rapid-acting analogs have a much higher absorption variability.
C) Rapid-acting analogs have a faster onset of action.
D) Rapid-acting analogs cannot be used in insulin pumps.
Explanation: Rapid-acting insulin analogs (lispro, aspart, glulisine) were engineered with amino acid substitutions that disrupt the tendency of insulin to self-associate into hexamers. This allows them to remain monomeric at physiological concentrations, enabling much faster subcutaneous absorption: onset 5–15 minutes (vs 30–60 min for Regular), peak 1–2 hours (vs 2–4 hr), duration 3–5 hours (vs 6–8 hr). This faster onset closely matches the normal physiological post-meal insulin spike and allows injection immediately before (or even after) meals — a significant practical advantage over Regular insulin which must be injected 30 minutes pre-meal.
Q74: In a patient with Type 1 Diabetes, clinical hyperglycemia typically appears after what percentage of β-cell loss?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ C
A) 10-20%
B) 40-50%
C) 80-90%
D) 100%
Explanation: In T1DM, the autoimmune destruction of β-cells is a gradual process that begins years before clinical diagnosis. The body has significant functional reserve — approximately 80–90% of β-cells must be destroyed before insulin secretion becomes insufficient to maintain normal glucose homeostasis. During the long 'pre-clinical' phase (Stage 1 and 2 T1DM), the remaining 10–20% of β-cells can maintain glucose, explaining why Stage 1 patients are normoglycemic. Once the threshold is crossed (>80–90% destroyed), insulin secretion fails rapidly and clinical hyperglycemia (Stage 3) emerges.
Q75: A patient with a fasting plasma glucose (FPG) of 115 mg/dL and an A1C of 6.1% would be classified as having:
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ C
A) Normal glucose tolerance
B) Type 2 Diabetes
C) Prediabetes
D) Gestational Diabetes
Explanation: Prediabetes diagnostic criteria (ADA): Impaired Fasting Glucose (IFG) = FPG 100–125 mg/dL; Impaired Glucose Tolerance (IGT) = 2-hr OGTT 140–199 mg/dL; High-risk A1C = 5.7%–6.4%. A patient with FPG 115 mg/dL (falls in 100–125 range) and A1C 6.1% (falls in 5.7%–6.4% range) meets prediabetes criteria by both measures. Neither value meets the diabetes threshold (FPG ≥126 mg/dL or A1C ≥6.5%). Prediabetes is not a benign state — it carries significant risk of progression to T2DM and is itself associated with increased cardiovascular risk.
Q76: Which of the following antihypertensive drug classes should be discontinued before conception in a woman with diabetes due to...
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ D
A) Beta-blockers (e.g., labetalol)
B) Calcium channel blockers
C) Methyldopa
D) ACE inhibitors (e.g., lisinopril)
Explanation: ACE inhibitors (and ARBs) are teratogenic — particularly in the 2nd and 3rd trimesters, they cause fetal renal dysplasia, oligohydramnios, skull ossification defects, and neonatal renal failure. They must be discontinued before conception or as soon as pregnancy is detected. Safe antihypertensives in pregnancy include labetalol (beta-blocker), methyldopa (central α-agonist), nifedipine (calcium channel blocker), and hydralazine. Beta-blockers require cautious use but are not contraindicated. ACE inhibitors are the antihypertensive class of choice in diabetic nephropathy — but NOT in pregnancy.
Q77: What is the primary advantage of a basal-bolus insulin regimen using analogs over an NPH/Regular regimen?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ C
A) It is less expensive.
B) It requires fewer daily injections.
C) It more closely mimics natural insulin secretion patterns.
D) It does not require blood glucose monitoring.
Explanation: A basal-bolus regimen using long-acting (glargine/degludec) and rapid-acting (lispro/aspart/glulisine) analogs most closely replicates normal physiological insulin secretion: constant low-level basal insulin mirrors overnight and interprandial hepatic glucose suppression, while rapid-acting bolus doses mirror the sharp post-meal insulin spike. NPH/Regular regimens have more pronounced peaks, higher variability in absorption, and require more rigid meal timing to avoid hypoglycemia. The analog regimen improves A1C, reduces hypoglycemia, and provides greater lifestyle flexibility.
Q78: A patient is prescribed U-500 regular insulin. Its time-action profile is most similar to which other insulin?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ B
A) Insulin Lispro (Rapid-acting)
B) NPH Insulin (Intermediate-acting)
C) Insulin Glargine (Long-acting)
D) U-100 Regular Insulin (Short-acting)
Explanation: Despite being Regular human insulin, U-500 has an entirely different time-action profile than U-100 Regular due to its 5-fold higher concentration. The dense subcutaneous depot dissolves slowly, producing: onset ~30 min, peak 4–8 hours, and duration 13–24 hours — closely resembling NPH (intermediate-acting). This delayed, prolonged profile allows U-500 to serve as both a mealtime and background (basal) insulin when given 2–3 times daily, without requiring a separate long-acting insulin. This is why it is sometimes called a 'prandial + basal hybrid' insulin.
Q79: What is the recommended 2-hour postprandial glucose goal for a pregnant woman with gestational diabetes?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ D
A) <180 mg/dL
B) <153 mg/dL
C) <140 mg/dL
D) ≤120 mg/dL
Explanation: The ADA postprandial glucose goals for pregnant women with diabetes are more stringent than for non-pregnant adults. The 2024 ADA Standards recommend: 1-hour postprandial ≤140 mg/dL OR 2-hour postprandial ≤120 mg/dL. These tighter targets reflect the goal of minimizing fetal exposure to hyperglycemia, which drives complications like macrosomia, neonatal hypoglycemia, and shoulder dystocia. The non-pregnant adult target for 2-hour postprandial is <180 mg/dL — significantly more lenient than the ≤120 mg/dL required in pregnancy.
Q80: Lipodystrophy, an adverse effect of insulin injections, can be minimized by:
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ D
A) Using a longer needle for injections.
B) Injecting into the same site every time.
C) Administering insulin intravenously.
D) Rotation of injection sites.
Explanation: Lipodystrophy (lipoatrophy or lipohypertrophy) at insulin injection sites is caused by repeated trauma and insulin deposition at the same subcutaneous location. Lipohypertrophy (the more common form — fibrofatty nodules) alters insulin absorption, causing unpredictable pharmacokinetics and erratic glucose control. The primary prevention is systematic rotation of injection sites: rotating within the same region (abdomen, thigh, arm, buttock) AND between different regions, ensuring at least 1 cm between injection points. Using the same site repetitively triggers the local tissue reaction.
Q81: What is the primary purpose of basal insulin in a Type 1 Diabetes regimen?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ C
A) To cover the carbohydrates eaten at meals.
B) To correct high blood glucose levels quickly.
C) To prevent ketosis and control fasting plasma glucose.
D) To stimulate the pancreas to produce more of its own insulin.
Explanation: Basal insulin (long-acting analogs like glargine or degludec) serves two primary purposes in T1DM: (1) suppression of hepatic glucose output between meals and overnight to prevent fasting hyperglycemia, and (2) prevention of ketogenesis during fasting. In T1DM, without any endogenous insulin, hepatic glucose production is unrestrained and ketogenesis occurs rapidly. Basal insulin does NOT cover meal-related glucose excursions (that is the role of bolus insulin) and does NOT stimulate the pancreas (T1DM has no functional β-cells to stimulate).
Q82: When initiating an insulin pump (CSII), which type of insulin is almost exclusively used?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ B
A) NPH insulin
B) Rapid-acting insulin analogs (e.g., Lispro, Aspart)
C) Long-acting insulin analogs (e.g., Glargine, Detemir)
D) Premixed 70/30 insulin
Explanation: Insulin pumps (CSII) use exclusively rapid-acting insulin analogs — lispro (Humalog), aspart (NovoLog), glulisine (Apidra), or faster-acting aspart (Fiasp). The pump delivers small basal rate infusions continuously throughout the day and bolus doses at mealtimes. Long-acting insulins (glargine, detemir, degludec) are incompatible with pump use because they are formulated for depot absorption and may crystallize or precipitate in the pump catheter. NPH is also unsuitable. Rapid-acting analogs are required due to their consistent kinetics and ability to remain stable in the pump reservoir.
Q83: A patient’s Total Daily Insulin (TDI) is 45 units. Using the ‘Rule of 1800’, what is their estimated Correction Factor (CF)?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ C
A) 1 unit will lower glucose by 11 mg/dL.
B) 1 unit will cover 40 grams of carbohydrates.
C) 1 unit will lower glucose by 40 mg/dL.
D) The patient needs 40 units to correct a high glucose.
Explanation: The Rule of 1800 (for rapid-acting analogs): Correction Factor = 1800 / TDI = 1800 / 45 = 40 mg/dL per unit. This means one unit of insulin will lower blood glucose by approximately 40 mg/dL. Clinical application: If a patient has glucose 240 mg/dL and target is 120 mg/dL, correction dose = (240 − 120) / 40 = 3 units. The Rule of 1800 gives the ISF/CF in units of mg/dL per unit of insulin. Answer A (11 mg/dL) would require TDI = 1800/11 ≈ 164 units. Answer B describes the ICR (carbohydrate coverage), not the CF.
Q84: Which of the following is a classic sign of diabetes resulting from osmotic diuresis caused by glucosuria?
🔬 Endocrine📂 Diabetes✅ C
A) Polyphagia (excessive hunger)
B) Weight loss
C) Polyuria (excessive urination)
D) Ketonuria (ketones in urine)
Explanation: Glucosuria (glucose in urine) occurs when blood glucose exceeds the renal threshold (~180 mg/dL), causing glucose to spill into the tubular filtrate. Glucose is an osmotic agent — it draws water into the tubular lumen, causing osmotic diuresis. This leads to polyuria (large urine volumes), which in turn causes polydipsia (excessive thirst) as the body attempts to compensate for fluid loss. These three 'polys' (polyuria, polydipsia, polyphagia) are the classic presenting symptoms of diabetes. Polyuria specifically results from osmotic diuresis from glucosuria.
