The combination of Cefazolin and Ciprofloxacin (B) lacks adequate activity against anaerobic organisms (e.g., B. fragilis group). Ceftriaxone plus Metronidazole (C) is a good choice for intra-abdominal infections, although it has limited activity against Enterococcus.
Q11: O.R. is a 73-year-old man who presents to the emergency department with a 3-day history of fever, chills, frequency, urgency,...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Urinary Tract Infections✅ D
A) Amoxicillin clavulanate 75 orally twice daily for 14 days.
B) Trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole double strength orally twice daily for 7 days.
C) Cefprozil 500 mg orally twice daily for 21 days.
D) Ciprofloxacin 500 mg orally twice daily for 28 days.
Explanation: Beta-lactam antibiotics (A, C) are generally not recommended as first-line agents for acute bacterial prostatitis. Although TMP/SMX is appropriate, the 7-day duration (B) is too short; treatment duration should be 4 weeks (28 days). Fluoroquinolones (Ciprofloxacin) are an appropriate antibiotic class for prostatitis, and the 28-day duration is also appropriate.
Q12: J.M. is a 72-year-old woman with a history of atrial fibrillation, hypertension, a right total hip replacement 8 months earli...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Tuberculosis✅ B
A) Vancomycin 1000 mg intravenously every 12 hours plus rifampin 300 mg orally twice daily for 2 weeks.
B) Cefazolin 2 g intravenously every 8 hours plus rifampin 300 mg orally twice daily for 6 weeks followed by long-term oral antibiotics.
C) Nafcillin 1 g intravenously every 4 hours for 6 weeks.
D) Daptomycin 6 mg/kg intravenously daily for 6 weeks followed by long-term oral antibiotics.
Explanation: Since the infection is caused by Methicillin-Sensitive Staphylococcus aureus (MSSA), using Vancomycin (A) or Daptomycin (D) is unnecessary and poses a risk of toxicity. For prosthetic joint infections caused by Staphylococcus that retain the prosthesis, the treatment involves a pathogen-specific intravenous therapy (Nafcillin or Cefazolin) combined with rifampin for 2–6 weeks, followed by rifampin plus a fluoroquinolone for 3 to 6 months. Option C is incorrect because rifampin must be combined with the primary antibiotic for prosthetic joint infections. Cefazolin plus Rifampin for 6 weeks followed by long-term oral antibiotics (B) is the best choice.
Q13: B.K. is a 58-year-old woman (height 66 inches, weight 82 kg) who is scheduled to undergo a total knee replacement tomorrow. S...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Osteomyelitis and Bone Infections✅ A
A) Cefazolin 2 g within 1 hour of the incision and no doses postoperatively.
B) Cefazolin 2 g within 4 hours of the incision and three doses every 8 hours postoperatively.
C) Cefazolin 1 g within 1 hour of the incision and three doses every 8 hours postoperatively.
D) Cefazolin 1 g within 4 hours of the incision and no doses postoperatively.
Explanation: For orthopedic procedures involving prosthetic materials, surgical prophylaxis is necessary. The preferred agent is Cefazolin at a dosage of at least 2 g (or 3 g for patients weighing 120 kg or more). The antibiotic must be administered within 1 hour of the incision. Administration beyond surgical closure is generally unnecessary, especially for shorter orthopedic procedures.
Q14: R.L. is a 68-year-old man who presents to the emergency department with coughing and shortness of breath... If R.L. were hosp...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Respiratory Infections✅ C
A) Ampicillin/sulbactam 3 g intravenously every 6 hours.
B) Piperacillin/tazobactam 4.5 g intravenously every 6 hours plus gentamicin 180 mg intravenously every 12 hours.
C) Ceftriaxone 2 g intravenously every 24 hours plus azithromycin 500 mg intravenously every 24 hours.
D) Doxycycline 100 mg intravenously every 12 hours.
Explanation: For hospitalized patients with non-severe CAP (R.L. has risk factors like CHF, requiring inpatient management), empiric treatment involves a respiratory fluoroquinolone or a beta-lactam plus a macrolide (or doxycycline). Ceftriaxone plus azithromycin is the best initial choice because it provides excellent activity against typical organisms (S. pneumoniae, H. influenzae) and atypical organisms (due to azithromycin). Ampicillin/sulbactam (A) and Piperacillin/tazobactam + Gentamicin (B) lack coverage for atypical organisms. Doxycycline monotherapy (D) is not recommended for hospitalized patients.
Q15: B.P. is a 66-year-old woman who underwent a two-vessel coronary artery bypass graft 8 days ago and has been on a ventilator.....
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Respiratory Infections✅ D
A) Ceftriaxone 2 g intravenously every 24 hours plus gentamicin 7 mg/kg intravenously every 24 hours plus linezolid 600 mg intravenously every 12 hours.
B) Piperacillin/tazobactam 4.5 g intravenously every 6 hours.
C) Levofloxacin 750 mg intravenously every 24 hours plus linezolid 600 mg intravenously every 12 hours.
D) Cefepime 2 g intravenously every 8 hours plus tobramycin 7 mg/kg intravenously every 24 hours plus vancomycin 15 mg/kg intravenously every 12 hours.
Explanation: B.P. has Ventilator-Associated Pneumonia (VAP) since it arose more than 48 hours after intubation. Because she has been hospitalized for 5 days or more (8 days), she is at increased risk for Multi-Drug Resistant (MDR) organisms, specifically P. aeruginosa and MRSA. Empiric therapy for HAP/VAP should cover S. aureus (MSSA), P. aeruginosa, and other Gram-negative organisms. Due to her risk factors for MDR organisms, she needs two antibiotics with activity against P. aeruginosa and one agent for MRSA. Option D (Cefepime + Tobramycin + Vancomycin) provides two antipseudomonal agents (Cefepime and Tobramycin) and coverage for MRSA (Vancomycin). Ceftriaxone (A) is incorrect because it lacks P. aeruginosa activity.
Q16: S.C. is a 46-year-old woman who presents to the clinic with purulent nasal discharge, nasal and facial congestion, headaches,...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Respiratory Infections✅ C
A) Cefpodoxime proxetil 200 mg orally twice daily.
B) Clindamycin 300 mg orally four times daily.
C) Amoxicillin/clavulanate 875 mg/125 mg orally twice daily.
D) No antibiotic therapy needed.
Explanation: The patient’s symptoms (lasting >10 days, or having improved and then worsened—the “double-sickening” pattern) suggest acute bacterial sinusitis. Amoxicillin/clavulanate is considered a first-line agent for bacterial sinusitis. No antibiotic therapy (D) is incorrect because the pattern suggests bacterial infection. Cefpodoxime or Clindamycin alone are not recommended for treatment.
Q17: G.N. is a 62-year-old woman who presents to the emergency department with a 3-day history of urinary frequency and dysuria......
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Urinary Tract Infections✅ B
A) Trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole double strength orally twice daily for 7 days. Monitor INR carefully.
B) Ciprofloxacin 400 mg intravenously twice daily and then 500 mg orally twice daily for a total of 7 days. Monitor INR carefully.
C) Gentamicin 140 mg intravenously every 24 hours for 3 days.
D) Tigecycline 100 mg once, then 50 mg intravenously every 12 hours and then doxycycline 100 mg orally twice daily for a total of 10 days.
Explanation: G.N. presents with symptoms and signs (fever, flank pain, abnormal vital signs, positive casts on urinalysis) consistent with complicated pyelonephritis. TMP/SMX (A) is inappropriate because the duration is too short (should be 14 days), and it has a significant drug interaction with warfarin. Ciprofloxacin for a total of 7 days is an appropriate regimen and duration for complicated pyelonephritis. Gentamicin for 3 days (C) is too short. Tigecycline/Doxycycline (D) is not recommended for complicated pyelonephritis.
Q18: G.N. returns to the clinic in 6 months with no urinary symptoms, but her chief concern is now an ulcer on her right foot... W...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Urinary Tract Infections✅ C
A) Nafcillin 2 g intravenously every 6 hours for 6–12 weeks.
B) Tobramycin 120 mg intravenously every 12 hours plus levofloxacin 750 mg intravenously every 24 hours for 1–2 weeks.
C) Piperacillin/tazobactam 3.375 g intravenously every 6 hours for 1–2 weeks.
D) Below-the-knee amputation followed by ceftriaxone 1 g intravenously every 24 hours for 1 week.
Explanation: G.N. has a diabetic foot infection, which are generally polymicrobial and can involve Gram-positive, Gram-negative, and anaerobic organisms. Given the macerated ulcer and history of water exposure (walking barefoot on the beach, suggesting potential P. aeruginosa risk), a regimen active against P. aeruginosa is likely necessary. Beta-lactamase inhibitor combinations, such as Piperacillin/tazobactam, are good agents because they cover the usual mix of organisms. Nafcillin (A) misses Gram-negative organisms and anaerobes. Tobramycin/Levofloxacin (B) misses anaerobes, and Tobramycin is risky in diabetic patients due to nephrotoxicity.
Q19: W.A. is a 55-year-old man who is admitted with weight loss, malaise, and severe back pain... A computed tomography guided bon...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Osteomyelitis and Bone Infections✅ A
A) Vancomycin 15 mg/kg intravenously every 12 hours for 6 weeks.
B) Nafcillin 2 g intravenously every 6 hours for 2 weeks.
C) Levofloxacin 750 mg orally every 24 hours for 6 weeks.
D) Ampicillin/sulbactam 3 g intravenously every 6 hours for 2 weeks.
Explanation: The patient has findings consistent with vertebral osteomyelitis (bone destruction of lumbar vertebrae L1 and L2). The bone biopsy shows Gram-positive cocci in clusters, likely S. aureus. Since sensitivities are unknown, Vancomycin is the best empiric choice to cover potential MRSA. Treatment duration for acute osteomyelitis is typically 3–6 weeks (average 6 weeks). Options B and D are too short (2 weeks). Oral Levofloxacin (C) is unlikely to achieve adequate bone concentrations to eradicate S. aureus.
Q20: D.M. is a 21-year-old university student who presents to the emergency department with the worst headache of his life... Whic...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Respiratory Infections✅ D
A) Penicillin G 4 million units intravenously every 4 hours.
B) Ceftriaxone 2 g intravenously every 12 hours.
C) Ceftriaxone 2 g intravenously every 12 hours plus dexamethasone 10 mg intravenously every 6 hours.
D) Ceftriaxone 2 g intravenously every 12 hours plus vancomycin 1000 mg intravenously every 8 hours plus dexamethasone 10 mg intravenously every 6 hours.
Explanation: D.M. presents with classic bacterial meningitis signs (fever, severe headache, neck stiffness, highly elevated WBC in CSF, low CSF glucose ratio to peripheral glucose). Empiric therapy for bacterial meningitis in children and adults (2–50 years) is Ceftriaxone plus Vancomycin. Vancomycin is crucial for activity against highly penicillin-resistant S. pneumoniae. Dexamethasone should also be added adjunctive therapy because it improves outcomes and decreases mortality in adults with pneumococcal meningitis.
Q21: D.M.’s CSF cultures grew N. meningitidis. Which is the best recommendation for meningitis prophylaxis?
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Tuberculosis✅ A
A) The health care providers in close contact with D.M. should receive rifampin 600 mg orally every 12 hours for four doses.
B) Everyone in D.M.’s dormitory and in all of his classes should receive rifampin 600 mg orally every 24 hours for 4 days.
C) Everyone in the emergency department at the time of D.M.’s presentation should receive the meningococcal conjugate vaccine.
D) Everyone in the emergency department at the time of D.M.’s presentation should receive rifampin 600 mg orally every 12 hours for four doses.
Explanation: Prophylaxis for N. meningitidis is recommended only for close contacts (household, day care) and those exposed to the index case’s oral secretions. Health care providers who were in close contact (e.g., during intubation or resuscitation) should receive prophylaxis. The correct regimen for adults is Rifampin 600 mg every 12 hours for four doses.
Q22: T.S. is a 48-year-old man who presents to the emergency department with fever, chills... A transesophageal echocardiogram rev...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Tuberculosis✅ D
A) Nafcillin therapy for 7–10 days.
B) Nafcillin plus rifampin plus gentamicin therapy for 6 weeks or longer.
C) Nafcillin plus gentamicin therapy for 2 weeks of both antibiotics.
D) Nafcillin therapy for 6 weeks.
Explanation: T.S. has native valve endocarditis caused by MSSA (S. aureus confirmed by culture). The recommended treatment for MSSA native valve endocarditis is Nafcillin (or Oxacillin or Cefazolin) for 6 weeks. Options A and C are incorrect because the duration (7–10 days or 2 weeks) is too short for S. aureus endocarditis. Option B is incorrect because Rifampin and Gentamicin are only necessary for prosthetic valve endocarditis, not native valve endocarditis.
Q23: Six months after treatment of his endocarditis, T.S. is visiting his dentist for a tooth extraction. Which antibiotic is best...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Endocarditis✅ B
A) Tooth extractions do not warrant endocarditis prophylaxis.
B) Administer amoxicillin 2 g 1 hour before the extraction.
C) Administer amoxicillin 3 g 1 hour before the extraction and 1.5 g every 6 hours for four doses after the extraction.
D) T.S. is not at increased risk of endocarditis and does not need prophylactic antibiotics.
Explanation: The patient is at increased risk of endocarditis due to his history of the disease. Dental procedures involving perforation of the oral mucosa (like a tooth extraction) warrant prophylaxis for those at risk. The current recommended dose for standard general prophylaxis is Amoxicillin 2 g 1 hour before the procedure.
Q24: R.K. is a 72-year-old man who presents to the emergency department with a 2-day history of redness and swelling of his upper ...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Urinary Tract Infections✅ B
A) Metronidazole 500 mg orally three times daily for 7 days.
B) Vancomycin 125 mg orally four times daily for 10 days.
C) Fidaxomicin 200 mg orally twice daily for 14 days.
D) Rifaximin 400 mg orally twice daily for 7 days.
Explanation: R.K. is experiencing a severe C. difficile infection (CDI), indicated by his high WBC (24,500 cells/mm³ > 15 x 10³ cells/mm³) and elevated SCr (1.74 mg/dL > 1.5 mg/dL). For severe CDI, the preferred first-line treatment options are Vancomycin 125 mg orally four times daily for 10 days or Fidaxomicin 200 mg orally twice daily for 10 days. Option C is incorrect because the duration (14 days) is too long. Metronidazole (A) is not optimal for severe infection. Vancomycin 125 mg orally four times daily for 10 days (B) is the best choice among the options.
Q25: You are a pharmacist who works closely with the surgery department... Which is the best practice for optimizing surgical prop...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Urinary Tract Infections✅ A
A) Antibiotics should be redosed for extended surgical procedures; redose if the surgery lasts longer than 4 hours or involves considerable blood loss.
B) All patients should be given antibiotics for 24 hours after the procedure; this will optimize prophylaxis.
C) Preoperative antibiotics can be given up to 4 hours before the incision; this will make giving the antibiotics logistically easier.
D) Vancomycin is the antibiotic of choice for surgical wound prophylaxis because of its long half-life and activity against MRSA.
Explanation: Redosing antibiotics is critical for surgical prophylaxis, especially if the surgery lasts longer than 4 hours (or more than two drug half-lives) or involves considerable blood loss. Giving antibiotics for 24 hours post-procedure (B) is generally unnecessary (except sometimes for cardiac procedures) and increases the risk of resistance. Antibiotics should be administered within 60 minutes before the incision (or 60–120 minutes for Vancomycin), not up to 4 hours before. Vancomycin (D) should not be used routinely for surgical prophylaxis unless the patient has a life-threatening allergy to beta-lactam antibiotics.
Q26: K.E. is a 29-year-old asymptomatic patient who is HIV positive. She recently found out she is pregnant and is estimated to be...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 HIV/AIDS✅ D
A) No drug therapy is needed; the risks to the fetus outweigh any benefits.
B) Administer zidovudine 300 mg twice daily orally throughout the pregnancy, followed by zidovudine during labor and subsequently to the infant for 6 weeks.
C) No drug therapy is needed now, but administer a single dose of nevirapine at the onset of labor.
D) Administer a potent combination antiretroviral therapy (ART) regimen.
Explanation: A potent combination ART regimen should be initiated in all HIV-infected patients, including pregnant women, regardless of the CD4 count. Combination ART regimens are preferred over single-drug therapy (B) because they significantly decrease the risk of transmission. Since she has a high viral load and low CD4 count, combination ART is necessary.
Q27: R.E. is a 33-year-old man who recently tested HIV positive. He is initiated on tenofovir alafenamide, emtricitabine, and dolu...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 HIV/AIDS✅ A
A) Watch for trouble sleeping because dolutegravir can cause insomnia.
B) If you think you are having a drug-related adverse effect, cut the dose of all of your drugs in half.
C) Talk to your pharmacist about drug interactions because both dolutegravir and tenofovir inhibit cytochrome P450 (CYP) 3A4.
D) Tenofovir and emtricitabine cause additive peripheral neuropathy, so let your pharmacist know if you have tingling in your extremities.
Explanation: Dolutegravir is associated with insomnia and CNS symptoms. Instructing the patient to cut the dose in half (B) is incorrect because antiretroviral drugs should always be used at the recommended dose. Neither dolutegravir nor tenofovir inhibit CYP3A4. Peripheral neuropathy (D) is not associated with tenofovir or emtricitabine.
Q28: P.P., a 43-year-old man who is HIV positive, presents to the clinic with a headache that has gradually worsened during the pa...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 HIV/AIDS✅ B
A) No maintenance treatment is necessary.
B) Administer fluconazole 200 mg daily orally.
C) Administer amphotericin B 1 mg/kg weekly intravenously.
D) He is protected as long as he is also receiving Pneumocystis jiroveci pneumonia (PJP) prophylaxis.
Explanation: Patients with cryptococcal meningitis require long-term secondary prevention/maintenance therapy. The preferred maintenance therapy is Fluconazole 200 mg daily for at least 1 year. Fluconazole is preferred over weekly intravenous Amphotericin B due to its ease of administration and lower toxicity. PJP prophylaxis agents have no activity against Cryptococcus.
Q29: A study compares the incidence of active tuberculosis (TB) infection in patients receiving isoniazid versus rifampin for late...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Tuberculosis✅ C
A) 5.
B) 50.
C) 200.
D) 2000.
Explanation: The Number Needed to Treat (NNT) is calculated as 1 / Absolute Risk Reduction (ARR). ARR = Incidence in Rifampin group (0.008) - Incidence in Isoniazid group (0.003) = 0.005. NNT = 1 / 0.005 = 200.
Q30: G.T. is a 34-year-old woman positive for HIV who is brought to the emergency department by her boyfriend... A computed tomogr...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 HIV/AIDS✅ D
A) Atovaquone for 4–6 weeks.
B) High-dose trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole plus clindamycin for 6 weeks.
C) Pyrimethamine plus sulfadiazine for 6 weeks.
D) Pyrimethamine plus clindamycin and leucovorin for 6 weeks.
Explanation: The patient has toxoplasmic encephalitis. Pyrimethamine plus sulfadiazine is the preferred therapy. However, leucovorin (folinic acid) must always be added to pyrimethamine to reduce its bone marrow–suppressive effects. Since Pyrimethamine + Sulfadiazine + Leucovorin is not an option, the alternative regimen of Pyrimethamine plus Clindamycin plus Leucovorin for 6 weeks is the correct choice, especially since the patient already takes dapsone for PJP prophylaxis, suggesting a potential sulfa sensitivity.
Q31: H.Y., a 49-year-old man with acute myeloid leukemia, is given a diagnosis of tuberculosis (TB) and initiated on empiric thera...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Tuberculosis✅ D
A) Fluconazole.
B) Voriconazole.
C) Isavuconazonium.
D) Amphotericin lipid formulation.
Explanation: The patient needs treatment for Aspergillosis while simultaneously receiving Rifampin for TB. Fluconazole (A) has no activity against Aspergillus. Voriconazole (B) and Isavuconazonium (C) both have significant drug interactions with Rifampin, which is a strong enzyme inducer, leading to significantly lower antifungal concentrations. Amphotericin lipid formulation (D) is the best option because it is active against Aspergillus and lacks drug interactions with the TB medications.
Q32: P.I. is a 35-year-old woman who presents to the clinic with a 2-week history of night sweats, fatigue, weight loss, and a per...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 HIV/AIDS✅ B
A) Isoniazid for 6 months.
B) Isoniazid, rifampin, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol for 2 months, followed by isoniazid and rifampin for 4 more months.
C) Isoniazid and rifampin for 6 months.
D) Levofloxacin, isoniazid, and rifampin for both TB and other bacterial causes of pneumonia.
Explanation: P.I. has active tuberculosis (TB), confirmed by symptoms, positive TST, and positive acid-fast bacilli sputum sample. The recommended therapy for drug-susceptible active TB in patients without HIV is an intensive phase of four drugs: Isoniazid, Rifampin, Pyrazinamide, and Ethambutol for 2 months (daily or five times weekly), followed by a continuation phase of Isoniazid and Rifampin for 4 more months. Therapy with fewer than three antibiotics in the intensive phase (A, C) is inappropriate. Fluoroquinolones (D) are not recommended as first-line monotherapy.
Q33: A prospective, double-blind study compared the effects of two therapies... Which is the best statistical test to use to compa...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 HIV/AIDS✅ C
A) Analysis of variance.
B) Chi-square test.
C) Student t-test.
D) Wilcoxon rank sum test.
Explanation: The end points (mean change in viral load, mean change in CD4 counts) are continuous data and, given the large sample size (350 patients), are assumed to be normally distributed. Therefore, a parametric test is indicated. The Student t-test is the best parametric test for comparing two groups. Analysis of variance (A) compares more than two groups, and the Chi-square test (B) compares categorical data.
Q34: J.Z. is a 42-year-old HIV-positive man with a diagnosis of cytomegalovirus (CMV) retinitis 9 months ago... Today, his CD4 cou...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 HIV/AIDS✅ A
A) Discontinue valganciclovir because his CD4 count has been above 100 cells/mm³ for 3 months.
B) Continue valganciclovir until the CD4 count has been above 100 cells/mm³ for 1 year.
C) Finish 1 year of therapy with valganciclovir and then discontinue the medication if his CD4 count is still above 100 cells/mm³.
D) Continue valganciclovir indefinitely.
Explanation: Secondary prophylaxis (chronic maintenance therapy) for CMV retinitis may be discontinued if the CD4 count is greater than 100 cells/mm³ for 3–6 months in response to ART, and the CMV lesions are inactive. Since his CD4 count (175, 312) has been above 100 cells/mm³ for at least 3 months (3 months ago to today), Valganciclovir can be discontinued (in consultation with an ophthalmologist).
Q35: K.T. is a 55-year-old woman who is hospitalized with a presumed Candida infection. She currently receives simvastatin, diazep...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Fungal Infections✅ C
A) Liposomal amphotericin
B) B. Fluconazole.
C) Micafungin.
D) Olorofim.
Explanation: Empiric treatment for Candidemia should start with an echinocandin (like Micafungin). Fluconazole should only be used if the organism is known or suspected to be sensitive to fluconazole. Micafungin is preferred empirically because echinocandins typically have fewer drug interactions compared to azoles, which is important given her polypharmacy (including dofetilide and statins, which are susceptible to CYP interactions). Liposomal amphotericin B (A) is generally reserved for resistance or intolerance.
Q36: T.E. is a 51-year-old man who has been HIV positive for 23 years... He is allergic to penicillins and sulfonamide antibiotics...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 HIV/AIDS✅ C
A) Trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole one double-strength tablet by mouth daily.
B) Dapsone 100 mg by mouth weekly.
C) Atovaquone 1500 mg by mouth daily.
D) Pentamidine 300 mg by nebulization once monthly.
Explanation: T.E. needs primary prophylaxis for PJP because his CD4 count is 112 cells/mm³ (< 200 cells/mm³). Due to his sulfonamide allergy, TMP/SMX (A) is contraindicated. Dapsone (B) is an alternative, but the dose listed (weekly) is incorrect; when used alone, Dapsone must be dosed daily. Pentamidine by nebulization (D) is difficult to administer. Atovaquone 1500 mg once daily is considered the preferred alternative prophylactic regimen based on safety, tolerability, and efficacy.
Q37: O.D. is a 38-year-old HIV positive man originally from Puerto Rico... A bronchoscopy is performed and the cultures are positi...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 HIV/AIDS✅ A
A) Amphotericin B lipid formulation for 1–2 weeks followed by itraconazole 200 mg three times daily for 3 days followed by 200 mg twice daily for 12 weeks total.
B) Micafungin 100 mg IV daily for 1–2 weeks followed by fluconazole 400 mg daily for 12 weeks total.
C) Voriconazole 200 mg twice daily for 6 weeks total.
D) Isavuconazole 200 mg every 8 hours for 2 days followed by 200 mg daily for 6 weeks total.
Explanation: Given that O.D. is hospitalized, worsening, and HIV-positive, this is considered a moderate to severe case of Histoplasmosis. The recommended treatment is the induction phase using a Lipid formulation of Amphotericin B (3–5 mg/kg/day) for 1–2 weeks, followed by the consolidation phase using Itraconazole to complete 12 weeks of therapy. Echinocandins (B) are generally ineffective against Histoplasma. Voriconazole (C) and Isavuconazole (D) are not currently recommended due to limited clinical data.
Q38: R.V. is a 35-year-old man who presents to the emergency department with a persistent cough... He is not HIV-infected. Which o...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 HIV/AIDS✅ B
A) Rifampin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol 3 times weekly for 2 months followed by rifampin and isoniazid 2 times weekly for 4 months.
B) Rifampin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol daily for 2 months followed by rifampin and isoniazid 5 times weekly for 4 months.
C) Rifampin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol daily for 2 months followed by rifampin, isoniazid, and ethambutol 3 times weekly for 4 months.
D) Rifampin, isoniazid, pyrazinamide, and ethambutol 5 times weekly for 2 months followed by rifampin, isoniazid, and pyrazinamide 2 times weekly for 4 months.
Explanation: This patient has active TB confirmed by symptoms, positive IGRA, and positive acid-fast bacillus sputum. The standard recommended regimen for active TB in patients without HIV involves an intensive phase of four drugs for 2 months, followed by a continuation phase of two drugs (Rifampin and Isoniazid) for 4 months. The ideal schedule is daily or 5 times weekly for both phases. Option A (3 times weekly/2 times weekly) is optional but not the recommended therapy. Option B reflects the daily/5 times weekly schedule. C and D include three drugs in the continuation phase, which is incorrect
Q39: A 68-year-old man with a history of COPD and diabetes is diagnosed with community-acquired pneumonia (CAP) in an outpatient s...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Respiratory Infections✅ B
A) Doxycycline monotherapy
B) A respiratory fluoroquinolone
C) Intravenous ceftriaxone plus a macrolide
D) Amoxicillin monotherapy
Explanation: Patients with COPD and diabetes have comorbidities; respiratory fluoroquinolone is recommended per IDSA/ATS CAP guidelines for outpatients with comorbidities.
Q40: A patient in the intensive care unit for 10 days develops ventilator-associated pneumonia (VAP). The hospital unit has a 15% ...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Respiratory Infections✅ C
A) A standard CAP regimen such as ceftriaxone plus azithromycin
B) A single broad-spectrum agent such as piperacillin/tazobactam
C) Two agents with anti-pseudomonal activity plus an agent with MRSA activity
D) An anti-pseudomonal β-lactam plus an aminoglycoside
Explanation: VAP with 15% MRSA incidence (>10%) requires two anti-pseudomonal agents plus MRSA coverage per IDSA/SHEA HAP/VAP guidelines.
Q41: A 45-year-old man presents with symptoms of influenza that began suddenly 60 hours ago. He is otherwise healthy with no chron...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Respiratory Infections✅ C
A) Prescribe baloxavir as a single dose
B) Prescribe azithromycin to prevent secondary bacterial pneumonia
C) Symptomatic treatment only
D) Prescribe oseltamivir for 5 days
Explanation: Neuraminidase inhibitors must be started within 48 hours of symptom onset to be effective; at 60 hours, only symptomatic treatment is appropriate.
Q42: In which of the following patient populations is it recommended to screen for and treat asymptomatic bacteriuria?
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Urinary Tract Infections✅ B
A) Patients with poorly controlled diabetes
B) Pregnant women
C) Elderly patients in nursing homes with indwelling catheters
D) Non-pregnant, premenopausal women
Explanation: Pregnant women are the recommended population for screening and treatment of asymptomatic bacteriuria per IDSA guidelines.
Q43: A patient is diagnosed with necrotizing fasciitis. In addition to broad-spectrum coverage against Gram-positive, Gram-negativ...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Skin and Soft Tissue Infections✅ B
A) Cefepime
B) Clindamycin
C) Metronidazole
D) Vancomycin
Explanation: Clindamycin (or linezolid) is added empirically to suppress toxin and cytokine production when Group A Streptococcus is suspected in necrotizing fasciitis.
Q44: A patient with a prosthetic hip infection caused by methicillin-sensitive *Staphylococcus aureus* (MSSA) undergoes debridemen...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Tuberculosis✅ B
A) Indefinite oral suppression with nafcillin
B) A prolonged course of rifampin combined with an oral fluoroquinolone
C) A 2-week course of intravenous vancomycin plus rifampin
D) Intravenous nafcillin for a total of 6 weeks only
Explanation: For MSSA prosthetic joint infection with retained prosthesis, rifampin plus an oral fluoroquinolone for 3-6 months is recommended following initial IV therapy.
Q45: What is the recommended empiric antibiotic regimen for suspected bacterial meningitis in a 65-year-old patient with no other ...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 CNS Infections✅ B
A) Vancomycin + meropenem
B) Ceftriaxone + vancomycin + ampicillin
C) Ceftriaxone + vancomycin
D) Ampicillin + gentamicin
Explanation: Ceftriaxone + vancomycin + ampicillin covers S. pneumoniae, MRSA, and Listeria, which is required for patients ≥50 years with suspected bacterial meningitis.
Q46: A patient with a history of previous bacterial endocarditis is scheduled for a dental procedure that involves perforation of ...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Endocarditis✅ A
A) Amoxicillin 2 g taken 1 hour before the procedure
B) Clindamycin 600 mg taken 1 hour before the procedure
C) Amoxicillin 2 g taken immediately after the procedure
D) A 5-day course of cephalexin starting the day of the procedure
Explanation: Amoxicillin 2 g orally 30-60 minutes before the dental procedure is the standard prophylaxis regimen for patients with previous endocarditis.
Q47: A patient is diagnosed with a severe, initial episode of *Clostridioides difficile* infection, indicated by a white blood cel...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Intra-abdominal and C. difficile Infections✅ A
A) Oral vancomycin 125 mg four times daily for 10 days
B) Intravenous metronidazole 500 mg every 8 hours
C) Rifaximin 400 mg three times daily for 20 days
D) Oral metronidazole 500 mg three times daily for 10 days
Explanation: Oral vancomycin 125 mg four times daily for 10 days is recommended for severe CDI (WBC ≥15,000 or SCr >1.5 mg/dL).
Q48: According to the principles of surgical prophylaxis, when is the optimal time to administer a preoperative dose of cefazolin?
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Surgical Prophylaxis✅ C
A) On call to the operating room
B) Immediately after the surgical incision is closed
C) Within 60 minutes before the surgical incision
D) 2 to 4 hours before the surgical incision
Explanation: Cefazolin should be administered within 60 minutes before the surgical incision to ensure adequate tissue levels at the time of incision.
Q49: According to current guidelines, when should antiretroviral therapy (ART) be initiated for a newly diagnosed, asymptomatic HI...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 HIV/AIDS✅ C
A) When the patient develops an AIDS-defining illness
B) When the CD4 count falls below 500 cells/mm3
C) Immediately, as treatment is recommended for all infected patients regardless of CD4 count
D) When the CD4 count falls below 350 cells/mm3
Explanation: ART is recommended immediately for all HIV-infected patients regardless of CD4 count per current DHHS guidelines.
Q50: A patient with HIV has a CD4 count of 150 cells/mm3. What is the primary opportunistic infection for which prophylaxis is ind...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 HIV/AIDS✅ D
A) Cytomegalovirus (CMV) retinitis
B) *Mycobacterium avium* complex (MAC)
C) Toxoplasmosis
D) *Pneumocystis jiroveci* pneumonia (PJP)
Explanation: At CD4 150 cells/mm³ (below 200), primary prophylaxis for PJP (Pneumocystis jiroveci pneumonia) is indicated.
Q51: A patient without HIV is diagnosed with drug-susceptible active pulmonary tuberculosis. What is the standard duration of the ...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 HIV/AIDS✅ D
A) 7 months
B) 2 months
C) 12 months
D) 4 months
Explanation: The continuation phase of standard TB treatment is 4 months (isoniazid + rifampin), following the 2-month intensive phase.
Q52: A neutropenic patient is diagnosed with invasive aspergillosis. Which antifungal agent is considered the preferred first-line...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Fungal Infections✅ B
A) Fluconazole
B) Voriconazole
C) An echinocandin (e.g., caspofungin)
D) Amphotericin B lipid formulation
Explanation: Voriconazole is the preferred first-line therapy for invasive aspergillosis per IDSA guidelines.
Q53: A patient is diagnosed with onychomycosis of the toenails. Which oral therapy and duration are most appropriate for treatment...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Fungal Infections✅ B
A) Terbinafine for 6 weeks
B) Terbinafine for 12 weeks
C) Griseofulvin for 12 weeks
D) Itraconazole for 4 weeks
Explanation: Terbinafine for 12 weeks is the preferred treatment for toenail onychomycosis — more effective and better tolerated than alternatives.
Q54: Hospital-Acquired Pneumonia (HAP) is formally defined as pneumonia occurring at least how long after a patient’s admission to...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Respiratory Infections✅ B
A) 24 hours
B) 48 hours
C) 72 hours
D) 96 hours
Explanation: HAP is defined as pneumonia in a nonventilated patient that occurs 48 hours or more after admission and was not incubating at the time of admission.
Q55: Which finding is considered a Major Criterion in the IDSA/ATS criteria for defining Severe Community-Acquired Pneumonia (CAP)...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Respiratory Infections✅ D
A) Confusion/disorientation
B) Leukopenia (WBC < 4.0 x 10³ cells/mm³)
C) Respiratory rate > 30 breaths/min
D) Respiratory failure requiring mechanical ventilation
Explanation: The major criteria for severe CAP include septic shock with the need for vasopressors or respiratory failure requiring mechanical ventilation. Confusion, leukopenia, and respiratory rate > 30 breaths/min are considered minor criteria.
Q56: For Influenza treatment to resolve symptoms 1 to 1.5 days sooner, Neuraminidase inhibitors (e.g., Oseltamivir) must be initia...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Respiratory Infections✅ C
A) 12 hours
B) 36 hours
C) 48 hours
D) 72 hours
Explanation: Neuraminidase inhibitors must be initiated within 48 hours of symptom onset for treatment benefits.
Q57: Which first-line agent for uncomplicated cystitis is specifically noted as being ineffective if the patient's creatinine clea...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Urinary Tract Infections✅ D
A) Trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole
B) Fosfomycin tromethamine
C) Cephalexin
D) Nitrofurantoin monohydrate/macrocrystals
Explanation: Nitrofurantoin (monohydrate/macrocrystals) is ineffective in patients with a CrCl less than 30 mL/minute/1.73 m².
Q58: What is the minimum recommended duration of antibiotic therapy for acute bacterial prostatitis?
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Urinary Tract Infections✅ C
A) 7 days
B) 14 days
C) 2–4 weeks
D) 6 months
Explanation: The therapy duration for acute bacterial prostatitis is typically 2–4 weeks, and may be extended for an additional 2 weeks if the patient remains symptomatic.
Q59: Which physical finding is characteristic of Erysipelas, helping to differentiate it from cellulitis?
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Skin and Soft Tissue Infections✅ B
A) Involvement primarily of the deep dermis and subcutaneous fat
B) Edge of infection is elevated and sharply demarcated from the surrounding tissue
C) Warmth, pain, and non-elevated, poorly defined margins
D) Usually caused by a mixed infection with Gram-positive and Gram-negative organisms
Explanation: Erysipelas involves the superficial dermis and the edge of the infection is elevated and sharply demarcated from the surrounding tissue. Cellulitis involves the deep dermis/subcutaneous fat and has non-elevated, poorly defined margins.
Q60: If Group A Streptococcus or Clostridium spp. are suspected in a patient with necrotizing fasciitis, which agent should be inc...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Skin and Soft Tissue Infections✅ C
A) Meropenem
B) Ceftriaxone
C) Clindamycin or linezolid
D) Piperacillin/tazobactam
Explanation: If S. pyogenes (Group A streptococci) or Clostridium spp. are suspected, clindamycin or linezolid should be included in the empiric regimen to suppress toxin and cytokine production.
Q61: What is the recommended strategy for antibiotic treatment following the initial intravenous therapy phase for a prosthetic jo...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Tuberculosis✅ C
A) Intravenous vancomycin monotherapy for 6 weeks
B) Oral Nafcillin or Dicloxacillin monotherapy for 1 month
C) Rifampin combined with a fluoroquinolone for 3 to 6 months
D) Oral clindamycin plus doxycycline for 12 weeks
Explanation: For retained staphylococcal prosthetic joint infections, the pathogen-specific intravenous therapy plus rifampin for 2–6 weeks is followed by rifampin plus ciprofloxacin or levofloxacin for 3 months (hip, elbow, shoulder, ankle) or 6 months (retained knee prosthesis).
Q62: Which criterion is explicitly required for transitioning a patient with osteomyelitis from parenteral (IV) to oral antibiotic...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Osteomyelitis and Bone Infections✅ B
A) Patient must have been stable for at least 7 days on IV therapy
B) C-reactive protein must be less than 2.0 mg/dL
C) The oral antibiotic chosen must be a macrolide
D) The patient must have completed all necessary surgical debridement
Explanation: Criteria for effective oral therapy for osteomyelitis include a highly bioavailable antibiotic, documented adherence, adequate surgical debridement, resolving clinical course, and a C-reactive protein less than 2.0 mg/dL.
Q63: What unique antibiotic must be included in the empiric meningitis regimen for older adults (age 50 years and older) to ensure...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 CNS Infections✅ B
A) Daptomycin
B) Ampicillin
C) Cefepime
D) Meropenem
Explanation: For older adults (50 years and older), the empiric regimen is Ceftriaxone plus Vancomycin plus Ampicillin, where Ampicillin covers Listeria monocytogenes.
Q64: Adjunctive Dexamethasone therapy is recommended for adult patients with meningitis, particularly to improve outcomes if the m...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Respiratory Infections✅ C
A) N. meningitidis
B) L. monocytogenes
C) S. pneumoniae
D) H. influenzae
Explanation: Dexamethasone is recommended because it resulted in significantly improved outcomes, including decreased mortality, in adults receiving it for S. pneumoniae meningitis.
Q65: What is the recommended minimum duration of Nafcillin or Cefazolin therapy for native valve endocarditis caused by methicilli...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Endocarditis✅ C
A) 2 weeks
B) 4 weeks
C) 6 weeks
D) Indefinite suppression
Explanation: For native valve endocarditis caused by MSSA, the recommended length of therapy using Oxacillin, Nafcillin, or Cefazolin is 6 weeks.
Q66: What is the standard antibiotic regimen for endocarditis prophylaxis in an adult patient with a prosthetic heart valve who is...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Endocarditis✅ C
A) Clindamycin 300 mg orally 1 hour before procedure
B) Cephalexin 500 mg orally 1 hour before procedure
C) Amoxicillin 2 g orally 1 hour before procedure
D) Ampicillin 2 g intravenously 1 hour before procedure
Explanation: Patients with prosthetic valves require prophylaxis for dental procedures. The Standard general prophylaxis regimen is Amoxicillin 2 g, 1 hour before the procedure.
Q67: What is the recommended duration of antibiotic therapy for secondary peritonitis following adequate source control (e.g., sur...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Intra-abdominal and C. difficile Infections✅ C
A) Less than 24 hours
B) 7 days
C) 4 days
D) 14 days
Explanation: The recommended therapy duration for secondary peritonitis is 4 days following source control.
Q68: Which finding meets the criteria for defining a severe initial episode of Clostridioides difficile infection (CDI)?
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Intra-abdominal and C. difficile Infections✅ B
A) WBC less than 15 x 10³ cells/mm³
B) Serum Creatinine (SCr) greater than 1.5 mg/dL
C) Hypotension or shock (defining fulminant infection)
D) Albumin 2.8 g/dL (not listed as specific severity measure)
Explanation: Severe CDI is defined by leukocytosis with WBC 15×103 cells/mm³ or greater or SCr greater than 1.5 mg/dL.
Q69: For a surgical procedure using Cefazolin for prophylaxis, when must an additional intraoperative dose be administered?
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Surgical Prophylaxis✅ D
A) If the procedure lasts longer than 1 hour
B) If the initial dose was given more than 2 hours before incision
C) If the patient weighs more than 120 kg
D) If the surgery lasts longer than 4 hours or involves considerable blood loss
Explanation: The antibiotic must be redosed if the surgery lasts longer than 4 hours (or more than two drug half-lives) or involves considerable blood loss.
Q70: What is the goal of treatment (viral suppression) for an HIV-infected patient receiving combination ART?
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 HIV/AIDS✅ C
A) CD4 T-cell count greater than 200 cells/mm³
B) HIV RNA below 200 copies/mL
C) HIV RNA below the level of detection of the assay (typically < 20 copies/mL to 75 copies/mL)
D) A 50% decrease in HIV RNA from baseline
Explanation: Viral suppression, which is the goal of treatment, is defined as HIV RNA below the level of detection of the assay (HIV RNA less than 20 copies/mL to 75 copies/mL).
Q71: Which ART component is associated with a benign increase in serum creatinine due to the inhibition of creatinine secretion, b...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 HIV/AIDS✅ D
A) Tenofovir disoproxil fumarate (TDF)
B) Ritonavir
C) Efavirenz
D) Dolutegravir
Explanation: Dolutegravir and Elvitegravir/Cobicistat (INSTIs) cause benign increases in creatinine because they inhibit creatinine secretion.
Q72: Secondary prophylaxis (chronic maintenance therapy) for Cytomegalovirus (CMV) retinitis may be discontinued if the CMV lesion...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 HIV/AIDS✅ B
A) 1 month
B) 3–6 months
C) 1 year
D) Until viral load is undetectable
Explanation: Chronic maintenance therapy for CMV retinitis may be discontinued if the lesions are inactive, and the CD4+ count is greater than 100 cells/mm³ for 3–6 months in response to ART.
Q73: Which Rifamycin is substituted for Rifampin when treating active TB in a patient whose ART regimen includes a ritonavir-boost...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 HIV/AIDS✅ B
A) Rifapentine
B) Rifabutin
C) Rifalazil (not in sources)
D) Rifametaxime (not in sources)
Explanation: PIs should not be administered concurrently with rifampin. Rifabutin can be substituted for rifampin; if the PI is boosted with ritonavir, the rifabutin dose should be decreased to 150 mg every day.
Q74: What is the preferred prophylactic regimen for an HIV-infected patient requiring PJP primary prophylaxis (CD4 < 200 cells/mm³...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 HIV/AIDS✅ D
A) Trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole double strength daily (despite allergy)
B) Dapsone 100 mg weekly
C) Pentamidine 300 mg by nebulization once monthly
D) Atovaquone 1500 mg orally once daily
Explanation: Trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole is the preferred agent, but due to a sulfonamide allergy, the preferred alternative prophylactic regimen based on safety, tolerability, and efficacy is Atovaquone 1500 mg once daily.
Q75: What is the preferred first-line agent for the treatment of Aspergillosis?
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Fungal Infections✅ B
A) Micafungin
B) Voriconazole
C) Liposomal amphotericin B
D) Fluconazole
Explanation: Voriconazole is listed as the first therapeutic option for Aspergillosis.
Q76: Which medication is currently noted as the preferred first-line oral therapy for nonhospitalized, symptomatic patients diagno...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Respiratory Infections✅ D
A) Dexamethasone 6 mg orally daily
B) Remdesivir 200 mg once, then 100 mg daily
C) Molnupiravir 200 mg orally twice daily
D) Ritonavir-boosted nirmatrelvir (Paxlovid)
Explanation: Ritonavir-boosted nirmatrelvir (Paxlovid) is preferred first-line therapy for symptomatic, nonhospitalized patients with COVID-19 who are not receiving supplemental oxygen, as of January 2023.
Q77: A patient is diagnosed with bacterial sinusitis that is considered severe. What is the recommended duration of antibiotic the...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Respiratory Infections✅ B
A) 10–14 days
B) 5–7 days
C) 3 days
D) 21 days
Explanation: The therapy duration for acute bacterial sinusitis in adults is 5–7 days. Children require 10–14 days of therapy.
Q78: In a hospital unit where the prevalence of MRSA respiratory infections is low (less than 10%), what is the clinical implicati...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Respiratory Infections✅ C
A) The patient must still receive Vancomycin for the first 48 hours empirically.
B) The MRSA nasal screen is inconclusive and should be repeated immediately.
C) The negative screen suggests that empiric MRSA antibiotic coverage is unnecessary.
D) The patient should receive Linezolid, regardless of the nasal screen result.
Explanation: In locations where the prevalence of MRSA respiratory infections is low (< 10%), a negative MRSA nasal screen suggests that MRSA antibiotics are unnecessary empirically.
Q79: When comparing the symptoms of Influenza, COVID-19, and the Common Cold, which symptom is characteristic of Influenza, typica...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Respiratory Infections✅ D
A) Loss of taste/smell
B) Hacking cough
C) Sneezing
D) High fever (> 101°F [38°C])
Explanation: High temperature (> 101°F [38°C]) of 3–4 days’ duration is a characteristic symptom listed for Influenza.
Q80: Which molecular class of β-Lactamases is noted in the sources as currently having no effective inhibitors available?
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Antimicrobial Resistance✅ D
A) Class A (Serine β-lactamases, e.g., KPC)
B) Class C (Serine β-lactamases, e.g., AmpC)
C) Class D (Serine β-lactamases, e.g., OXA family)
D) Class B (Metallo β-lactamases, e.g., NDM-1)
Explanation: Metallo β-lactamases (Molecular Class B), which include the IMP and VIM families (CRE), are listed as having None under the "Effective Inhibitors" category.
Q81: For an HIV-infected patient receiving ART, a change in HIV RNA (viral load) is considered statistically significant if the ch...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 HIV/AIDS✅ B
A) 1.0 log (10-fold)
B) 0.5 log (3-fold)
C) 0.3 log (2-fold)
D) 10 copies/mL
Explanation: While on treatment, changes greater than 3-fold (about 0.5 log) in HIV RNA are considered statistically significant.
Q82: Which mechanism of antibiotic resistance is characteristic of glycopeptide resistance (e.g., vancomycin resistance) in Entero...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Antimicrobial Resistance✅ D
A) Decreased uptake (altered porins)
B) Enzyme modification and degradation (β-lactamases)
C) Increased excretion (efflux pumps)
D) Altered target site (e.g., vanA and vanB genes)
Explanation: Glycopeptides (vancomycin) resistance is caused by an Altered target site (e.g., vanA and vanB genes).
Q83: Which description best characterizes the CSF findings typically associated with Viral Meningitis, differentiating it from Bac...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 CNS Infections✅ B
A) Glucose less than 50 mg/dL and protein greater than 150 mg/dL.
B) 5–300 WBCs/mm³ (predominantly lymphocytes).
C) Lactic acid greater than 35 mg/dL.
D) Greater than 1000–5000 WBCs/mm³ (predominantly neutrophils).
Explanation: Viral Meningitis typically shows 5–300 WBCs/mm³ (predominantly lymphocytes). Options A, C, and D are characteristic of Bacterial Meningitis.
Q84: What is the recommended duration of antibiotic therapy for native valve endocarditis caused by Penicillin-susceptible oral st...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Endocarditis✅ B
A) 2 weeks
B) 4 weeks
C) 6 weeks
D) 7 days
Explanation: The length of therapy for Penicillin-susceptible oral streptococci native valve endocarditis using Penicillin G (PCN G) is 4 weeks.
Q85: What is the preferred initial antifungal regimen for a non-neutropenic patient with candidemia (bloodstream Candida infection...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Fungal Infections✅ C
A) Fluconazole 400 mg daily
B) Voriconazole
C) Echinocandin (caspofungin, anidulafungin, or micafungin)
D) Amphotericin B deoxycholate
Explanation: The preferred initial therapy for candidemia is an Echinocandin (caspofungin, anidulafungin, rezafungin, or micafungin). Fluconazole is only used if the organism is known or suspected to be susceptible.
Q86: Which intervention is mandatory to reduce the bone marrow–suppressive effects of pyrimethamine when treating Toxoplasmosis?
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 HIV/AIDS✅ B
A) Vitamin B12 (Cyanocobalamin)
B) Folinic acid (Leucovorin)
C) Folic acid (Folacin)
D) Pyridoxine (Vitamin B6)
Explanation: When treating toxoplasmosis, folinic acid (leucovorin) (10–25 mg/day) must be added to the regimen (Pyrimethamine plus Sulfadiazine) to reduce bone marrow–suppressive effects of pyrimethamine.
Q87: When initiating combination ART in an HIV-infected pregnant woman, which class of antibiotics is generally avoided during the...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 HIV/AIDS✅ D
A) Nucleoside Reverse Transcriptase Inhibitors (NRTIs)
B) Integrase Strand Transfer Inhibitors (INSTIs)
C) Protease Inhibitors (PIs)
D) Tetracyclines
Explanation: Although the source context is UTI treatment in pregnancy, it lists Tetracyclines (along with Fluoroquinolones and Aminoglycosides) as antibiotics to avoid during pregnancy. (Note: While ART is used to prevent perinatal transmission, tetracyclines are a general contraindication in pregnancy.)
Q88: Which combination ART agent causes a benign increase in serum creatinine by inhibiting creatinine secretion, but is not consi...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 HIV/AIDS✅ D
A) Tenofovir disoproxil fumarate (TDF)
B) Zidovudine (ZDV)
C) Ritonavir
D) Bictegravir
Explanation: Bictegravir (as well as Dolutegravir and Elvitegravir/Cobicistat) causes benign increases in creatinine because it inhibits creatinine secretion. TDF is associated with renal toxicity.
Q89: What is the primary goal established by the CDC regarding the prevention of perinatal HIV transmission?
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 HIV/AIDS✅ B
A) Ensuring all HIV-infected pregnant women receive Zidovudine.
B) Decreasing transmissions to less than 1% through suppressive combination ART.
C) Ensuring the infant receives Zidovudine for at least 6 weeks postpartum.
D) Initiating ART only if the maternal CD4 count is below 200 cells/mm³.
Explanation: The goal of the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention is to decrease transmissions to less than 1% through suppressive combination antiretroviral therapy (ART) administered throughout pregnancy.
Q90: What is the standard total duration of therapy recommended for pulmonary tuberculosis (TB) in a patient without HIV who has c...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 HIV/AIDS✅ C
A) 4 months
B) 6 months
C) 9 months
D) 12 months
Explanation: If a patient without HIV has pulmonary TB and the culture is positive after 2 months of TB treatment, the total duration should be extended to 9 months. (The standard duration is 6 months; this extension is triggered by the positive culture after the intensive phase).
Q91: Which of the following is an accepted alternative regimen for the treatment of latent TB infection (LTBI) in a patient who is...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 HIV/AIDS✅ C
A) Isoniazid monotherapy for 3 months
B) Rifampin 300 mg daily for 6 months
C) Rifampin 600 mg daily for 4 months
D) Rifapentine plus isoniazid daily for 6 months
Explanation: Recommended dosing regimens for LTBI in non-HIV infected patients include Rifampin 600 mg daily for 4 months.
Q92: Which characteristic differentiates Influenza (Flu) from the Common Cold, according to the sources?
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Respiratory Infections✅ C
A) Gradual onset of symptoms
B) Presence of a dry cough that can become severe
C) High fever (greater than 101°F [38°C]) lasting 3–4 days
D) Occasional headache and slight myalgia
Explanation: High temperature (greater than 101∘F [38∘C]) of 3–4 days’ duration is listed as characteristic of Influenza, while temperature is only occasional in the common cold.
Q93: Empiric treatment for hospitalized patients with non-severe Community-Acquired Pneumonia (CAP) should include a β-Lactam (suc...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Respiratory Infections✅ C
A) A non-respiratory fluoroquinolone (e.g., Ciprofloxacin)
B) An aminoglycoside (e.g., Gentamicin)
C) A macrolide (or doxycycline)
D) An antipseudomonal carbapenem (e.g., Meropenem)
Explanation: Empiric treatment of hospitalized patients with non-severe pneumonia includes a β-Lactam (ampicillin/sulbactam, ceftriaxone, or ceftaroline) plus a macrolide (or doxycycline).
Q94: In a hospital unit where the incidence of MRSA respiratory infections is greater than 20%, empiric therapy for Hospital-Acqui...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Respiratory Infections✅ B
A) Ceftazidime
B) Vancomycin or Linezolid
C) Aztreonam
D) Ciprofloxacin
Explanation: An antibiotic with activity against MRSA should be included if MRSA incidence in the hospital unit is greater than 20%. The options if MRSA activity is necessary are Vancomycin or Linezolid.
Q95: What is the recommended duration for the use of Rifampin in the regimen for treating Latent TB Infection (LTBI) in a patient ...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 HIV/AIDS✅ C
A) 2 months
B) 3 months
C) 4 months
D) 6–9 months
Explanation: For patients who are not infected with HIV, one recommended dosing regimen is Rifampin 600 mg daily for 4 months.
Q96: In bacterial meningitis, which of the following cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) lab results is characteristic of the infection?
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 CNS Infections✅ C
A) Protein less than 50 mg/dL
B) Glucose greater than 70 mg/dL
C) WBC greater than 1000 cells/mm³ (predominantly neutrophils)
D) Lactic acid less than 14 mg/dL
Explanation: Bacterial meningitis typically results in WBC greater than 1000–5000 cells/mm³ (predominantly neutrophils), protein greater than 150 mg/dL, glucose less than 50 mg/dL, and lactic acid greater than 35 mg/dL.
Q97: Which drug is used for chemoprophylaxis against N. meningitidis in adults who are identified as close contacts of an index ca...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Tuberculosis✅ C
A) Penicillin G
B) Doxycycline
C) Ciprofloxacin 500 mg orally, one dose
D) Rifampin 300 mg every 12 hours for four doses
Explanation: Ciprofloxacin 500 mg orally, one dose, is listed as a chemoprophylaxis option for adults who are close contacts of an N. meningitidis case. (Note: Rifampin dosing for adults is 600 mg every 12 hours, four doses.)
Q98: When treating a brain abscess that originated from trauma or neurosurgery, which combination of antibiotics is suggested for ...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Skin and Soft Tissue Infections✅ C
A) Metronidazole plus Ceftriaxone
B) Penicillin plus Metronidazole
C) Vancomycin plus Cefepime
D) Vancomycin plus Metronidazole plus Ceftriaxone
Explanation: Suggested empiric regimens for a brain abscess secondary to Trauma or Neurosurgery is Vancomycin plus Cefepime.
Q99: For a total knee replacement lasting longer than 4 hours, what is the required adjustment to the antibiotic prophylaxis regim...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Surgical Prophylaxis✅ C
A) Switch from Cefazolin to Vancomycin due to the longer duration.
B) Increase the initial dose of Cefazolin by 50%.
C) Redose the antibiotic intraoperatively.
D) Give antibiotics for 24 hours postoperatively instead of stopping at surgical closure.
Explanation: Prophylaxis principles require that the antibiotic must be redosed if the surgery lasts longer than 4 hours (or more than two half-lives of the antibiotic).
Q100: For necrotizing fasciitis caused by S. pyogenes (Group A Streptococcus), the definitive treatment includes high-dose intraven...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Skin and Soft Tissue Infections✅ B
A) Daptomycin
B) Clindamycin or linezolid
C) Imipenem
D) Cefazolin
Explanation: Streptococcal necrotizing fasciitis treatment is high-dose intravenous penicillin plus clindamycin or linezolid.
Q101: What is the recommended duration of therapy for prosthetic valve endocarditis caused by Penicillin-susceptible oral streptoco...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Endocarditis✅ C
A) 2 weeks
B) 4 weeks
C) 6 weeks
D) Indefinite suppression
Explanation: The length of therapy for prosthetic valve endocarditis caused by Penicillin-susceptible oral streptococci is 6 weeks.
Q102: Which opportunistic infection (OI) in an HIV patient with a low CD4 count (less than 50 cells/mm³) requires regular funduscop...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 HIV/AIDS✅ D
A) Pneumocystis jiroveci Pneumonia (PJP)
B) Mycobacterium avium Complex (MAC)
C) Cryptococcosis
D) Cytomegalovirus (CMV)
Explanation: Primary prophylaxis is not recommended for CMV retinitis; instead, in patients with CD4 counts less than 50 cells/mm³, regular funduscopic examinations are recommended.
Q103: When treating severe Histoplasmosis, what is the recommended duration for the initial induction phase using Amphotericin B li...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Fungal Infections✅ B
A) 3 days
B) At least 1–2 weeks
C) 4 weeks
D) 12 weeks
Explanation: For moderate to severe Histoplasmosis, the treatment involves Lipid formulation amphotericin B for at least 1–2 weeks followed by Itraconazole.
Q104: Which combination ART agent must be avoided in patients with a history of using long-acting cabotegravir (CAB-LA) for pre-exp...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 HIV/AIDS✅ B
A) Dolutegravir/lamivudine
B) Darunavir/ritonavir or darunavir/cobicistat plus tenofovir/emtricitabine
C) Bictegravir/tenofovir alafenamide/emtricitabine
D) Doravirine/tenofovir disoproxil fumarate/lamivudine
Explanation: The PI-based regimen Darunavir/ritonavir or darunavir/cobicistat plus tenofovir/emtricitabine is listed with the caution: "For people with HIV who do not have a history of using long-acting cabotegravir (CAB-LA) as pre-exposure prophylaxis (PrEP)".
Q105: What is the recommended treatment duration for uncomplicated cystitis using Trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole?
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Urinary Tract Infections✅ B
A) 1 day
B) 3 days
C) 5 days
D) 7–14 days
Explanation: Trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole (160 mg/800 mg twice daily) is recommended for 3 days for uncomplicated cystitis.
Q106: Which risk factor for postoperative wound infection requires only 102 microorganisms to cause infection, compared to the 105 ...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Skin and Soft Tissue Infections✅ C
A) Obesity
B) Diabetes
C) Presence of foreign body
D) Extremes of age
Explanation: A healthy person tolerates an inoculum of 105 microorganisms, but in the presence of a foreign body, only 102 are needed to cause infection.
Q107: Which molecular class of β-Lactamases, which includes IMP and VIM family enzymes, is currently noted in the sources as having...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Antimicrobial Resistance✅ B
A) Class A (e.g., KPC)
B) Class B (Metallo β-lactamases)
C) Class C (e.g., AmpC)
D) Class D (e.g., OXA family)
Explanation: Metallo β-lactamases (Molecular Class B), exemplified by the IMP and VIM family, are listed as having None under "Effective Inhibitors".
Q108: Which mechanism of resistance against β-Lactam antibiotics is mediated by the expression of the mecA gene in Staphylococcus s...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Antimicrobial Resistance✅ D
A) Enzyme modification and degradation (β-Lactamases)
B) Increased excretion (Efflux pumps)
C) Decreased uptake (Altered porins)
D) Altered target site (altered penicillin-binding proteins [PBPs])
Explanation: Resistance to β-Lactams through mechanisms like altered penicillin-binding proteins (PBPs) and the mecA gene is classified as an Altered target site resistance mechanism.
Q109: In a patient receiving combination ART, a change in HIV viral load is considered statistically significant if the change is g...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 HIV/AIDS✅ B
A) 1.0 log
B) 0.5 log
C) 0.2 log
D) 20 copies/mL
Explanation: While on treatment, changes greater than 3-fold (about 0.5 log) in HIV RNA are considered statistically significant.
Q110: What is the recommended duration of antibiotic therapy for uncomplicated pyelonephritis when treated with Levofloxacin (750 m...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Urinary Tract Infections✅ B
A) 3 days
B) 5 days
C) 7 days
D) 14 days
Explanation: Levofloxacin 750 mg orally daily for uncomplicated pyelonephritis is a 5-day duration. Ciprofloxacin requires 7 days, and trimethoprim/sulfamethoxazole requires 14 days.
Q111: What is the standard duration of therapy for acute bacterial prostatitis?
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Urinary Tract Infections✅ C
A) 7 days
B) 14 days
C) 2–4 weeks
D) 6–8 weeks
Explanation: Therapy duration for acute bacterial prostatitis is typically 2–4 weeks, which can be extended for an additional 2 weeks if the patient remains symptomatic.
Q112: Which condition is a contraindication for the use of Nitrofurantoin in the treatment of uncomplicated cystitis?
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Urinary Tract Infections✅ B
A) History of chronic liver failure
B) Creatinine clearance (CrCl) less than 30 mL/minute/1.73 m²
C) Pregnancy in the second trimester
D) Prior use of a β-Lactam antibiotic
Explanation: Nitrofurantoin monohydrate/macrocrystals is noted as ineffective in patients with a CrCl less than 30 mL/minute/1.73 m².
Q113: Which definitive treatment regimen is indicated for S.pneumoniae meningitis when the pathogen exhibits a Penicillin MIC of 2....
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Respiratory Infections✅ C
A) Penicillin G monotherapy
B) Ceftriaxone monotherapy
C) Vancomycin plus Ceftriaxone
D) Meropenem monotherapy
Explanation: If the Penicillin MIC is 2.0 mcg/mL or greater, the regimen is Vancomycin plus Ceftriaxone.
Q114: Which regimen is required for long-term oral suppression following the initial intravenous therapy for a prosthetic knee infe...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Tuberculosis✅ C
A) Fluoroquinolone monotherapy for 3 months
B) Rifampin monotherapy for 6 months
C) Rifampin plus a fluoroquinolone for 6 months
D) Pathogen-specific intravenous therapy for 12 weeks
Explanation: If a prosthetic joint is retained, the oral continuation phase is rifampin plus ciprofloxacin or levofloxacin for 3 months (hip, elbow, shoulder, ankle) or 6 months (retained knee prosthesis).
Q115: In the empiric treatment of a brain abscess secondary to Trauma or Neurosurgery, what is the core antibiotic combination?
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Skin and Soft Tissue Infections✅ B
A) Penicillin plus Metronidazole
B) Vancomycin plus Cefepime
C) Metronidazole plus Ceftriaxone
D) Vancomycin plus Ceftriaxone plus Metronidazole
Explanation: The suggested empiric regimen for a brain abscess secondary to Trauma or Neurosurgery is Vancomycin plus Cefepime.
Q116: What is the required prophylactic dose of Cefazolin for a patient weighing 135 kg undergoing total knee replacement?
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Infectious Disease General✅ B
A) 1 g
B) 3 g
C) 2 g
D) 4 g
Explanation: The appropriate prophylactic dosing for Cefazolin is 2 g, or 3 g for patients weighing 120 kg or more.
Q117: For a dental procedure involving gingival tissue in a patient with a previous history of bacterial endocarditis, which oral r...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Endocarditis✅ C
A) Cephalexin 2 g orally 1 hour before procedure
B) Azithromycin 500 mg orally 1 hour before procedure
C) Amoxicillin 2 g orally 1 hour before procedure
D) Amoxicillin 3 g orally 1 hour before procedure
Explanation: Standard general prophylaxis for dental procedures is Amoxicillin 2 g, 1 hour before the procedure.
Q118: When treating native valve endocarditis caused by methicillin-sensitive Staphylococcus aureus (MSSA), what is the mandatory l...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Endocarditis✅ C
A) 2 weeks
B) 4 weeks
C) 6 weeks
D) 12 weeks
Explanation: For MSSA native valve endocarditis, the length of therapy using Oxacillin, Nafcillin, or Cefazolin is 6 weeks.
Q119: Which agent is the preferred initial therapy for candidemia in a non-neutropenic patient, prior to known species identificati...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Fungal Infections✅ C
A) Fluconazole
B) Amphotericin B lipid formulation
C) Echinocandin (e.g., Caspofungin or Micafungin)
D) Voriconazole
Explanation: The preferred initial therapy for candidemia is an Echinocandin (caspofungin, anidulafungin, rezafungin, or micafungin).
Q120: What is the required prophylactic co-treatment when using Pyrimethamine to treat Toxoplasmosis, due to the need to reduce bon...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 HIV/AIDS✅ C
A) Pyridoxine (Vitamin B6)
B) Folic acid (Folacin)
C) Folinic acid (Leucovorin)
D) Vitamin B12 (Cyanocobalamin)
Explanation: You must Add folinic acid (leucovorin) 10–25 mg/day to reduce bone marrow–suppressive effects of pyrimethamine.
Q121: Which is the most appropriate minimum duration of therapy for moderate to severe Histoplasmosis?
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Fungal Infections✅ C
A) 7 days of IV therapy
B) 4 weeks of oral therapy
C) 1–2 weeks of IV therapy followed by oral therapy for 12 weeks total
D) 6–9 months of oral therapy
Explanation: Moderate to severe illness requires Lipid formulation amphotericin B for at least 1–2 weeks followed by itraconazole to complete 12 weeks of therapy.
Q122: Which antiretroviral agent causes a benign increase in serum creatinine by inhibiting creatinine secretion, but is not consid...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 HIV/AIDS✅ D
A) Tenofovir disoproxil fumarate (TDF)
B) Ritonavir
C) Efavirenz
D) Dolutegravir
Explanation: Dolutegravir (and other INSTIs like Bictegravir and Elvitegravir/Cobicistat) cause benign increases in creatinine because they inhibit creatinine secretion. TDF is associated with renal toxicity.
Q123: What is the required adjunct medication for all patients receiving Isoniazid for Tuberculosis (TB) treatment who are at incre...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 HIV/AIDS✅ C
A) Rifampin
B) Pyrazinamide
C) Pyridoxine
D) Ethambutol
Explanation: Pyridoxine should be given to all those receiving isoniazid and at increased risk of neuropathy (e.g., pregnant women, breastfeeding infants, HIV infected persons, patients with diabetes, alcoholism, malnutrition, or chronic renal failure, persons of advanced age).
Q124: Secondary prophylaxis (chronic maintenance therapy) for Mycobacterium avium Complex (MAC) can be discontinued if the patient ...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 HIV/AIDS✅ B
A) 50 cells/mm³
B) 100 cells/mm³
C) 200 cells/mm³
D) 500 cells/mm³
Explanation: Chronic maintenance therapy for MAC may be discontinued after 12 months of therapy if CD4 count is more than 100 cells/mm³ for 6 months or longer because of potent combination ART
Q125: A 24-year-old pregnant woman presents to the urgent care clinic with fever and urinary frequency and urgency. She is diagnose...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Urinary Tract Infections✅ D
A) Nitrofurantoin
B) Amoxicillin
C) Cephalexin
D) Doxycycline
Explanation: Doxycycline (a tetracycline) should be avoided due to the potential harm to the fetus. Nitrofurantoin, amoxicillin (a penicillin), and cephalexin (a cephalosporin) are generally considered safe.
Q126: Which of the following is the primary method of β-lactam resistance with Streptococcus pneumoniae?
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Respiratory Infections✅ A
A) Modification of target site
B) Decreased drug levels due to changes in permeability
C) Decreased drug levels due to an efflux pump
D) Enzymatic inactivation
Explanation: S. pneumoniae resistance to β-lactam antibiotics involves alteration in one or more of the major penicillin-binding proteins.
Q127: Which of the following agents is considered a narrow-spectrum antibiotic?
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Tuberculosis✅ C
A) Ceftriaxone
B) Ciprofloxacin
C) Isoniazid
D) Imipenem
Explanation: Isoniazid is only active against Mycobacterium tuberculosis, while ceftriaxone, ciprofloxacin, and imipenem are considered broad spectrum due to their activity against multiple types of bacteria and increased risk for contributing to the development of a superinfection.
Q128: Which of the following antibiotics exhibits concentration-dependent killing?
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Antimicrobials✅ D
A) Clindamycin
B) Linezolid
C) Vancomycin
D) Daptomycin
Explanation: Clindamycin, linezolid, and vancomycin exhibit time-dependent killing, while daptomycin works best when administered in a fashion that optimizes concentration-dependent killing.
Q129: Which of the following antibiotics exhibits a long postantibiotic effect that permits once-daily dosing?
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Antimicrobials✅ A
A) Gentamicin
B) Penicillin G
C) Vancomycin
D) Aztreonam
Explanation: Aminoglycosides, including gentamicin, possess a long postantibiotic effect, especially when given as a high dose every 24 hours. Penicillin G, clindamycin, and vancomycin have a relatively short postantibiotic effect and require dosing that maintains concentrations above the MIC for a longer portion of the dosing interval.
Q130: A 58-year-old man with a history of hepatitis C, cirrhosis, and ascites presents with spontaneous bacterial peritonitis. Whic...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Urinary Tract Infections✅ C
A) Penicillin G.
B) Tobramycin.
C) Erythromycin.
D) Vancomycin.
Explanation: Erythromycin is metabolized by the liver and should be used with caution in patients with hepatic impairment. Penicillin G, tobramycin, and vancomycin are primarily eliminated by the kidneys.
Q131: JS is a 3-day-old neonate, born at 37 weeks’ gestation, who presents with new onset fever, lethargy, and decreased desire to ...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Antimicrobials✅ D
A) Chloramphenicol
B) Sulfamethoxazole/trimethoprim
C) Tetracycline
D) Ampicillin
Explanation: Chloramphenicol and sulfonamides (sulfamethoxazole) can cause toxic effects in newborns due to poorly developed renal and hepatic elimination processes. Tetracycline can have effects on bone growth and development and should be avoided in newborns and young children. Ampicillin is safe and effective in this population.
Q132: When evaluating drug therapy for meningitis, which of the following factors is expected to have the LEAST influence on the pe...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 CNS Infections✅ B
A) Lipid solubility of the drug
B) Minimum inhibitory concentration of the drug
C) Protein binding of the drug
D) Molecular weight of the drug
Explanation: Although the minimum inhibitory concentration impacts the effectiveness of the drug against a given bacteria, it does not affect the ability of a drug to penetrate into the brain. Lipid solubility, protein binding, and molecular weight all determine the likelihood of a drug to penetrate the blood–brain barrier and concentrate in the brain.
Q133: A 72-year-old male presents with fever, cough, malaise, and shortness of breath. His chest x-ray shows bilateral infiltrates ...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Respiratory Infections✅ D
A) 1 g every 6 hours given over 30 minutes
B) 2 g every 12 hours given over 3 hours
C) 4 g every 24 hours given over 30 minutes
D) 4 g given as continuous infusion over 24 hours
Explanation: The clinical efficacy of cefepime is based on the percentage of time that the drug concentration remains above the MIC. A continuous infusion would allow for the greatest amount of time above the MIC compared to intermittent (30 minutes) and prolonged infusions (3 to 4 hours).
Q134: Which of the following adverse drug reactions precludes a patient from being rechallenged with that drug in the future?
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Intra-abdominal and C. difficile Infections✅ B
A) Itching/rash from penicillin
B) Stevens-Johnson syndrome from sulfamethoxazole–trimethoprim
C) Gastrointestinal (GI) upset from clarithromycin
D) Clostridium difficile superinfection from moxifloxacin
Explanation: Stevens-Johnson syndrome is a severe idiosyncratic reaction that can be life threatening, and these patients should never be rechallenged with the offending agent. Itching/rash is a commonly reported reaction in patients receiving penicillins but is not life threatening. A patient may be rechallenged if the benefits outweigh the risk (for example, pregnant patient with syphilis) or the patient could be exposed through a desensitization procedure. GI upset is a common side effect of clarithromycin but is not due to an allergic reaction. Moxifloxacin is a broad-spectrum antibiotic that can inhibit the normal flora of the GI tract, increasing the risk for the development of superinfections like C. difficile. This is not an allergic reaction, and the patient can be rechallenged; however, the patient might be at risk for developing C. difficile infection again.
Q135: A 45-year-old man presented to the hospital 3 days ago with severe cellulitis and a large abscess on his left leg. Incision a...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Skin and Soft Tissue Infections✅ C
A) Ertapenem
B) Ceftaroline
C) Daptomycin
D) Piperacillin/tazobactam
Explanation: Daptomycin is approved for skin and skin structure infections caused by MRSA and is given once daily. A and D are incorrect because they do not cover MRSA. Ceftaroline covers MRSA, but it must be given twice daily.
Q136: Which of the following adverse effects is associated with daptomycin?
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Antimicrobials✅ D
A) Ototoxicity
B) Red man syndrome
C) QTc prolongation
D) Rhabdomyolysis
Explanation: Ototoxicity and red man syndrome are associated with vancomycin. QTc prolongation is associated with telavancin. Myalgias and rhabdomyolysis have been reported with daptomycin therapy and require patient education and monitoring.
Q137: A 72-year-old man is admitted to the hospital from a nursing home with severe pneumonia. He was discharged from the hospital ...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Respiratory Infections✅ A
A) Vancomycin + cefepime + ciprofloxacin
B) Vancomycin + cefazolin + ciprofloxacin
C) Telavancin + cefepime + ciprofloxacin
D) Daptomycin + cefepime + ciprofloxacin
Explanation: Vancomycin provides adequate coverage against MRSA, and cefepime and ciprofloxacin provide adequate empiric coverage of Pseudomonas. B is incorrect because cefazolin does not have activity against Pseudomonas. C is incorrect because telavancin should be avoided if possible with drugs that prolong the QTc interval, in this case ciprofloxacin. Daptomycin is inactivated by pulmonary surfactant and should not be used for pneumonia.
Q138: A 23-year-old man presents with acute appendicitis that ruptures shortly after admission. He is taken to the operating room f...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Intra-abdominal and C. difficile Infections✅ B
A) Cefepime
B) Piperacillin/tazobactam
C) Aztreonam
D) Ceftaroline
Explanation: While all of these agents cover most strains of E. coli, piperacillin/tazobactam is the only drug on this list that provides coverage against Bacteroides species.
Q139: A 68-year-old man presents from a nursing home with fever, increased urinary frequency and urgency, and mental status changes...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Urinary Tract Infections✅ C
A) Cefepime
B) Ertapenem
C) Aztreonam
D) Ceftaroline
Explanation: Based on the severity of the allergic reaction, aztreonam is the choice of all the β-lactams. Although cross-reactivity with cephalosporins and carbapenems is low, the risk rarely outweighs the benefit in these cases.
Q140: A 25-year-old man presents to the urgent care center with a painless sore on his genitals that started 2 weeks ago. He report...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Sexually Transmitted Infections✅ A
A) Benzathine penicillin G
B) Ceftriaxone
C) Aztreonam
D) Vancomycin
Explanation: A single treatment with penicillin is curative for primary and secondary syphilis. No antibiotic resistance has been reported, and it remains the drug of choice unless the patient has a severe allergic reaction.
Q141: A 20-year-old woman presents to the emergency room with headache, stiff neck, and fever for 2 days and is diagnosed with meni...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Respiratory Infections✅ C
A) Cefazolin
B) Cefdinir
C) Cefotaxime
D) Cefuroxime axetil
Explanation: Cefotaxime is the only drug on this list with adequate CSF penetration to treat meningitis. Cefdinir and cefuroxime axetil are only available orally, and cefazolin’s CSF penetration and spectrum of coverage against S. pneumoniae are likely inadequate to treat meningitis.
Q142: Which of the following cephalosporins has activity against gram-negative anaerobic pathogens like Bacteroides fragilis?
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Antimicrobials✅ A
A) Cefoxitin
B) Cefepime
C) Ceftriaxone
D) Cefazolin
Explanation: The cephamycins (cefoxitin and cefotetan) are the only cephalosporins with in vitro activity against anaerobic gram-negative pathogens. Cefepime, ceftriaxone, and cefazolin have no appreciable activity against Bacteroides fragilis.
Q143: In which of the following cases would it be appropriate to use telavancin?
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Respiratory Infections✅ C
A) A 29-year-old pregnant woman with ventilator-associated pneumonia
B) A 76-year-old man with hospital-acquired pneumonia also receiving amiodarone for atrial fibrillation
C) A 36-year-old man with cellulitis and abscess growing MRSA
D) A 72-year-old woman with a diabetic foot infection growing MRSA who has moderate renal dysfunction
Explanation: A is not a good option due to the potential of telavancin harming the fetus. Option B is not a good choice because the patient is on amiodarone, and telavancin can cause QTc prolongation. Option D is not an appropriate choice because the patient has baseline renal dysfunction and telavancin should be avoided unless benefit outweighs the risk. Option C is the best choice since telavancin is approved for skin and skin structure infections, and the patient has no apparent contraindication.
Q144: An 18-year-old woman presents to the urgent care clinic with symptoms of a urinary tract infection. Cultures reveal Enterococ...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Urinary Tract Infections✅ D
A) Cephalexin
B) Vancomycin
C) Cefdinir
D) Amoxicillin
Explanation: Option A and C are incorrect because enterococci are inherently resistant to all cephalosporins. Option B is incorrect because oral vancomycin is not absorbed and would not reach the urinary tract in sufficient quantities to treat a urinary tract infection. Option D is the best choice, as amoxicillin is well absorbed orally and concentrates in the urine.
Q145: Which of the following describes the mechanism of action of tetracycline antibiotics?
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Antimicrobials✅ A
A) Bind the 30S subunit of the bacterial ribosome, preventing binding of tRNA to the mRNA–ribosome complex.
B) Bind the 30S ribosomal subunit, interfering with assembly of the functional ribosomal apparatus.
