E) Toxic epidermal necrolysis
Explanation: Toxic epidermal necrolysis. This patient’s physical findings along with history of sulfamethoxazole use are suggestive of Stevens–Johnson syndrome (SJS). These symptoms in a patient taking a drug with the sulfonamide moiety (also known as sulfa drugs) is worrisome because it may signal the beginning stages of toxic epidermal necrolysis. Toxic epidermal necrolysis (TEN) is characterized by ulcerations of the mucous membranes with blistering and sloughing of the skin. The mortality incidence for TEN is around 30%. (A) Anaphylaxis is the extreme form of a type I hypersensitivity reaction. It presents as shortness of breath from bronchoconstriction, possibly accompanied by shock from vasodilation. The clinical picture in this patient does not suggest this type of hypersensitivity reaction. (B) Her symptoms and history are suggestive of SJS, so the immediate concern is TEN. Sulfamethoxazole is not known to cause lactic acidosis. (C) Her symptoms and history are suggestive of SJS, so the immediate concern is TEN. Sulfamethoxazole is not known to cause rhabdomyolysis. (D) The appearance of a new rash in a patient taking a drug with the sulfonamide moiety is worrisome because it may signal the beginning stages of toxic epidermal necrolysis. Her new symptoms are not likely related to an infection.
Q509: A 38-year-old businessman is on a trip to Mexico when he develops diarrhea. He has to give a presentation in a few hours and ...
🫁 GIT📂 GI Motility✅ D
A) Anticholinergic
B) Broad-spectrum antibiotic
C) Gram-negative antibiotic
D) Inhibitor of myenteric plexus activity
E) Sympathetic nervous system stimulant
Explanation: Inhibitor of myenteric plexus activity. Diphenoxylate is an opioid-receptor agonist. It binds 12/14/12 11:05 PM Drugs Affecting Other Systems (Including GI and Pulmonary) to #-receptors on the myenteric plexus and, like other opioids, decreases myenteric plexus activity. By inhibiting the myenteric plexus, GI smooth muscle activity is decreased. Stool transit time is increased, and it is thought that this allows more time for water absorption from the feces to decrease diarrhea. (A) Atropine is an example of an anticholinergic that can be used to treat diarrhea. Diphenoxylate does not influence signaling with acetylcholine, although it is commonly administered with atropine that does. (B) Many antidiarrheals (including diphenoxylate) have no antibiotic activity. They simply increase transit time in the bowels to allow for more water reabsorption—any underlying infection will be left to run its course. Inhibiting diarrhea in a patient with an infection may actually lengthen the patient’s recovery time. (C) Many antidiarrheals (including diphenoxylate) have no antibiotic activity. They simply increase transit time in the bowels to allow for more water reabsorption—any underlying infection will be left to run its course. Inhibiting diarrhea in a patient with an infection may actually lengthen the patient’s recovery time. (E) Clonidine is an example of a sympathetic nervous system stimulant that can be used for diarrhea. Data are limited regarding its efficacy in treating diarrhea, and it is usually only used in refractory cases. Diphenoxylate does not treat diarrhea by modulating sympathetic nervous system activity.
Q510: A 26-year-old man presents to an urgent care clinic with a dry cough for the past week. The cough was associated with a fever...
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Pulmonary General✅ B
A) Butorphanol
B) Dextromethorphan
C) Diphenoxylate
D) Guaifenesin
E) Loperamide
Explanation: Dextromethorphan. Dextromethorpan is an opioid used for cough suppression. Dextromethorphan is an agonist of #-, &-, and '-receptors for analgesia, but it has a very low potency. It exhibits its cough suppressant properties by acting centrally on the cough center in the medulla. (A) Butorphanol is an opioid used most commonly for pain control, not cough suppression. (C) Diphenoxylate is an opioid used for diarrhea, not cough suppression. (D) Guaifenesin is an expectorant used for productive coughs, but it is not an opioid. (E) Loperamide is an opioid used for diarrhea, not cough suppression.
Q511: A 72-year-old woman presents to her primary care physician with vision loss over the past year. She has noticed painless loss...
🧠 Neurology📂 Neurology✅ C
A) Acetazolamide
B) Epinephrine
C) Latanoprost
D) Pilocarpine
E) Physostigmine
Explanation: Latanoprost. Latanoprost is a prostaglandin F2! analog that increases the outflow of aqueous humor from the eye. A side effect of latanoprost is causing darkening of the iris, which the patient exhibits with her blue eyes turning brown. (A) Acetazolamide can cause blurry vision, but not darkening of the iris. (B) Epinephrine can cause mydriasis or burn when placed in the eye, but not darkening of the iris. (D) Pilocarpine can cause miosis, but not darkening of the iris. (E) Physostigmine can cause miosis, but not darkening of the iris.
Q512: A 34-year-old man with a seizure disorder presents to the emergency department with severe left-sided flank pain. The pain be...
🧠 Neurology📂 Pain Management✅ E
A) Acetaminophen
B) Butorphanol
C) Fentanyl
D) Morphine
E) Tramadol
Explanation: Tramadol. The patient has a history of seizures, and therefore should avoid tramadol. Tramadol decreases the seizure threshold. In patients 197 without a seizure history, it is very rare for tramadol to cause a seizure. (A) Acetaminophen does not lower the seizure threshold. (B) Butorphanol should not be used in with other opioids because it can lead to withdrawal symptoms, but it does not lower the seizure threshold. (C) Fentanyl can lead to respiratory depression at high doses but does not lower the seizure threshold. (D) Morphine can lead to respiratory depression at high doses but does not lower the seizure threshold.
Q513: A 37-year-old man complains of chest pain after meals. He says this pain has gone on for a few months, and he gets only minim...
🫁 GIT📂 Peptic Ulcer✅ B
A) Atropine
B) Cimetidine
C) Misoprostol
D) Octreotide
E) Omeprazole
Explanation: Cimetidine. There are many drugs that decrease the amount of acid in the stomach to reduce symptoms of heartburn. One of the stimuli for acid secretion is histamine released from ECL cells binding to H2 receptors on parietal cells. This binding activates the “proton pumps,” which are ATPases on the lumenal surface that force hydrogen ions into the lumen. Cimetidine slows acid production by blocking H2 receptors. (A) Atropine decreases gastric acid secretion by blocking acetylcholine signaling from the vagus nerve to parietal cells. It is not commonly used to treat GERD because of its systemic anticholinergic side effects. (C) Misoprostol is a prostaglandin analog. Prostaglandins inhibit acid secretion by binding Gi receptors, which lead to a decrease in intracellular cAMP and in turn to decreased H$/K$ ATPase activity. It does not interfere with histamine signaling. (D) Octreotide is a somatostatin analog. Although it would decrease gastric acid production, it is not used to treat GERD because of its systemic side effects. (E) Omeprazole decreases the amount of acid secreted by binding to and inhibiting the ATPase on the lumenal surface of parietal cells responsible for pumping hydrogen ions into the lumen. It does not interfere with histamine signaling.
Q514: An 8-year-old boy is brought to the pediatrician because of his continued bedwetting despite many treatment attempts. He has ...
🧠 Neurology📂 Psychiatry✅ B
A) Citalopram
B) Imipramine
C) Mirtazapine
D) Trazodone
E) Venlafaxine
Explanation: Imipramine. Imipramine is a tricyclic antidepressant with antimuscarinic properties. This leads to urinary retention and is why it is used in the treatment of enuresis. Usually, bedwetting alarms and desmopressin are tried before imipramine. (A) Citalopram is a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor that is not used for enuresis. (C) Mirtazapine is an atypical antidepressant that can be used in anorexics for weight gain, but not enuresis. (D) Trazodone is an atypical antidepressant that can be used for insomnia, but not for enuresis. (E) Venlafaxine is a serotoninnorepinephrine reuptake inhibitor that is not used for enuresis.
Q515: A 7-year-old boy is brought to the ambulatory care clinic by his mother because of acting out in class and at home. In class,...
🧠 Neurology📂 Psychiatry✅ D
A) Clomipramine
B) Fluoxetine
C) Lithium
D) Methylphenidate
E) Venlafaxine
Explanation: Methylphenidate. This boy has ADHD and the first-line treatment is methylphenidate. Methylphenidate increases the presynaptic release of norepinephrine, which increases the activity in the central nervous system. (A) Clomipramine is a tricyclic antidepressant that can also be used for 12/14/12 11:05 PM obsessive-compulsive disorder, not ADHD. (B) Fluoxetine is a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor used for major depression, not ADHD. (C) Lithium is a mood stabilizer used in the treatment of bipolar disorder, not ADHD. (E) Venlafaxine is a serotoninnorepinephrine reuptake inhibitor that is also used for anxiety, not ADHD.
Q516: A 68-year-old man with congestive heart failure presents to the emergency department with shortness of breath. He has $2 pitt...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ E
A) Collecting tubule
B) Descending loop of Henle
C) Distal convoluted tubule
D) Proximal convoluted tubule
E) Thick ascending loop of Henle
Explanation: Thick ascending loop of Henle. Ethacrynic acid acts on the Na$/K$/2Cl% cotransport, like furosemide, in the thick ascending loop of Henle. Ethacrynic acid is used in fluid overloaded states such as congestive heart failure, nephrotic syndrome, and pulmonary edema. (A) K$ sparing diuretics, such as spironolactone, act on the collecting tubule. (B) Mannitol, an osmotic diuretic, acts on the descending loop of Henle and the proximal convoluted tubule. (C) Hydrochlorothiazide acts on the distal convoluted tubule. (D) Acetazolamide, a carbonic anhydrase inhibitor, acts on the proximal convoluted tubule.
Q517: A 39-year-old woman with chronic migraine headaches manages her condition with ibuprofen 200 mg. Most days, she does well wit...
🧠 Neurology📂 Pain Management✅ A
A) Gastrointestinal upset
B) Hives
C) Seizure disorder
D) Teratogenicity
E) Throat tightness
Explanation: Gastrointestinal upset. The most common side effect of ibuprofen is gastrointestinal upset. Bleeding and rash are also common. Patients must be aware of these adverse effects. (B) Rash is more common than hives in patients taking ibuprofen. (C) Seizure disorder is uncommon in patients taking ibuprofen. (D) Teratogenicity is unlikely in patients taking ibuprofen. (E) Throat tightness is unexpected in patients taking ibuprofen.
Q518: A 48-year-old woman with a history of chronic migraine headaches has failed therapy with conservative measures and ibuprofen....
🧠 Neurology📂 Pain Management✅ A
A) Cold extremities
B) Diarrhea
C) Improved muscle strength
D) Skin dryness
E) Tachycardia
Explanation: Cold extremities. Ergot alkaloids are associated with vasoconstrictive complications. Patients may experience ischemia, cold extremities, and vasospasm. In addition, retroperitoneal fibrosis and cardiac valve fibrosis can also develop. (B) Nausea and vomiting are side effects of ergot alkaloids. (C) Muscle weakness would be expected with an ergot alkaloid. (D) Skin coldness would be expected with an ergot alkaloid. (E) Bradycardia is a side effect of ergot alkaloids.
Q519: A 54-year-old woman with a history of migraine headaches has begun on therapy with Topamax as prophylaxis. She is poorly comm...
🧠 Neurology📂 Pain Management✅ B
A) Depression
B) Kidney stones
C) Nearsightedness
D) Pulmonary deficits
E) Tinnitus
Explanation: Kidney stones. Topamax can cause dehydration. In a person with a history of kidney stones, this may increase the incidence of stone formation. Further, this medication should not be given to patients with acute myopia and glaucoma. (A) Topamax should not be given to patients with suicidal ideation. (C) Topamax should not be given to patients with acute myopia and glaucoma. (D) Topamax should not be given to patients with hepatic or renal involvement. (E) Topamax can be given to patients with tinnitus.
Q520: An 11-year-old boy with tonsillar hypertrophy is going to undergo elective tonsillectomy. He will be intubated for the proced...
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Pulmonary General✅ A
A) Bradycardia
B) Erythema
C) Hypertension
D) Temperature change
E) Tinnitus
Explanation: Bradycardia. Adverse effects associated with lidocaine jelly include dizziness, visual disturbances, tremor, bradycardia, and hypotension. (B) Erythema is a side effect of topical lidocaine. (C) Hypotension and bradycardia would be expected with this agent. (D) Temperature change would not be expected with lidocaine jelly. (E) Tinnitus would not be expected with lidocaine jelly.
Q521: A 22-year-old woman recently began medical treatment for a seizure disorder. She presents with dysuria; and a urine dipstick ...
🧠 Neurology📂 Epilepsy✅ D
A) Ethosuximide
B) Phenobarbital
C) Phenytoin
D) Topiramate
E) Valproic acid
Explanation: Topiramate. This patient’s presentation is consistent with nephrolithiasis or kidney stones. This can be attributed to her seizure medication because she has no prior history of stones. Of the seizure medications listed, only topiramate is known to cause nephrolithiasis. Topiramate is used in the treatment of partial seizures and generalized tonic-clonic seizures as well as for migraine prophylaxis. Its exact mechanism is unknown but appears to block seizure spread rather than raise seizure threshold. (A) Ethosuximide is an anticonvulsant used to treat absence seizures. It is not known to cause nephrolithiasis. (B) Phenobarbital is a CNS depressant used to treat many seizure types. Its major side effects are caused by its CNS depression. It is not known to cause nephrolithiasis. (C) Phenytoin is an anticonvulsant used to treat many types of seizures. One of its more unique side effects is gingival hyperplasia. It is not known to cause nephrolithiasis. (E) Valproic acid is an anticonvulsant used to treat multiple seizure types as well as for migraine prophylaxis. It is not known to cause nephrolithiasis.
Q522: A 54-year-old man with hypertension, alcoholic cirrhosis, and HIV disease is hospitalized for abdominal pain, fatigue, and we...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Heart Failure✅ E
A) Acetazolamide
B) Chlorthalidone
C) Furosemide
D) Hydrochlorothiazide
E) Spironolactone
Explanation: Spironolactone. Spironolactone is very effective in the treatment of hepatic edema. These patients are frequently resistant to the diuretic action of loop diuretics, although a combination with spironolactone may be beneficial. (A) Acetazolamide would be helpful in treating hypertension but not the ascites and fluid overload. (B) Chlorthalidone would treat hypertension but not markedly improve the edema. (C) Furosemide may improve peripheral fluid edema but have limited success with central ascites fluid removal. (D) Hydrochlorothiazide would be helpful in management of outpatient hypertension without significant peripheral edema.
Q523: A 59-year-old man with a history of recurrent bilateral kidney stones is placed on a medication to decrease calcium excretion...
❤️ Cardiovascular📂 Hypertension✅ B
A) Furosemide
B) Hydrochlorothiazide
C) Spironolactone
D) Triamterene
E) This is not a medication effect
Explanation: Hydrochlorothiazide. Hydrochlorothi azide is effective in increasing calcium reabsorption, thus decreasing the amount of calcium excreted and decreasing the formation of kidney stones that contain calcium phosphate or calcium oxalate. However, hydrochlorothiazide can also inhibit the excretion of uric acid and cause its accumulation, leading to an attack of gout in some individuals. (A) Furosemide increases the excretion of calcium and can cause an increase in kidney stone formation. (C) Spironolactone neither cause an increase in excretion of urine calcium nor cause gout. (D) Triamterene is a potassiumsparing diuretic that does not cause hypercalciuria. (E) This is a medication side effect that is important for physicians to be aware of. 12/14/12 11:05 PM Drugs Affecting Other Systems (Including GI and Pulmonary)
Q524: A 24-year-old G1P1 woman presents to the emergency department days after giving birth to her first child. She lost a large am...
🫁 GIT📂 GI Motility✅ A
A) Constipation
B) Diarrhea
C) Hypercoagulability
D) Hypotension
E) Seizures Glomerular filtrate 4 1 3 2 5
Explanation: Constipation. Ferrous sulfate is one of the iron salts used to provide dietary iron to patients. It is often prescribed for patients who are anemic but may also be used simply for iron supplementation to prevent anemia and other complications of low iron, as in this case. Most iron in the body is found in the hemoglobin of red cells, but many enzymes (e.g., those found in mitochondria) also require iron to function. One of the most common side effects of iron therapy is constipation, and iron supplements may need to be given with a stool softener to counteract this effect. (B) Constipation is a much more common side effect than diarrhea with iron supplementation. Diarrhea may result when the iron supplementation causes significant GI irritation. (C) The estrogen found in many oral contraceptives increases the risk of deep vein thrombosis (DVTs). This is not a side effect of ferrous sulfate. (D) Ferrous sulfate does not generally cause vascular instability of any kind. Hypotension may be caused by many antihypertensive drugs. (E) Ferrous sulfate is not known to cause seizures. Of the side effects listed, constipation is by far the most common.
Q525: A 44-year-old man with multiple medical problems is placed on a carbonic anhydrase inhibitor which inhibits bicarbonate reabs...
🫁 GIT📂 Liver Disease✅ A
A) Location 1
B) Location 2
C) Location 3
D) Location 4
E) Location 5
Explanation: Location 1. Location 1 is the proximal convoluted tubule. In this area, acetazolamide acts. This agent is a carbonic anhydrase inhibitor that inhibits bicarbonate reabsorption and acts as a weak diuretic. (B) Location 2 is the descending loop of Henle. (C) Location 3 is the ascending loop of Henle. Loop diuretics such as furosemide act here. (D) Location 4 is the distal convoluted tubule. Thiazide diuretics act here. (E) Location 5 is the collecting duct, and potassium-sparing diuretics act here.
Q526: A 46-year-old woman is undergoing a laparoscopic supracervical hysterectomy for menometrorrhagia secondary to extensive leiom...
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Pulmonary General✅ C
A) Stage I
B) Stage II
C) Stage III
D) Stage IV
E) Stage V
Explanation: Stage III. Anesthesia is divided into stages and planes to more accurately measure the depth of a patient’s anesthesia. Stage I is characterized by analgesia and amnesia. Stage II is characterized by a loss of consciousness. Stage III is characterized by muscle relaxation, a regular breathing pattern, and loss of the eyelash and corneal reflexes. Stage III is also known as surgical anesthesia and is further subdivided into four planes. Stage IV is characterized by medullary depression and is the overdose stage. There is no stage higher than IV. (A) Stage I is characterized by analgesia and amnesia. The eyelash reflex (blinking of the eyelid when the eyelash is touched) is still present in this stage. (B) Stage II is characterized by loss of consciousness. The eyelash reflex (blinking of the eyelid when the eyelash is touched) is still present in this stage. (D) Stage IV is characterized by medullary depression and is the overdose stage. There is no eyelid reflex but breathing is severely impaired and irregular. (E) There is no stage of anesthesia higher than IV. Stage III is characterized by muscle relaxation, a regular breathing pattern, and loss of the eyelash and corneal reflexes. 199
Q527: A 34-year-old man presents to the emergency department with fever and right upper quadrant pain for the past 8 days since ret...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Infectious Disease General✅ C
A) Diethylcarbamazine
B) Ivermectin
C) Praziquantel
D) Pyrantel pamoate
E) Sodium stibogluconate
Explanation: Praziquantel. This patient ingested raw fish and contracted Clonorchis sinensis. C. sinensis affects the biliary system and can cause cholangitis, which this patient displays. The treatment of choice is praziquantel, which is thought to paralyze the helminth by increasing the cell membranes permeability to calcium. (A) Diethylcarbamazine is used to treat parasitic infections, such as Toxocara, but not Clonorchis. (B) Ivermectin is used to treat parasitic infections, such as Onchocerca, but not Clonorchis. (D) Pyrantel pamoate is used to treat parasitic infections, such as Enterobius and Ascaris, but not Clonorchis. (E) Sodium stibogluconate is used to treat parasitic infections, such as Taenia and Diphyllobothrium, but not Clonorchis.
Q528: A 32-year-old man who traveled to Brazil 1 week ago presents to the emergency department with swelling of his right eye. He h...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Infectious Disease General✅ C
A) Chloroquine
B) Melarsoprol
C) Nifurtimox
D) Sodium stibogluconate
E) Suramin
Explanation: Nifurtimox. The patient is suffering from an acute infection with Trypanosoma cruzi, which causes Chagas disease, and the treatment of choice is nifurtimox. It can be used in the acute stage of the infection but not in the chronic stage when the characteristic dilated cardiomyopathy and megacolon occur. (A) Chloroquine is the treatment of choice for malaria, but resistance has been increasing. (B) Melarsoprol is the treatment of choice for central nervous system invasion by Trypanosoma gambiense, but not T. cruzi. (D) Sodium stibogluconate is the treatment of choice for Clonorchis sinensis. (E) Suramin is the treatment of choice for blood-borne T. gambiense, but not T. cruzi.
Q529: A 42-year-old woman presents to the emergency department with fevers and headaches for the past 3 days. The fevers are off an...
🦠 Infectious Disease📂 Infectious Disease General✅ D
A) Chloroquine
B) Clindamycin
C) Metronidazole
D) Primaquine
E) Sodium stibogluconate
Explanation: Primaquine. Primaquine is added to the regimen for treatment of Plasmodium vivax and Plasmodium ovale to eradicate the dormant stages in the liver. Without primaquine, once the blood stream infection is cleared, it is likely that recurrence will still occur. However, primaquine only will not cure malaria, so both primaquine and quinine is usually prescribed. (A) Chloroquine is used in the treatment of Plasmodium vivax to clear the blood stream infection, but it will not affect the hypnozoites in the liver. (B) Clindamycin is used in the treatment of Babesia, but not Plasmodium. (C) Metronidazole is used to treat Trichomonas and Giardia, but not Plasmodium. (E) Sodium stibogluconate is the treatment of choice for Clonorchis sinensis.
Q530: A 33-year-old woman patient needs to be anesthetized before a dilation and curettage for abnormal uterine bleeding. Her anest...
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Pulmonary General✅ D
A) Enflurane
B) Diethyl ether
C) Halothane
D) Nitrous oxide
E) Propofol
Explanation: Nitrous oxide. One of the first anesthetic agents was inhaled diethyl ether, often called simple ether. One major drawback to ether was its flammability, which would be especially dangerous in modern operating rooms equipped with electrosurgical devices. Similar molecules were subsequently found and developed in which carbons are halogenated, which decreases flammability (such as halothane and enflurane). Another early inhaled anesthetic that may still be used is nitrous oxide. Nitrous oxide is not a halogenated hydrocarbon and is not flammable (although it is an oxidizer). It is often used in 12/14/12 11:05 PM combination with other anesthetics to increase their efficacy. (A) Enflurane is an inhaled anesthetic containing halogenated carbons. It produces rapid anesthesia and allows quick recovery. (B) Diethyl ether was one of the first anesthetic agents. It was inhaled, but a major drawback was its flammability. (C) Halothane is an inhaled anesthetic containing halogenated carbons. In rare instances, it may result in hepatotoxicity. (E) Propofol is a drug given IV and produces hypnosis, but not analgesia. Its onset is rapid and may cause pain at the injection site.
Q531: An 18-year-old man is brought to the emergency department by a friend after smoking crack cocaine because he was “acting funn...
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Pulmonary General✅ D
A) Increase norepinephrine synthesis
B) Inhibition of catechol-O-methyltransferase (COMT)
C) Inhibition of monoamine oxidase (MAO)
D) Inhibition of norepinephrine reuptake
E) Stimulation of nicotinic receptors in postganglionic autonomic neurons
Explanation: Inhibition of norepinephrine reuptake. Cocaine’s peripheral effects are mediated by norepinephrine. Once released, norepinephrine binds to postsynaptic receptors. To halt signaling, norepinephrine is taken up into the presynaptic cell or diffuses away from the synapse to be degraded by COMT and MAO. Cocaine inhibits the reuptake of norepinephrine into the nerve terminals and causes some degree of norepinephrine release. (A) Cocaine does not increase synthesis of norepinephrine. (B) Cocaine does not inhibit COMT. (C) Cocaine does not inhibit MAO. (E) Nicotine stimulates the nicotinic acetylcholine receptors found in autonomic ganglia. Cocaine is not known to interact with acetylcholine receptors.
Q532: The potency of inhaled anesthetics is defined as the minimum alveolar concentration. Five anesthetic gases are infused into 2...
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Pulmonary General✅ E
A) Gas A MAC 0.75%
B) Gas B MAC 1.2%
C) Gas C MAC 2%
D) Gas D MAC 6%
E) Gas E MAC 105% 177
Explanation: Gas E MAC 105%. The potency of inhaled anesthetics is defined quantitatively as the minimum alveolar concentration (MAC). This is the end-tidal concentration of anesthetic gas needed to eliminate movement among 50% of patients challenged by a standardized skin incision. (Note: MAC is the median effective dose [ED50] of the anesthetic.) MAC is usually expressed as the percentage of gas in a mixture required to achieve the effect. Gas E is nitrous oxide. (A) Gas A is halothane. (B) Gas B is isoflurane. (C) Gas C is sevoflurane. (D) Gas D is desflurane.
Q533: The replacement of the normal lung gases with the inspired anesthetic mixture defines which of the following terms?
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Pulmonary General✅ B
A) Anesthetic uptake
B) Alveolar wash-in
C) Minimum alveolar concentration
D) Solubility
Explanation: Alveolar wash-in. This term refers to the replacement of the normal lung gases with the inspired anesthetic mixture. The time required for this process is directly proportional to the functional residual capacity of the lung (the volume of gas remaining in the lungs at the end of a normal expiration) and inversely proportional to the ventilatory rate. It is independent of the physical properties of the gas. As the partial pressure builds within the lung, anesthetic transfer from the lung begins. (A) Anesthetic uptake is the product of gas solubility in the blood, cardiac output, and the anesthetic gradient between alveolar and blood partial pressure gradients. (C) The potency of inhaled anesthetics is defined quantitatively as the minimum alveolar concentration (MAC). (D) Solubility is determined by a physical property of the anesthetic molecule called the blood/gas partition coefficient.
Q534: A 44-year-old man with depression is currently being managed with paroxetine. He presents to his primary care physician compl...
🧠 Neurology📂 Psychiatry✅ E
A) Continue medication at current dose
B) Dose frequency of medication at every other day
C) Referral for outpatient psychotherapy
D) Referral for outpatient sexual therapy
E) Stop paroxetine and begin bupropion
Explanation: Stop paroxetine and begin bupropion. Loss of libido, delayed ejaculation, and anorgasmia are underreported side effects often noted by clinicians, but these are not prominently featured in the list of standard side effects. One option for managing SSRI-induced sexual dysfunction is to replace the offending antidepressant with an antidepressant having fewer sexual side effects such as bupropion or mirtazapine. This would be the best management for this patient. (A) This patient will continue to have sexual dysfunction if the medication is continued. (B) Changing dosing to every other day will reduce therapeutic effect of this medication. (C) It would be easier to change the medication than to begin a course of psychotherapy. (D) It would be easier to change the medication than to begin a course of sexual therapy.
Q535: A 27-year-old man was prescribed with an antidepressant for his insomnia. He now presents to the emergency department with pr...
🧠 Neurology📂 Psychiatry✅ E
A) Bupropion
B) Duloxetine
C) Imipramine
D) Sertraline
E) Trazodone
Explanation: Trazodone. Although many antidepressants have been reported to cause priapism, trazodone is perhaps the antidepressant best known for it. Trazodone works primarily by inhibiting serotonin reuptake. Postural hypotension and decreased libido are other possible side effects of trazodone therapy. Trazodone has also been used to decrease alcohol cravings. (A) Bupropion is an antidepressant also used for smoking cessation. It is not known to cause priapism. (B) Duloxetine inhibits serotonin and norepinephrine reuptake. It is not known to cause priapism. (C) Imipramine is a tricyclic antidepressant. It can also be used in some cases of enuresis. It is not known to cause priapism. (D) Sertraline is a selective serotonin reuptake inhibitor. It is not commonly associated with priapism.
Q536: A 73-year-old man presents to the primary care clinic with increasing shortness of breath. He has had shortness of breath for...
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Pulmonary Hypertension✅ B
A) Albuterol
B) Bosentan
C) Guaifenesin
D) N-acetylcysteine
E) Theophylline
Explanation: Bosentan. Bosentan inhibits endothelin-1 receptors and is used in the treatment of pulmonary hypertension. The inhibition of endothelin-1 receptors leads to pulmonary vasodilation, which decreases vascular resistance. (A) Albuterol would be more appropriate to treat asthma, not pulmonary hypertension. (C) Guaifenesin is an expectorant used to treat a productive cough, not pulmonary hypertension. (D) N-acetylcysteine is a mucolytic used to treat a productive cough, not pulmonary hypertension. (E) Theophylline is used to treat asthma, not pulmonary hypertension. 12/14/12 11:05 PM
Q537: A 45-year-old patient with Step 4 asthma is currently using medium-dose ICS-LABA (non-formoterol) plus SABA as needed. They h...
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Asthma✅ B
A) Increase to high-dose maintenance ICS-LABA + SABA.
B) Switch to medium-dose ICS-formoterol maintenance and reliever therapy (MART).
C) Add a LAMA to the current regimen.
D) Add low-dose maintenance OCS.
Explanation: GINA 2025 Track 1 is preferred because MART significantly reduces the risk of severe exacerbations and OCS exposure compared to SABA-based tracks.
Q538: Which biomarker profile is most predictive of a 'good asthma response' to the anti-TSLP biologic tezepelumab?
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Asthma✅ B
A) Low blood eosinophils and low FeNO.
B) High blood eosinophils and high FeNO.
C) Positive skin prick test and low IgE.
D) Elevated sputum neutrophils.
Explanation: While tezepelumab is effective across various phenotypes, the magnitude of exacerbation reduction is highest in those with elevated Type 2 biomarkers (eosinophils and FeNO).
Q539: A patient with severe asthma is starting dupilumab. Which of the following is a specific laboratory monitoring requirement or...
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Asthma✅ B
A) Screen for tuberculosis before initiation.
B) Monitor for transient blood eosinophilia.
C) Check serum IgE levels to adjust the dose monthly.
D) Monitor for neutralizing antibodies every 6 months.
Explanation: Dupilumab can cause transient increases in blood eosinophils (4-13% of patients).
Q540: For a patient using budesonide-formoterol 200/6 mcg (metered dose) as MART, what is the absolute maximum number of inhalation...
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Asthma✅ C
A) 6 inhalations.
B) 8 inhalations.
C) 12 inhalations.
D) 18 inhalations.
Explanation: The maximum total formoterol dose is 72 mcg metered (54 mcg delivered), which equals 12 inhalations of this specific formulation.
Q541: A 68-year-old smoker with COPD and chronic bronchitis has an eGFR of 45 and a blood eosinophil count of 350 cells/µL. They ha...
🫧 Pulmonary📂 COPD✅ C
A) LAMA monotherapy.
B) LABA + LAMA.
C) LABA + LAMA + ICS.
D) LAMA + low-dose Azithromycin.
Explanation: GOLD 2025/GINA guidelines recommend triple therapy (LABA+LAMA+ICS) for patients with multiple exacerbations, hospitalizations, or blood eosinophils ≥ 300 cells/µL.
Q542: Why should high-dose ICS be avoided in patients with COPD features?
🫧 Pulmonary📂 COPD✅ B
A) Risk of severe hyperkalemia.
B) Increased risk of pneumonia.
C) It causes rapid lung function decline.
D) It masks the symptoms of lung cancer.
Explanation: High-dose ICS in COPD is associated with a significantly increased risk of pneumonia.
Q543: Which of the following defines 'heavy smoking' according to recent agricultural/environmental COPD risk studies?
🫧 Pulmonary📂 COPD✅ B
A) ≥ 10 cigarettes per day.
B) Brinkman Index ≥ 400.
C) ≥ 20 pack-years.
D) Smoking duration > 15 years.
Explanation: The Brinkman Index (daily cigarettes x years) ≥ 400 is classified as heavy smoking and is a major independent predictor of COPD.
Q544: According to pulmonary rehabilitation guidelines, when should rehabilitation ideally commence following an acute exacerbation...
🫧 Pulmonary📂 COPD✅ B
A) 24 hours after admission.
B) Within 2 weeks of hospital discharge.
C) 4-6 weeks after clinical stability is reached.
D) Only after the patient fails to improve with triple therapy.
Explanation: Commencing rehab within 2 weeks of discharge is associated with improved exercise capacity and reduced readmissions.
Q545: A patient with COPD and diabetes uses a high-dose ICS inhaler. According to research from the National Emphysema Foundation, ...
🫧 Pulmonary📂 COPD✅ B
A) No effect on glycemic control.
B) 34% increase in the rate of new diabetes diagnoses and progression.
C) ICS reduces insulin resistance.
D) Only oral steroids, not inhaled, affect diabetes.
Explanation: Analysis of over 380,000 patients showed a 34% increase in diabetes onset or progression with inhaler use, rising to 64% with the highest doses.
Q546: What is the 'Step 2' preferred controller choice for a 4-year-old with asthma symptoms 3 times a week?
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Asthma✅ B
A) As-needed SABA only.
B) Daily low-dose ICS.
C) Daily LTRA (Montelukast).
D) As-needed ICS-formoterol.
Explanation: For children ≤ 5 years, daily low-dose ICS is the preferred Step 2 therapy to control symptoms and reduce the risk of severe exacerbations.
Q547: In an acute asthma exacerbation for a 3-year-old child in the Emergency Department, which corticosteroid regimen is now consi...
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Asthma✅ B
A) 7 days of dexamethasone.
B) 1-2 days of oral dexamethasone (0.3-0.6 mg/kg).
C) A single dose of IV hydrocortisone only.
D) Nebulized budesonide every 2 hours for 24 hours.
Explanation: Dexamethasone for 1-2 days is non-inferior to longer prednisolone courses, has better adherence, and lower risk of vomiting in children.
Q548: Which criterion is MANDATORY for the diagnosis of asthma in children ≤ 5 years according to GINA 2025?
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Asthma✅ C
A) Positive skin prick test to house dust mite.
B) FEV1/FVC ratio < 0.70.
C) Timely clinical response to asthma treatment.
D) FeNO > 50 ppb.
Explanation: Diagnosis requires: (1) recurrent acute wheezing or symptoms, (2) exclusion of other causes, AND (3) timely response to treatment (SABA or ICS trial).
Q549: Which factor correctly describes the circadian variability of FeNO and Blood Eosinophil Count (BEC)?
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Asthma✅ B
A) Both are highest in the early morning.
B) BEC is highest in the early morning; FeNO is higher in the afternoon.
C) Both are highest in the afternoon.
D) Variability is only seen in patients with parasitic infections.
Explanation: BEC is higher in the early morning, while FeNO is lower in the early morning and higher in the afternoon.
Q550: A patient taking ritonavir (a potent CYP3A4 inhibitor) requires asthma therapy. Which combination is most likely to result in...
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Asthma✅ C
A) SABA only.
B) LAMA monotherapy.
C) ICS-salmeterol or ICS-vilanterol.
D) Montelukast.
Explanation: Ritonavir inhibits the metabolism of many ICS and LABAs, significantly increasing systemic exposure and the risk of adrenal crisis.
Q551: A patient needs to step down from Step 4 asthma treatment. If their symptoms have been well controlled for 3 months, what is ...
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Asthma✅ B
A) Stop the LABA immediately.
B) Reduce the ICS dose by 25-50%.
C) Switch to SABA as needed only.
D) Discontinue all treatment for a 2-week 'washout' period.
Explanation: Stepping down ICS by 25-50% at 3-month intervals is considered safe and effective.
Q552: Calculate the 'Brinkman Index' for a patient who has smoked 15 cigarettes a day for 30 years.
🫧 Pulmonary📂 COPD✅ B
A) 45.
B) 450.
C) 150.
D) 900.
Explanation: 15 x 30 = 450. This exceeds the threshold (≥ 400) for heavy smoking risk.
Q553: Which statement is TRUE regarding the use of spacers in young children (≤ 5 years)?
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Asthma✅ B
A) Multiple puffs should be delivered into the spacer at once to save time.
B) Tidal breathing (5-10 breaths) is the only required technique.
C) Face masks should be used until the age of 10.
D) Plastic spacers should be rinsed thoroughly with water after every use.
Explanation: Tidal breathing (5-10 breaths) is sufficient to empty the spacer for a young child.
Q554: When using the 'MANDALA' study regimen (as-needed ICS-SABA), what is the maximum number of doses (2 puffs each) allowed per d...
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Asthma✅ B
A) 4 doses.
B) 6 doses.
C) 8 doses.
D) 12 doses.
Explanation: The maximum is 6 doses (12 total inhalations) per day.
Q555: A patient with 'Asthma+COPD' (overlap) presents with an FEV1/FVC of 0.65. What is the fundamental rule for their initial ther...
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Asthma✅ C
A) Avoid ICS to prevent pneumonia.
B) LAMA+LABA is the absolute first-line choice.
C) Treat as asthma; MUST include ICS to reduce death risk.
D) Use as-needed SABA only until a CT scan is performed.
Explanation: In patients with features of both, ICS is essential for safety to reduce the risk of severe exacerbations and death.
Q556: A 55-year-old female with severe uncontrolled asthma has a BEC of 250 cells/µL at 8:00 AM and a FeNO of 22 ppb. According to ...
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Asthma✅ B
A) BEC will increase; FeNO will decrease.
B) BEC will decrease; FeNO will increase.
C) Both BEC and FeNO will increase due to daytime activity.
D) Both BEC and FeNO will decrease due to the effect of daily ICS.
Explanation: BEC and FeNO show marked circadian but opposite variability. BEC is highest in the early morning, while FeNO is lower in the early morning and higher in the afternoon.
Q557: A patient with difficult-to-treat asthma has a FeNO of 65 ppb despite prescription of high-dose ICS-LABA. What is the most ap...
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Asthma✅ B
A) Immediate escalation to a biologic agent.
B) A 1-week FeNO suppression test with directly observed or monitored high-dose ICS.
C) Bronchoscopy with endobronchial biopsy.
D) Switching to a LAMA triple-combination inhaler.
Explanation: In approximately two-thirds of patients with persistently high FeNO despite high-dose prescriptions, the FeNO is suppressed by 1 week of monitored ICS, indicating past poor adherence.
Q558: Which of the following is a major update in the GINA 2025 Track 2 (alternative) Step 4 recommendations?
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Asthma✅ B
A) Addition of a LAMA is now mandatory before increasing ICS dose.
B) Step 4 has been changed from 'medium-to-high dose' to 'medium-dose' ICS-LABA to minimize adverse effects.
C) SABA monotherapy is permitted if the patient is over 65.
D) High-dose ICS is now the preferred first-line for all smokers.
Explanation: GINA 2025 changed Step 4 to medium-dose ICS-LABA to emphasize minimizing exposure to the adverse effects of high-dose ICS, suggesting that high-dose use be limited to 3-6 months if possible.
Q559: A 30-year-old pregnant woman with well-controlled asthma (Step 2) asks if she should stop her ICS to protect the fetus. What ...
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Asthma✅ C
A) Switch to SABA monotherapy during the first trimester.
B) Discontinue ICS and only use them if an exacerbation occurs.
C) Continue ICS-containing therapy; cessation is a significant risk factor for severe exacerbations.
D) Reduce the ICS dose by 50% immediately upon confirmation of pregnancy.
Explanation: Advantages of treating asthma in pregnancy markedly outweigh the risks; cessation of ICS is a significant risk factor for exacerbations and adverse perinatal outcomes.
Q560: A 68-year-old male with a BEC of 350 cells/µL and a history of two moderate COPD exacerbations last year is starting maintena...
🫧 Pulmonary📂 COPD✅ C
A) LABA + LAMA.
B) LAMA monotherapy.
C) LABA + LAMA + ICS.
D) LAMA + Roflumilast.
Explanation: Triple therapy (LABA+LAMA+ICS) is recommended for patients with BEC ≥ 300 cells/µL, especially those with a history of ≥ 2 moderate exacerbations or ≥ 1 hospitalization.
Q561: A patient presents with features of both asthma and COPD (FEV1/FVC < 0.7 post-BD and a history of childhood allergies). What ...
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Asthma✅ B
A) LABA-LAMA monotherapy is the safest starting point.
B) ICS-containing therapy is essential to reduce the risk of severe exacerbations and death.
C) Avoid ICS due to the risk of pneumonia in patients with any COPD features.
D) Only use oral corticosteroids (OCS) for maintenance to avoid local inhaler side effects.
Explanation: For patients with features of both, they must be treated as asthma, meaning ICS is essential for safety. Do not give LABA or LAMA without ICS in this population.
Q562: According to recent research on the impact of inhaler use on metabolic health, high-dose ICS in COPD/Asthma patients is assoc...
🫧 Pulmonary📂 COPD✅ B
A) 20% decrease in the risk of diabetes due to reduced systemic inflammation.
B) 64% increase in the risk of new-onset diabetes and 54% increase in progression.
C) No metabolic risk if using a spacer.
D) Increased risk of type 1 diabetes only.
Explanation: Analysis of over 380,000 patients showed that high-dose ICS use significantly increases both the onset and progression of diabetes.
Q563: Which biomarker profile is the strongest predictor of a good asthma response to anti-IL5/5Rα biologics (e.g., Mepolizumab, Be...
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Asthma✅ B
A) Elevated serum IgE and positive skin prick test.
B) Higher blood eosinophils and a higher number of severe exacerbations in the previous year.
C) Elevated FeNO and childhood-onset asthma.
D) Low blood eosinophils but high sputum neutrophils.
Explanation: Higher BEC and a history of frequent severe exacerbations are the strongest independent predictors of response for anti-IL5/5Rα biologics.
Q564: A 3-year-old child presents with their first episode of wheezing during a viral cold. The child is otherwise well. What is th...
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Asthma✅ B
A) Asthma; start daily low-dose ICS.
B) Bronchiolitis; follow-up and monitor for recurrence.
C) Suspected asthma; start a 3-month trial of medium-dose ICS.
D) Cystic Fibrosis; refer for immediate sweat testing.
Explanation: A first episode of wheezing before 12 months (or very early in life) is usually due to bronchiolitis, not asthma. Asthma is a diagnosis of recurrence in this age group.
Q565: For a 4-year-old child diagnosed with asthma who has symptoms more than twice a week, what is the preferred Step 2 controller...
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Asthma✅ B
A) As-needed SABA only.
B) Daily low-dose ICS plus as-needed SABA.
C) Intermittent high-dose ICS at the onset of viral illness.
D) Daily LTRA (Montelukast).
Explanation: Daily low-dose ICS is the preferred initial controller for children ≤ 5 years with symptoms > 2 days/week or ≥ 1 severe exacerbation in the last year.
Q566: What are the three clinical criteria now required to confirm an asthma diagnosis in a child aged 5 years or younger per the G...
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Asthma✅ B
A) Positive skin prick test, FEV1 < 80%, and family history.
B) Recurrent wheezing, exclusion of alternative causes, and a timely clinical response to asthma treatment.
C) Nocturnal cough, FeNO > 50 ppb, and eosinophils > 300.
D) History of bronchiolitis, low birth weight, and current smoking in the household.
Explanation: The diagnosis in young children is primarily clinical, requiring recurrent episodes, no alternative cause, and documented response to therapy (SABA or ICS trial).
Q567: In an acute asthma exacerbation for a 4-year-old, if the child is unable to speak or drink and has an O2 saturation of 91%, w...
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Asthma✅ C
A) Mild exacerbation; give 2 puffs of SABA and reassess in 1 hour.
B) Moderate exacerbation; start OCS and SABA every 20 minutes.
C) Severe/Life-threatening; start SABA/oxygen and arrange immediate transfer to a high-level care facility (ICU).
D) Suspected pneumonia; order a chest X-ray before giving bronchodilators.
Explanation: Features like being unable to speak/drink, cyanosis, and O2 saturation < 92% indicate a severe episode requiring urgent hospitalization.
Q568: A patient with severe asthma is prescribed Tezepelumab. Which of the following correctly describes its unique mechanism of ac...
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Asthma✅ C
A) It binds directly to the IL-5 receptor on eosinophils.
B) It binds to the Fc portion of free IgE.
C) It binds to TSLP, an epithelial-derived cytokine upstream of the inflammatory cascade.
D) It blocks the IL-4 receptor alpha subunit shared by IL-4 and IL-13.
Explanation: Tezepelumab is an anti-TSLP (thymic stromal lymphopoietin) monoclonal antibody that acts upstream of the entire inflammatory cascade.
Q569: A patient taking ritonavir (a potent CYP3A4 inhibitor) for HIV is started on fluticasone propionate for asthma. Why is this c...
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Asthma✅ B
A) Ritonavir reduces the efficacy of ICS, leading to uncontrolled asthma.
B) Ritonavir inhibits fluticasone metabolism, significantly increasing systemic levels and risk of iatrogenic Cushing's syndrome/adrenal suppression.
C) It causes immediate bronchospasm.
D) It increases the risk of ritonavir-induced hepatotoxicity.
Explanation: Potent CYP3A4 inhibitors like ritonavir and ketoconazole can markedly increase systemic exposure to certain ICS (like fluticasone) and LABAs (salmeterol/vilanterol), leading to severe side effects.
Q570: Which inhaled medication is specifically noted for causing 'paradoxical bronchoconstriction' and should be used with caution?
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Asthma✅ A
A) All inhaled medications (as a class warning).
B) Only DPIs, not pMDIs.
C) Only biologics.
D) Only LAMAs.
Explanation: Paradoxical bronchoconstriction is a potential side effect for any inhaled medication and should be monitored during the first dose.
Q571: For a patient with severe asthma and a BEC ≥ 300 cells/µL, why must the pharmacist ensure a screen for Strongyloides (parasit...
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Asthma✅ B
A) Strongyloides causes the biologic to be metabolized faster.
B) Eosinophils are needed to defend against parasites; suppressing them in a patient with an active infection can lead to disseminated, life-threatening disease.
C) The biologic will cause the parasite to become resistant to ivermectin.
D) It is only required if the patient has a history of travel to the Arctic.
Explanation: High BEC may be due to parasites; treating with OCS or biologics without clearing the infection can lead to Hyperinfection Syndrome.
Q572: Calculate the Brinkman Index for a patient who has smoked 25 cigarettes a day for 20 years. Is this patient at high risk for ...
🫧 Pulmonary📂 COPD✅ B
A) 45; Low risk.
B) 500; High risk (threshold ≥ 400).
C) 250; Moderate risk.
D) 1000; Extremely high risk.
Explanation: Brinkman Index = (daily cigarettes) x (years). 25 x 20 = 500. A BI ≥ 400 is classified as 'heavy smoking' and is a strong independent predictor of COPD.
Q573: In the management of a severe asthma exacerbation in an acute care facility, which of the following is NO LONGER recommended ...
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Asthma✅ B
A) Intravenous magnesium sulfate.
B) Nebulized magnesium sulfate.
C) Controlled oxygen to maintain saturation 93-95%.
D) Oral dexamethasone for 1-2 days.
Explanation: While IV magnesium is still an option for severe cases, nebulized magnesium is no longer recommended by GINA 2025.
Q574: When stepping down asthma treatment, GINA 2025 recommends reducing the ICS dose by what percentage at each 3-month interval?
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Asthma✅ B
A) 10%.
B) 25-50%.
C) 75%.
D) Step down is not recommended; treatment should be for life.
Explanation: Reducing the ICS dose by 25-50% every 3 months is considered safe for most well-controlled patients.
Q575: Which factor is most significantly associated with an increased risk of asthma-related death?
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Asthma✅ C
A) Use of a spacer with a pMDI.
B) A history of one moderate exacerbation 2 years ago.
C) Overuse of SABA, specifically ≥ 12 canisters per year.
D) Being overweight (BMI 27).
Explanation: Dispensing of 12 or more SABA canisters a year is associated with a markedly increased risk of death.
Q576: A patient with COPD features has a post-bronchodilator increase in FEV1 of 450 mL and 15%. What does this marked response sug...
🫧 Pulmonary📂 COPD✅ B
A) Pure, irreversible COPD.
B) A high probability of asthma or Asthma+COPD overlap.
C) The spirometer was likely miscalibrated.
D) The patient is a 'non-responder' to therapy.
Explanation: While some response is seen in COPD, a marked response (> 12% and 400 mL) strongly favors an asthma component.
Q577: What is the recommended target oxygen saturation for an adult patient experiencing a severe asthma exacerbation?
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Asthma✅ C
A) 100% to ensure maximum oxygenation.
B) 88-92% to avoid hypercapnia.
C) 93-95% (controlled oxygen therapy).
D) Oxygen is not indicated unless the patient is cyanotic.
Explanation: Controlled oxygen (93-95%) is associated with better outcomes than high-concentration oxygen.
Q578: Which medication is contraindicated in patients with asthma unless they have a history of successful coronary event managemen...
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Asthma✅ A
A) Beta-blockers (including ophthalmic drops).
B) SGLT2 inhibitors.
C) GLP-1 Receptor Agonists.
D) Metformin.
Explanation: Beta-blockers can cause fatal bronchospasm; they must be decided on a case-by-case basis under close supervision.
Q579: A patient with severe asthma on Step 5 treatment has low Type 2 biomarkers (low FeNO, low BEC). What is the priority for thei...
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Asthma✅ B
A) Start a trial of an anti-IL5 biologic immediately.
B) Review the basics: differential diagnosis, inhaler technique, adherence, and comorbidities.
C) Increase OCS to 40 mg daily indefinitely.
D) Discontinue all ICS therapy.
Explanation: For 'Type 2-low' asthma, the priority is addressing modifiable factors and looking for alternative diagnoses before further escalation.
Q580: According to the 2024-2025 protocol, what should be added in a severe asthma exacerbation if the patient fails to reach 60% p...
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Asthma✅ B
A) 2 liters of normal saline bolus.
B) Intravenous magnesium sulfate (2g single infusion).
C) Intravenous aminophylline.
D) D5W with 40 mEq Potassium.
Explanation: IV magnesium sulfate is indicated for severe episodes that do not respond to initial SABA treatment.
Q581: In children aged ≤ 5 years, which inhaler delivery system is preferred for those aged 3-5 who can follow instructions?
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Asthma✅ B
A) Nebulizer with face mask.
B) pMDI plus spacer with mouthpiece.
C) DPI (Dry Powder Inhaler).
D) SABA monotherapy via 'blow-by' method.
Explanation: A pMDI with a spacer and mouthpiece is the preferred device as soon as the child can demonstrate good technique (usually by age 3-5).
Q582: For a patient using 'AIR-only' (as-needed-only low-dose ICS-formoterol) at Step 1-2, what is the mandatory counseling regardi...
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Asthma✅ B
A) They must use a separate SABA inhaler 15 minutes before exercise.
B) They can use their ICS-formoterol inhaler before exercise, and no separate SABA is needed.
C) Exercise is contraindicated until they move to Step 3.
D) They should take their inhaler 4 hours after exercise only.
Explanation: ICS-formoterol is effective for preventing exercise-induced bronchoconstriction (EIB) and replaces the need for a separate SABA pre-exercise.
Q583: Which comorbidity is specifically associated with 'at least 4 lifetime courses of OCS' in adults?
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Asthma✅ C
A) Increased risk of pneumonia only.
B) Increased risk of sepsis and thromboembolism.
C) Dose-dependent increased risk of diabetes, heart failure, and osteoporosis.
D) No long-term risk if taken for less than 5 days each.
Explanation: Even short, repeated courses of OCS have significant cumulative systemic risks including diabetes, heart failure, and osteoporosis.
Q584: A 4-year-old child presents with a history of three episodes of wheezing in the past year, all occurring during viral colds. ...
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Asthma✅ B
A) Confirmed Asthma.
B) Suspected Asthma.
C) Viral Bronchiolitis only.
D) Transient Early Wheeze.
Explanation: Diagnosis in children ≤ 5 years requires three criteria: recurrent acute wheezing, no likely alternative cause, AND a timely clinical response to treatment. If only 1 or 2 are met, the condition should be recorded as suspected asthma until a response to a diagnostic trial is confirmed.
Q585: A 35-year-old male with severe asthma (Step 5) is well-controlled on a biologic agent and high-dose ICS-LABA. After 6 months ...
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Asthma✅ C
A) Discontinue the biologic first.
B) Stop the maintenance ICS and switch to as-needed-only AIR.
C) Gradually decrease or stop OCS (if used) first, then consider reducing add-on treatments like LAMA.
D) Reduce the LABA dose by 50% before touching the OCS.
Explanation: Minimizing oral corticosteroids (OCS) is the highest priority due to cumulative side effects. Maintenance ICS-containing therapy should generally not be stopped completely in severe asthma patients.
Q586: A 28-year-old pregnant woman in her second trimester presents with worsening asthma symptoms. She is hesitant to continue her...
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Asthma✅ B
A) ICS use is associated with a 20% increase in fetal weight.
B) Cessation of ICS during pregnancy is a significant risk factor for severe exacerbations and adverse perinatal outcomes.
C) SABA monotherapy is the only FDA-approved regimen for the second trimester.
D) ICS therapy is only necessary if the patient has a history of near-fatal asthma.
Explanation: The advantages of treating asthma in pregnancy markedly outweigh potential risks. Stopping ICS is a major risk factor for exacerbations, which can lead to pre-eclampsia, low birth weight, and increased perinatal mortality.
Q587: A 65-year-old smoker with COPD (FEV1 55% predicted) has a blood eosinophil count (BEC) of 350 cells/µL and has had one hospit...
🫧 Pulmonary📂 COPD✅ C
A) LAMA monotherapy.
B) LABA + LAMA.
C) LABA + LAMA + ICS (Triple Therapy).
D) SABA as needed only.
Explanation: For COPD patients with a history of hospitalizations, ≥ 2 moderate exacerbations/year, or a BEC ≥ 300 cells/µL, triple therapy is strongly recommended.
Q588: In the management of a moderate asthma exacerbation for a 4-year-old, which systemic corticosteroid regimen is preferred due ...
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Asthma✅ B
A) Prednisolone for 5 days.
B) Dexamethasone 0.3-0.6 mg/kg for 1-2 days.
C) Intravenous methylprednisolone for 7 days.
D) No systemic steroids are indicated for this age group.
Explanation: Dexamethasone for 1-2 days is non-inferior to longer prednisolone courses, has better adherence, and carries a lower risk of vomiting in children.
Q589: A patient uses a pMDI for their ICS. They report local side effects such as thrush (oral candidiasis). What should the pharma...
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Asthma✅ B
A) The patient's blood glucose.
B) The patient's inhaler technique and use of a spacer.
C) The expiration date of the medication.
D) The patient's diet.
Explanation: Spacers reduce local deposition in the oropharynx, and rinsing/spitting after use further reduces thrush risk.
Q590: A 60-year-old patient with 'Asthma+COPD' overlap has a history of smoking. Which treatment is NOT recommended?
🫧 Pulmonary📂 Asthma✅ C
A) Low-dose ICS.
B) LABA + LAMA.
C) LABA/LAMA monotherapy without ICS.
D) Triple therapy (LABA+LAMA+ICS).
Explanation: Patients with any asthma features should never be treated with long-acting bronchodilators alone due to the risk of severe exacerbations and death.
